topics.title: Classification of process utility systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT636 - PROCESS UTILITY AND PIPING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of process utility systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **Process Utility and Piping Engineering (PECHT636)**, the term **process utility system** refers to the integrated network of ancillary support services, fluids, and energy streams that are **not part of the main product stream** but are indispensable for the **safe, continuous, and efficient operation** of a chemical, petrochemical, pharmaceutical, or any process manufacturing plant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved Wording):**
> *"Process utilities are the secondary inputs (services) such as steam, water, air, refrigerants, electricity, and effluent-handling facilities that are generated, conditioned, distributed, and consumed within a process plant to facilitate the conversion of raw materials into finished products."*

These utilities are sometimes also referred to as **"offsites"** or **"support services"** in large-scale refinery and petrochemical complexes.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Plant as a Living Organism

Imagine a process plant as a **living human body**:

| Body System | Equivalent Process Utility | Function |
|-------------|---------------------------|----------|
| Circulatory System (blood) | Water, Steam, Brine | Transport of heat and mass |
| Respiratory System (oxygen/air) | Compressed Air, Instrument Air | Oxidation, pneumatic control |
| Nervous System (electrical signals) | Electricity, DCS signals | Power & control |
| Digestive System (food/water) | Demineralized Water, Fuel | Energy input |
| Excretory System | Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP) | Waste disposal |
| Cooling (sweating) | Cooling Tower, Chilled Water | Heat rejection |

Just as a human body cannot function without blood circulation or oxygen, a chemical reactor cannot operate without steam for heating, cooling water for temperature control, or compressed air for instrument actuation. The utility system is the **backbone of plant operability**.

### 1.3 Why Classification is Essential

Classification provides a **systematic taxonomy** for:
1. **Engineering Design** — Helps in sizing equipment (boilers, cooling towers, compressors).
2. **Cost Estimation** — Utilities often account for **20–40% of operating cost**.
3. **Safety & Hazard Analysis** — Different utilities carry different risks (e.g., LP steam vs. fuel gas).
4. **Energy Auditing** — Pin-points inefficiencies in specific utility streams.
5. **Regulatory Compliance** — Statutory bodies (e.g., Factory & Boilers Inspectorate, Kerala State Pollution Control Board) classify utilities distinctly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** As per KTU 2024 PECHT636 Module 1, students must demonstrate the ability to **Classify, identify, and map** the principal utilities used across pharmaceutical, petrochemical, fertilizer, and food-processing industries.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Primary Classification of Process Utilities

The classification of process utilities is approached via **three orthogonal frameworks** that KTU examiners frequently test:

#### Framework A — Classification by **Physical State / Phase**

| Phase | Utility Examples | Typical Plant Use |
|-------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Liquid** | Water (DM, Soft, Cooling, Chilled), Brine, Molten Salt | Heat transfer media, solvent |
| **Gas / Vapour** | Steam (LP, MP, HP), Compressed Air, Nitrogen, Natural Gas, Flue Gas | Heating, inerting, pneumatic drives |
| **Solid** | Coal, Lignite, Biomass (fuel), Catalysts, Silica Gel (desiccant) | Combustion, drying, adsorption |
| **Mixed / Multi-phase** | Two-phase refrigerant (R-134a, R-717), Hot oil + vapor | Refrigeration, high-temp heating |

#### Framework B — Classification by **Functional Purpose**

This is the **most exam-relevant** classification in KTU papers.

**1. Heating Utilities** (raise process temperature)
- Steam (saturated, superheated) — most common
- Hot oil / Thermic fluid (for temperatures above steam range)
- Hot water (low-grade heating, 60–95 °C)
- Flue gas (direct-fired furnaces)

**2. Cooling Utilities** (remove process heat)
- Cooling water (CW) — once-through or recirculated via cooling tower
- Chilled water (4–10 °C) — for low-temperature duty
- Refrigerants (NH₃, R-134a, R-410A) — for sub-zero temperatures
- Brine (CaCl₂, NaCl solution) — for very low temperatures
- Cryogenic fluids (LN₂, LCO₂) — specialty pharma

**3. Process / Reaction Utilities**
- Compressed Air (CA) — agitation, atomization, oxidation
- Instrument Air (IA) — pneumatically actuated valves
- Nitrogen (N₂) — inert blanketing, purging
- Oxygen / Ozone — oxidation, water treatment
- Hydrogen — hydroprocessing, hydrogenation

**4. Power & Electrical Utilities**
- Electrical power (HT/LT distribution)
- Emergency power (DG sets)
- UPS & battery banks

**5. Water Treatment Utilities**
- Raw water → Treated water → DM water → Ultra-pure water

**6. Effluent / Waste Utilities**
- Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)
- Sewage Treatment Plant (STP)
- Hazardous waste handling

#### Framework C — Classification by **Source / Origin**

| Source | Examples |
|--------|----------|
| Natural | Water from river/lake, atmospheric air, solar energy |
| Purchased | Grid electricity, piped natural gas, bulk LPG |
| Captive / On-site generated | Steam from in-house boiler, compressed air from compressor, N₂ from PSA/VPSA |

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Utility Engineering Core Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet** — Frequently tested in KTU Part-A & Part-B.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Steam Enthalpy (saturated) | $H_g = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | Steam quality |
| 2 | Heat Duty (sensible) | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kW or kJ/h | Heating/cooling load |
| 3 | Heat Duty (latent) | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot \lambda$ | kW | Boiling/condensation |
| 4 | Boiler Efficiency | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{Q_{absorbed}}{Q_{fuel}} \times 100$ | % | Steam generation cost |
| 5 | Cooling Tower Range | $\Delta T_{range} = T_{hot\,out} - T_{cold\,in}$ | °C | Cooling water design |
| 6 | Cooling Tower Approach | $T_{approach} = T_{cold\,out} - T_{wb}$ | °C | Tower performance |
| 7 | Compression Work (isothermal) | $W = \dot{m} \cdot R \cdot T_1 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | kW | Compressor power |
| 8 | Compression Work (adiabatic) | $W = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} \cdot P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | kW | Air compressor design |
| 9 | Refrigeration Effect (COP) | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{comp}}$ | — | Chiller efficiency |
| 10 | Steam Consumption | $\dot{m}_{steam} = \dfrac{Q_{req}}{\lambda_{steam}}$ | kg/h | Reboiler sizing |
| 11 | Cooling Water Flow | $\dot{m}_{cw} = \dfrac{Q_{reject}}{C_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T_{cw}}$ | kg/h | Condenser duty |
| 12 | Make-up Water | $M = E + D + B$ | m³/h | Cooling tower balance |

Where in **#12**: $E$ = evaporation loss, $D$ = drift loss, $B$ = blowdown loss.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Significance

In an **integrated refinery**, utilities represent one of the **largest cost centers**:
- A modern refinery uses **0.4–0.6 tons of steam per ton of crude processed**.
- Cooling water circulation ratios can be as high as **100:1** in continuous plants.
- Compressed air leaks cost industries an estimated **30–40% of compressor energy** (DOE, USA estimate).

Therefore, **classification is the first step in energy benchmarking and pinch analysis** — a topic frequently examined in KTU under Module 5 and Module 6 of related courses.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Steam Utility Sizing (KTU Numerical Pattern)

> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU 2-Mark Sub-Question):**
> A shell-and-tube heat exchanger heats **20,000 kg/h** of an oil from **40 °C to 90 °C**. The oil has a mean specific heat of **2.1 kJ/(kg·K)**. Saturated steam at **3 bar(g)** (saturation temperature ≈ 133.5 °C) is used. Calculate the **(a)** heat duty in kW, **(b)** steam consumption in kg/h assuming saturated steam at latent heat **λ = 2164 kJ/kg**.

#### Step 1 — Identify the Process Goal
We must size a **heating utility** (steam) to deliver a known heat duty $Q$.

#### Step 2 — Compute the Sensible Heat Required
The oil undergoes a **sensible temperature rise** of $\Delta T = 90 - 40 = 50 \,°C$.

$$Q = \dot{m}_{oil} \cdot C_{p,oil} \cdot \Delta T$$

Insert the numerical values:

$$Q = 20{,}000 \; \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{h}} \cdot 2.1 \; \dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg \cdot K}} \cdot 50 \; \text{K}$$

$$Q = 2{,}100{,}000 \; \dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{h}}$$

#### Step 3 — Convert to kW (since 1 kW = 3600 kJ/h)

$$Q = \dfrac{2{,}100{,}000}{3600} \; \text{kW}$$

$$Q = 583.33 \; \text{kW}$$

#### Step 4 — Calculate Steam Consumption
Assuming the steam **fully condenses** (latent-heat-only release, neglecting sub-cooling):

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = \dfrac{Q_{req}}{\lambda_{steam}}$$

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = \dfrac{583.33 \; \text{kW}}{2164 \; \text{kJ/kg}}$$

Convert kW to kJ/h by multiplying by 3600:

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = \dfrac{583.33 \times 3600}{2164}$$

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = \dfrac{2{,}100{,}000}{2164}$$

$$\dot{m}_{steam} \approx 970.4 \; \text{kg/h}$$

#### Step 5 — Real-World Interpretation
- Steam-to-oil mass ratio = $970.4 / 20{,}000 = 0.0485$ kg steam / kg oil.
- This **low steam ratio** is typical of sensible-heat-only heating duties.
- For **evaporators and reboilers** (latent-heat duties), this ratio jumps to **1.0–1.5** because each kg of solvent needs ~1 kg of steam.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always **state the steam condition** (pressure, saturation temperature, latent heat). Partial marks are deducted for "Steam at 3 bar" without specifying saturation temperature.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Cooling Tower Mass Balance (Analytical Matrix)

A counterflow induced-draft cooling tower handles **500 m³/h** of recirculated water with the following duty parameters:

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Hot water inlet temp ($T_1$) | 42 | °C |
| Cold water outlet temp ($T_2$) | 32 | °C |
| Wet-bulb temperature ($T_{wb}$) | 26 | °C |
| Evaporation loss coefficient ($k$) | 0.00085 | °C⁻¹ |
| Drift loss ($D$) | 0.02 | % of circulation |
| Cycles of Concentration (COC) | 3 | — |

#### Step 1 — Compute Cooling Tower Range

$$\Delta T_{range} = T_1 - T_2 = 42 - 32 = 10 \; °C$$

#### Step 2 — Compute Approach

$$T_{approach} = T_2 - T_{wb} = 32 - 26 = 6 \; °C$$

*Interpretation:* A 6 °C approach indicates a **medium-efficiency** tower.

#### Step 3 — Evaporation Loss

$$E = k \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta T_{range}$$

$$E = 0.00085 \cdot 500 \cdot 10 = 4.25 \; \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

#### Step 4 — Drift Loss

$$D = 0.0002 \cdot 500 = 0.10 \; \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

#### Step 5 — Blowdown (from COC definition)

Blowdown is set by cycles of concentration $COC = \dfrac{C_B}{C_M}$, where $C_B$ and $C_M$ are blowdown and makeup dissolved solids. Mass-balance on dissolved solids:

$$\dot{V}_M \cdot C_M = \dot{V}_B \cdot C_B$$

Since $COC = 3$, and evaporation $E$ leaves salt behind:

$$\dot{V}_B = \dfrac{E}{COC - 1} = \dfrac{4.25}{3 - 1} = 2.125 \; \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

#### Step 6 — Total Make-up Water

$$M = E + D + B = 4.25 + 0.10 + 2.125 = 6.475 \; \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

#### Analytical Matrix Summary

| Stream | Flow (m³/h) | % of Circulation |
|--------|-------------|------------------|
| Evaporation ($E$) | 4.250 | 0.850 |
| Drift ($D$) | 0.100 | 0.020 |
| Blowdown ($B$) | 2.125 | 0.425 |
| **Make-up ($M$)** | **6.475** | **1.295** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Even though evaporation is the largest single loss (4.25 m³/h), the **blowdown** (2.125 m³/h) is a *controllable* parameter via COC tuning. Increasing COC from 3 to 5 would reduce $B$ to 1.06 m³/h — a direct **water and chemical cost saving**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Classification & Selection Decision Matrix

> **Engineering Scenario:** A pharmaceutical plant must select a cooling utility for a **crystallizer operating at −5 °C**. Available options: (i) cooling tower water, (ii) chilled water, (iii) refrigerant brine, (iv) cryogenic N₂.

| Utility | Available Temp Range | Suitability for −5 °C | Verdict |
|---------|---------------------|------------------------|---------|
| Cooling tower water | 25–35 °C | Cannot reach sub-zero | ❌ Not suitable |
| Chilled water | 4–10 °C | Marginal (10 °C > −5 °C) | ⚠️ Barely possible with low approach |
| Refrigerant brine (CaCl₂) | −10 to −20 °C | Comfortably covers −5 °C | ✅ **Recommended** |
| Cryogenic LN₂ | −196 °C | Overkill, very expensive | ❌ Economically unjustified |

> [!TIP]
> **Decision Logic:** The selection follows the **principle of minimum thermodynamic driving force at lowest capital cost**. Brine gives sufficient $\Delta T$ without the cryogenic expense.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Process Utility Systems] --> rootB[By Physical State]
    rootA --> rootC[By Function]
    rootA --> rootD[By Source]

    rootB --> rootB1[Liquid]
    rootB --> rootB2[Gas or Vapour]
    rootB --> rootB3[Solid]
    rootB --> rootB4[Multiphase]

    rootB1 --> rootB1a[Water variants]
    rootB1 --> rootB1b[Brines]
    rootB1 --> rootB1c[Thermic fluids]

    rootB2 --> rootB2a[Steam LP MP HP]
    rootB2 --> rootB2b[Compressed Air]
    rootB2 --> rootB2c[Nitrogen]
    rootB2 --> rootB2d[Natural Gas]

    rootC --> rootC1[Heating Utilities]
    rootC --> rootC2[Cooling Utilities]
    rootC --> rootC3[Process Reactant Utilities]
    rootC --> rootC4[Power Utilities]
    rootC --> rootC5[Water Utilities]
    rootC --> rootC6[Effluent Utilities]

    rootC1 --> rootC1a[Saturated Steam]
    rootC1 --> rootC1b[Superheated Steam]
    rootC1 --> rootC1c[Hot Oil]
    rootC1 --> rootC1d[Flue Gas]

    rootC2 --> rootC2a[Cooling Tower Water]
    rootC2 --> rootC2b[Chilled Water]
    rootC2 --> rootC2c[Refrigerant]
    rootC2 --> rootC2d[Brine]
    rootC2 --> rootC2e[Cryogenic Fluids]

    rootC3 --> rootC3a[Instrument Air]
    rootC3 --> rootC3b[Process Air]
    rootC3 --> rootC3c[Nitrogen Blanketing]
    rootC3 --> rootC3d[Hydrogen]

    rootC4 --> rootC4a[HT Power]
    rootC4 --> rootC4b[LT Power]
    rootC4 --> rootC4c[DG Set]

    rootD --> rootD1[Natural Source]
    rootD --> rootD2[Purchased Grid]
    rootD --> rootD3[Captive Plant]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Utility Distribution Network

```mermaid
graph LR
    plant[Process Plant Boundary] --> gen[Generation or Source]
    gen --> cond[Conditioning and Treatment]
    cond --> dist[Distribution Network Piping]
    dist --> user1[Heating User Reboiler HEX]
    dist --> user2[Cooling User Condenser Jacket]
    dist --> user3[Instrument Air User Valves]
    dist --> user4[Inert Gas User Blanketing]
    user1 --> ret1[Condensate Return]
    user2 --> ret2[Hot Water Return]
    user3 --> ret3[Vented Air]
    user4 --> ret4[Vented Nitrogen]
    ret1 --> cond
    ret2 --> gen
    ret3 --> atm[Atmosphere]
    ret4 --> atm
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Architecture:**
> - **Generation → Conditioning → Distribution → End Use → Return Loop** is the universal flow for almost every utility.
> - The **return loop** (condensate, blowdown, vent) is critical for **energy and water recovery**, a major scoring point in KTU 14-mark questions on utility optimization.

### 4.3 Sankey-style Comparison of Utility Energy Shares (Typical Refinery)

```mermaid
graph TD
    src[Total Energy Input] --> stm[Steam System 40 percent]
    src --> cw[Cooling Water 18 percent]
    src --> el[Electricity 25 percent]
    src --> fu[Fuel Fired 12 percent]
    src --> cgh[Cogeneration Recovery 5 percent]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Energy Audit Heuristic:** In a typical refinery, **steam system losses alone exceed 20%** of total energy. This single fact justifies an entire module of utility engineering and is a frequent KTU essay question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECHT636]**
*Define process utilities. List any six major process utilities used in a chemical process plant.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):**
> Process utilities are the auxiliary services and energy streams—such as steam, water, air, electricity, and refrigeration—that support the operation of the main production process but are not part of the product stream itself.

> **Six Major Utilities (½ mark each):**
> 1. **Steam** — heating medium for reboilers, vaporizers, tracing.
> 2. **Cooling water** — heat rejection in condensers and coolers.
> 3. **Compressed air** — pneumatic conveying, atomization.
> 4. **Instrument air** — actuation of control valves.
> 5. **Demineralized water** — boiler feed, pharmaceutical formulation.
> 6. **Nitrogen** — inerting, blanketing of flammable liquids.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECHT636]**
*Distinguish between instrument air and process air. Why are they kept as separate systems?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Instrument Air | Process Air |
|--------|----------------|-------------|
| Purpose | Pneumatic control valves, instruments | Mixing, oxidation, atomization |
| Quality | Oil-free, dry (dew point ≈ −40 °C) | Moderate quality acceptable |
| Pressure | 4–6 bar(g), regulated | Variable, often higher |
| Continuity | Must be uninterrupted | Can tolerate breaks |

> **Reason for separation (1 mark):** Instrument air must be **absolutely clean and dry**; any contamination can damage precision valve actuators or plug orifice plates, leading to plant trips. Mixing the two systems risks oil-mist carryover into control loops, which is unacceptable for safety-critical instrumentation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, PECHT636]**

**(a)** Classify the process utilities used in a typical **petrochemical plant** based on **functional purpose**. Explain any **four** classes with at least two examples per class. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **steam distribution network** of a process plant, including generation, conditioning, distribution, and condensate recovery. List the **major energy-loss points** and two conservation measures. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Classification by Functional Purpose:**

1. **Heating Utilities** *(1 mark)*
   - *Saturated steam* (LP 3–5 bar, MP 10–15 bar, HP 40+ bar).
   - *Thermic fluid / hot oil* — used where steam temperature is insufficient (>250 °C).

2. **Cooling Utilities** *(1 mark)*
   - *Cooling tower water* — bulk heat rejection.
   - *Chilled water / brine* — sub-ambient temperatures.

3. **Process / Reaction Utilities** *(1 mark)*
   - *Instrument air* — pneumatic actuators.
   - *Nitrogen* — inert blanket on storage tanks of hydrocarbons.

4. **Power Utilities** *(1 mark)*
   - *HT/LT electricity* from grid or captive power.
   - *DG set* for emergency backup.

5. **Water Utilities** *(1 mark)*
   - *Demineralized water* for boilers.
   - *Soft water* for cooling make-up.

6. **Effluent Utilities** *(1 mark)*
   - *Effluent Treatment Plant* for wastewater.
   - *Flare stack* for gaseous waste.

7. *(Bonus)* **Specialty utilities** — Refrigerants, cryogenic fluids.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Steam Distribution Network Schematic (verbal description, 3 marks):**

The flow sequence is:
> **Fuel + Air → Boiler → Steam Drum → Steam Header → Pressure Reducing Station → Distribution Mains → End Users (HEX, Reboilers) → Condensate Collection → Condensate Pump → Deaerator → Boiler Feed.**

**Major Energy-Loss Points (2 marks):**
1. **Boiler flue-gas losses** (largest single loss, 8–15%).
2. **Un-insulated steam pipelines and fittings** (radiation losses).
3. **Flash-steam losses** at condensate receivers.
4. **Leaking steam traps** and failed PRV stations.

**Conservation Measures (2 marks):**
1. **Insulate all HP/MP steam lines** with mineral wool / calcium silicate; recover cost in < 1 year.
2. **Install high-pressure condensate recovery** to feed hot condensate back to the deaerator, saving 10–15% fuel.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, PECHT636]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **classification of process water utilities**, highlighting the difference between **raw water, clarified water, softened water, demineralized (DM) water, and deionized (DI) water**. Mention typical industrial applications of each. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A process condenser rejects **800 kW** of heat to cooling water. Cooling water enters at **30 °C** and is allowed to rise by **8 °C**. Calculate:
   (i) Required cooling water flow in **kg/h**.
   (ii) If the cooling tower operates with wet-bulb temperature = **25 °C** and approach = **5 °C**, determine the **cold water temperature**.
   (iii) If the plant uses 5 cycles of concentration and evaporation coefficient $k = 0.00085$, compute the **make-up water rate**. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Classification of Process Water (7 marks — 1 mark per water type + 2 marks for application table):**

| Type | Description | Typical Application |
|------|-------------|---------------------|
| Raw water | Untreated from river/borewell | Initial intake, non-critical rinsing |
| Clarified water | Coagulation + sedimentation | Cooling tower make-up, general washing |
| Softened water | Hardness (Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺) removed by ion-exchange | Medium-pressure boiler feed, process water |
| Demineralized (DM) water | All ions removed by cation + anion exchangers | High-pressure boiler feed, pharmaceutical |
| Deionized (DI) water | Mixed-bed polish after DM | Semiconductor, lab-grade, injectable formulations |

**Key Distinction:** DM removes *dissolved ions*; DI is DM *plus* removal of silica and organics to < 0.1 µS/cm conductivity.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**(i) Cooling water flow rate**

$$\dot{m}_{cw} = \dfrac{Q}{C_p \cdot \Delta T}$$

With $C_p$ for water = **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**:

$$\dot{m}_{cw} = \dfrac{800 \, \text{kW} \times 3600}{4.186 \times 8}$$

$$\dot{m}_{cw} = \dfrac{2{,}880{,}000}{33.488}$$

$$\dot{m}_{cw} \approx 86{,}007 \; \text{kg/h}$$

**(ii) Cold water (cooling tower outlet) temperature**

Approach is defined relative to wet-bulb:

$$T_{cold\,out} = T_{wb} + T_{approach} = 25 + 5 = 30 \; °C$$

*Note:* This implies $T_{cold\,out}$ equals $T_{hot\,in}$ — i.e., **cooling tower is barely doing useful work**; in practice approach of 5 °C with 8 °C range is borderline.

**(iii) Make-up water**

$$E = k \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta T_{range} = 0.00085 \cdot 86{,}007 \cdot 8 = 584.8 \; \text{kg/h}$$

Assuming drift $D \approx 0.2\% \cdot \dot{V} = 0.002 \cdot 86{,}007 = 172 \; \text{kg/h}$

$$\dot{V}_B = \dfrac{E}{COC - 1} = \dfrac{584.8}{5 - 1} = 146.2 \; \text{kg/h}$$

$$\dot{V}_M = E + D + B = 584.8 + 172 + 146.2 = 903 \; \text{kg/h}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — Mixing kW with kJ/h is the #1 reason students lose 1–2 marks in Part B.
> 2. **Forgetting approach definition** — Approach is *from wet-bulb*, NOT from hot water inlet. Wrong definition costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Not stating assumption** — Always write "Assuming $C_p$ of water = 4.186 kJ/(kg·K) and negligible drift." KTU examiners award the unit mark only when assumptions are explicit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember — Module 1, Topic: Classification of Process Utility Systems**

- ✅ **Process utilities ≠ product stream** — they are *support services*.
- ✅ **Three classification frameworks:** Physical state, Functional purpose, Source/origin.
- ✅ **Most exam-relevant classification** is **by function** (Heating / Cooling / Process / Power / Water / Effluent).
- ✅ **Steam grades:** LP (3–5 bar), MP (10–15 bar), HP (40+ bar). Always quote pressure and saturation temperature together.
- ✅ **Instrument air ≠ Process air** — instrument air is oil-free, dry, dew-point controlled.
- ✅ **Water hierarchy:** Raw → Clarified → Softened → DM → DI (each step purifies further).
- ✅ **Cooling tower range** = $T_{hot} - T_{cold}$ ; **approach** = $T_{cold} - T_{wb}$.
- ✅ **Make-up water:** $M = E + D + B$ ; $B = E / (COC - 1)$.
- ✅ **Steam consumption** formula: $\dot{m} = Q / \lambda$ — always state the latent heat value used.
- ✅ **Utility cost** can be 20–40% of operating cost — hence the emphasis on **energy audit and pinch analysis** in later modules.
- ✅ **Plant returns loop** — condensate, blowdown, vents — is critical for energy/water recovery (KTU essay-worthy).
- ✅ **Decision logic:** select the *minimum utility grade* that satisfies the thermodynamic duty (avoid cryogenic overkill).
- ✅ **Common units:** kW, kg/h, m³/h, °C, kJ/kg — never mix without conversion.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of process utilities in chemical industries and plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT636 - PROCESS UTILITY AND PIPING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Importance of process utilities in chemical industries and plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Utilities** are the **secondary, supporting services and consumable resources** (such as steam, water, compressed air, refrigerants, electricity, fuel, and inert gases) that are **NOT the primary product of manufacture** but are **absolutely essential for the smooth, safe, continuous, and economical operation** of any chemical, petrochemical, pharmaceutical, or process plant.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT636)** Module 1 framework, process utilities form the **operational backbone** of every chemical plant. They provide the energy, mass-transfer media, temperature control, and inert environments necessary to drive chemical reactions, separate products, maintain safety, and ensure product quality.

### 1.2 Key Terminology Used in KTU Syllabus

| Term | KTU Definition |
|------|----------------|
| **Utility** | Any service input (energy or material) used by the process but not part of the product. |
| **Plant Utility System** | The integrated network of equipment and pipelines that delivers utilities throughout the plant. |
| **Steam Generation** | Production of steam at defined pressure/temperature for process heating. |
| **Cooling Medium** | A fluid (water, brine, refrigerant) used to remove process heat. |
| **Inert Gas Blanketing** | Use of N₂/CO₂ to prevent oxidation, explosion, or moisture absorption. |
| **Black Utility** | Utilities that are part of process mass balance (e.g., fuel for boiler). |
| **White Utility** | Utilities that only provide service (e.g., instrument air, cooling water). |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Body" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a chemical plant as a living human body.** The reactors, distillation columns, and heat exchangers are the **organs** that do the actual "work" of producing chemicals. The **process utilities are the bloodstream, lungs, and nervous system**:
> - **Steam** = warm blood carrying heat energy to every organ.
> - **Cooling water** = the sweat/lungs that remove waste heat.
> - **Compressed air** = the nervous system powering instruments and valves.
> - **Electricity** = the heartbeat keeping every motor alive.
> - **Water (DM/Softened)** = the clean plasma feeding the process.
>
> Without utilities, even the best-designed reactor is just an empty, useless shell — exactly like a body without blood.

### 1.4 Why "Importance" is a Separate KTU Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In most plants, **15% to 30% of total operating cost** comes from utilities. In energy-intensive sectors (ammonia, ethylene, refinery), this can rise to **40–60%**. Studying their importance helps chemical engineers:
> 1. Optimize energy cost (the **largest controllable expense** in a plant).
> 2. Ensure plant **safety and reliability**.
> 3. Design **efficient piping networks** (the subject of this course itself).
> 4. Meet **environmental and regulatory** norms (especially for water and emissions).

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Highlighted by KTU

The KTU syllabus expects familiarity with these physical constants and standards:
- **Latent heat of steam at 1 atm**: **2,256 kJ/kg**
- **Specific heat of water**: **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**
- **Standard boiler pressure**: **10–40 bar (gauge)** for process plants
- **Standard cooling water inlet temperature**: **30–32 °C**
- **Standard instrument air dew point**: **−40 °C**
- **Standard plant power frequency (India)**: **50 Hz, 415 V / 11 kV / 33 kV**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Pillars of Process Utilities

Every chemical plant — whether a small pharmaceutical unit in Hyderabad or a giant refinery in Kochi — relies on **six core utility categories**:

#### 2.1.1 Water Utilities (The Most Used Utility)

- **Cooling Water (CW)** — Removes heat from condensers, coolers, and jackets.
- **Boiler Feed Water (BFW)** — High-purity water fed to boilers for steam generation.
- **Deminerialised (DM) Water** — Used in reactors where ions would poison catalysts.
- **Process Water** — Direct participant in reactions (e.g., dilution, hydrolysis).
- **Potable Water** — For human consumption and plant hygiene.

**Why critical?** Water is the universal solvent and heat-transfer medium. Nearly **80% of chemical reactions** use water in some form.

#### 2.1.2 Steam (The Most Versatile Utility)

- **Saturated Steam** — Used for direct heating, humidification, sterilization.
- **Superheated Steam** — Used in turbines and high-temperature reactors.
- **Steam Pressure Tiers (KTU Standard)**:
  - **LP Steam**: 3–5 bar
  - **MP Steam**: 10–15 bar
  - **HP Steam**: 40–100 bar

**Why critical?** Steam carries **latent heat efficiently** (2,256 kJ/kg) — much more than hot water of the same mass.

#### 2.1.3 Air Utilities

- **Instrument Air (IA)** — Oil-free, dry air for pneumatic valves and instruments.
- **Plant Air (PA)** — General-purpose air for cleaning, pneumatic tools.
- **Breathing Air** — For personnel in confined spaces.
- **Compressed Air** — High-pressure air for blow-molding, ejectors.

#### 2.1.4 Refrigeration and Cooling

- **Mechanical Refrigeration** — For temperatures down to **−40 °C**.
- **Brine Systems** — CaCl₂ / ethylene glycol for sub-zero cooling.
- **Cryogenic Systems** — Liquefied N₂ / LNG for ultra-low temperatures.

#### 2.1.5 Electrical Power

- **HT Supply (11 kV / 33 kV)** — From state grid or captive power.
- **LT Supply (415 V)** — For motors, lighting, instruments.
- **Emergency Power (Diesel Generators)** — Critical for safety systems.

#### 2.1.6 Fuels and Inert Gases

- **Fuels** — Natural gas, furnace oil, coal, biomass for boilers/ furnaces.
- **Inert Gases** — Nitrogen (N₂) for blanketing; CO₂ for fire suppression.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **frequently tested** in KTU ESE questions on Module 1.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat transfer | kW |
| 2 | $Q = m \cdot \lambda$ | Latent heat (boiling/condensation) | kW |
| 3 | $m_{steam} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{\lambda_s}$ | Steam requirement for heating | kg/h |
| 4 | $m_{cw} = \dfrac{Q_{removed}}{C_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})}$ | Cooling water flow rate | kg/h |
| 5 | $\text{BHP} = \dfrac{m_{steam} \cdot (h_{out} - h_{in})}{9550 \cdot \eta}$ | Boiler Horsepower calculation | HP |
| 6 | $W_{comp} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | Compressor work (isentropic) | kW |
| 7 | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{input}}$ | Coefficient of Performance (refrigeration) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $C_{utility} = \sum (m_i \cdot P_i)$ | Total utility cost per hour | ₹/h |
| 9 | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{\text{Useful energy output}}{\text{Total energy input}}$ | Plant energy efficiency | % |
| 10 | $\text{Specific Steam Consumption} = \dfrac{m_{steam}}{m_{product}}$ | kg steam per kg product | kg/kg |

> **Boundary Condition Note:** All temperatures in °C must be converted to **K** (add 273.15) when using thermodynamic formulas. KTU examiners often deduct marks for this conversion.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- In a **refinery**, utility cost ≈ **30–45%** of operating expense.
- In an **ammonia plant**, **reforming steam + process steam ≈ 1.5–2.0 tons of steam per ton of ammonia**.
- In a **pharma plant**, **DM water cost is 5–8× higher than raw water** due to purification.
- In **petrochemical cracker units**, **fuel gas saving of 1% can save crores of rupees annually**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The course PECHT636 itself is built on the fact that **utility piping constitutes 40–60% of total plant piping** by length and is responsible for the majority of plant downtime if poorly designed.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — The "Importance Hierarchy" of Utilities

This matrix is built on **operational impact × frequency of use**, which KTU examiners love to test.

| Utility | Frequency of Use | Impact on Plant Shutdown if Lost | Cost Share (Typical) |
|---------|------------------|----------------------------------|----------------------|
| Electricity | **Continuous (24/7)** | **Immediate total shutdown** | 25–35% |
| Cooling Water | **Continuous** | **Reactor runaway within minutes** | 10–15% |
| Steam | **Continuous** | **Process heating stops** | 15–25% |
| Instrument Air | **Continuous** | **All control valves fail (plant trip)** | 5–8% |
| DM Water | **Batch/Daily** | **Catalyst poisoning risk** | 5–10% |
| Refrigeration | **As needed** | **Loss of product spec** | 8–12% |
| Inert Gas (N₂) | **Intermittent** | **Fire/explosion risk in flammable units** | 3–6% |

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Steam Requirement for a Reactor Heating Duty

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 3-mark style):**
A chemical reactor requires **2,000 MJ/h** of heat input to maintain 150 °C. Available steam is at **5 bar saturated (λ = 2,108 kJ/kg)**. The condensate leaves at **120 °C (C_p = 4.186 kJ/kg·K)**. Calculate the steam flow rate if heat recovery from condensate is considered.

**Step 1 — Identify heat sources**

The steam gives up its **latent heat** when condensing, plus the condensate from 120 °C to the reactor temperature (150 °C) gives up **sensible heat**.

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= Q_{latent} + Q_{sensible} \\
Q_{latent} &= m_s \cdot \lambda_s \\
Q_{sensible} &= m_s \cdot C_{p,water} \cdot (T_{reactor} - T_{cond,out})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Write total heat equation**

$$Q_{total} = m_s \cdot \lambda_s + m_s \cdot C_{p,water} \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 3 — Factor out the steam mass**

$$Q_{total} = m_s \left[ \lambda_s + C_p \cdot \Delta T \right]$$

**Step 4 — Compute ΔT for condensate cooling**

Condensate enters at saturation temp of 5 bar steam. From steam tables: $T_{sat} @ 5 bar = 151.86 °C$.
Reactor operating temperature = 150 °C.

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= T_{sat} - T_{reactor} \\
&= 151.86 - 150 \\
&= 1.86 \, °C
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the sensible heat term per kg**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_p \cdot \Delta T &= 4.186 \times 1.86 \\
&= 7.786 \, kJ/kg
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Compute total heat given by 1 kg of steam**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{per\,kg} &= \lambda_s + C_p \cdot \Delta T \\
&= 2108 + 7.786 \\
&= 2115.786 \, kJ/kg
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Convert required duty to same units**

$$Q_{total} = 2{,}000 \, MJ/h = 2{,}000{,}000 \, kJ/h$$

**Step 8 — Solve for steam mass flow**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_s &= \dfrac{Q_{total}}{Q_{per\,kg}} \\
&= \dfrac{2{,}000{,}000}{2115.786} \\
&= 945.33 \, kg/h
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{m_s \approx 945.3 \, kg/h \approx 0.945 \, ton/h}$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Many students forget the **sensible heat term** (Step 4–5). This gives an under-design of ~0.37%, which can accumulate to significant errors in large plants.

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation — Cooling Water Flow Rate

**Problem:** A shell-and-tube condenser must remove **1,500 kW** of heat. Cooling water enters at **30 °C** and may be heated to a maximum of **45 °C**. Calculate CW flow rate.

**Step 1 — Apply sensible heat balance**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_{cw} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$\begin{aligned}
1500 \, kW &= \dot{m}_{cw} \times 4.186 \times (45 - 30)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Solve**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{cw} &= \dfrac{1500}{4.186 \times 15} \\
&= \dfrac{1500}{62.79} \\
&= 23.89 \, kg/s
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to common plant unit (m³/h)**

Using water density $\rho = 1000 \, kg/m^3$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{cw} &= 23.89 \times 3.6 \\
&= 86.0 \, m^3/h
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\dot{m}_{cw} \approx 86 \, m^3/h}$

### 3.4 The "Utility Cost Index" — A KTU Favorite

To compare utility efficiency across plants, engineers use:

$$\text{UCI} = \dfrac{\text{Total utility cost (₹/h)}}{\text{Product output (kg/h)}}$$

Lower UCI = more efficient plant. **Best-in-class petrochemical plants** achieve UCI ≈ **₹1.5–2.5 per kg of product**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Chemical Plant with Utilities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Storage] --> B[Pre-treatment Unit]
    B --> C[Reactor / Reaction Section]
    C --> D[Separation Section]
    D --> E[Purification]
    E --> F[Product Storage]

    U1[Steam Generation Plant] -->|LP/MP Steam| C
    U1 -->|MP Steam| D
    U2[Cooling Tower + CW Pumps] -->|Cooling Water| C
    U2 -->|Cooling Water| D
    U2 -->|Cooling Water| E
    U3[Demineralisation Plant] -->|DM Water| U1
    U3 -->|DM Water| C
    U4[Compressed Air Station] -->|Instrument Air| G1[Control Valves]
    U4 -->|Plant Air| H1[Pneumatic Tools]
    U5[Refrigeration Plant] -->|Chilled Water / Brine| C
    U6[Captive Power / Grid] -->|HT/LT Power| M1[Pumps]
    U6 -->|HT/LT Power| M2[Compressors]
    U6 -->|HT/LT Power| M3[Agitators]
    U7[N2 Plant / Storage] -->|Nitrogen Blanket| F
    U7 -->|Nitrogen Purge| C

    G1 -.Controls.-> C
    G1 -.Controls.-> D
    G1 -.Controls.-> E
```

### 4.2 Steam Pressure Cascade — Utility Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BOIL[Boiler HP Steam 40 bar] --> TURB[Turbine]
    TURB --> MP[MP Steam 15 bar]
    TURB --> LP[LP Steam 5 bar]
    MP --> R1[High Temp Reactor]
    LP --> R2[Reboilers / Heat Exchangers]
    LP --> R3[Trace Heating]
    MP --> R4[Steam Ejectors]
```

### 4.3 Water Treatment Hierarchy (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RW[Raw Water Source] --> SC[Screening & Sedimentation]
    SC --> SF[Sand Filtration]
    SF --> AC[Activated Carbon Filter]
    AC --> SOFT[Softener]
    SOFT --> DM[Demineraliser Ion Exchange]
    DM -->|DM Water| BFW[Boiler Feed Tank]
    DM -->|DM Water| PROC[Process User]
    SOFT -->|Soft Water| CW[Cooling Water Make-up]
    CW --> CT[Cooling Tower]
    CT -->|Recycled CW| USERS[Process Heat Exchangers]
    USERS -->|Hot CW Return| CT
```

### 4.4 Utility Failure Impact Tree (Why Importance Matters)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Electricity Failure] --> S1[All Pumps Stop]
    F1 --> S2[All Compressors Stop]
    S1 --> S3[Cooling Water Stops]
    S2 --> S4[Instrument Air Stops]
    S3 --> S5[Reactor Overheating]
    S4 --> S6[Control Valves Fail]
    S5 --> S7[EMERGENCY PLANT SHUTDOWN]
    S6 --> S7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The above tree clearly demonstrates a **cascade effect** — failure of just ONE utility (electricity) can shut down FOUR others (CW, IA, control, heating). This is the **KTU Board Examiner's favourite visual argument** for why utilities are "importantly important."

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Define process utilities. List any **four major process utilities** used in a chemical industry.

**Model Answer:**
Process utilities are the auxiliary services (energy and material inputs) that support plant operations but are not part of the final product.

Four major utilities:
1. **Steam** — for heating.
2. **Cooling water** — for heat removal.
3. **Compressed air** — for pneumatic control.
4. **Electricity** — for motors, pumps, lighting.

(Any four accepted; student can also mention fuel, refrigeration, inert gas.)

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Why is **cooling water** considered the most widely consumed utility in process plants?

**Model Answer:**
Cooling water is the most consumed utility because:
1. Almost every **exothermic reaction, condenser, and cooler** requires it.
2. Water has **high specific heat (4.186 kJ/kg·K)** and is cheap.
3. It is **recyclable** through cooling towers, reducing fresh demand.
4. Typical consumption: **50–100 m³ per ton of product** in refineries.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
>
> **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **different process utilities** used in a typical chemical plant. State the **importance** of each. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A reactor requires **5,000 MJ/h** of heat. Steam is available at **10 bar saturated (λ = 2,015 kJ/kg)**. Condensate is discharged at **100 °C**. Calculate the **steam flow rate** required. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

The answer must include the six utility categories with one-line importance:

1. **Water** — solvent, coolant, BFW; ensures continuous heat removal.
2. **Steam** — efficient heating medium; carries latent heat.
3. **Compressed Air** — drives pneumatic instruments and valves for safe control.
4. **Electricity** — powers all rotating equipment; without it the plant stops.
5. **Refrigeration** — enables low-temperature reactions and product stability.
6. **Fuel / Inert Gas** — provides energy for boilers and prevents fire/explosion.

Importance: Together they ensure **safety, continuity, quality, and economy** of plant operation. They occupy **40–60% of plant piping**.

(Include a neat block diagram showing utilities feeding into reactor, separator, and storage.)

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Given data**
$$Q = 5{,}000 \, MJ/h = 5 \times 10^6 \, kJ/h, \quad \lambda_s = 2015 \, kJ/kg$$
At 10 bar, $T_{sat} = 179.9 °C$, condensate exits at 100 °C.

**Step 2 — Sensible heat given by condensate cooling**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= 179.9 - 100 = 79.9 \, °C \\
C_p \cdot \Delta T &= 4.186 \times 79.9 = 334.46 \, kJ/kg
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total heat per kg of steam**

$$\begin{aligned}
q_{total} &= 2015 + 334.46 = 2349.46 \, kJ/kg
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Steam flow rate**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_s &= \dfrac{5 \times 10^6}{2349.46} \\
&= 2128.2 \, kg/h
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{m_s \approx 2.13 \, ton/h}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
>
> **(a)** Discuss the **role and importance of steam** as a process utility. Compare **LP, MP, and HP steam** with their typical applications. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A shell-and-tube heat exchanger removes **800 kW** of heat. Cooling water enters at **28 °C** and leaves at **42 °C**. Calculate (i) cooling water flow rate in **kg/s** and (ii) annual water cost in **₹** if water costs **₹8 per m³** and plant operates **8,000 hours/year**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Role of steam:**
1. Heating medium (carries latent heat 2,256 kJ/kg at 1 atm).
2. Atomizing fluid in burners.
3. Driver for turbines and ejectors.
4. Sterilization in pharma.
5. Humidification in air-conditioning.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | LP Steam | MP Steam | HP Steam |
|-----------|----------|----------|----------|
| Pressure | 3–5 bar | 10–15 bar | 40–100 bar |
| Temperature | 130–150 °C | 180–200 °C | 250–300 °C |
| Use | Reboilers, tracing | Process heating | Turbines, superheating |

**Importance:** Steam is the most versatile utility because of its high latent heat, easy transport, precise temperature control, and ability to be tapped at multiple pressure levels.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**(i) Cooling water flow rate**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{cw} &= \dfrac{Q}{C_p \cdot \Delta T} \\
&= \dfrac{800}{4.186 \times (42 - 28)} \\
&= \dfrac{800}{4.186 \times 14} \\
&= \dfrac{800}{58.604} \\
&= 13.65 \, kg/s
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Annual water volume and cost**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{cw} &= 13.65 \times 3.6 = 49.14 \, m^3/h \\
V_{annual} &= 49.14 \times 8000 = 3{,}93{,}120 \, m^3/year \\
\text{Cost} &= 3{,}93{,}120 \times 8 = ₹ \, 31{,}44{,}960
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Flow rate = $\boxed{13.65 \, kg/s}$
- Annual cost = $\boxed{₹ \, 31.45 \, lakhs \approx ₹ \, 31.5 \, lakhs}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't forget unit conversion** (MJ → kJ, kg/s → m³/h). Many students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 2. **Steam problems demand BOTH latent AND sensible heat** if condensate subcools. Skipping this is the most common deduction.
> 3. In Part A answers, **listing without explanation** fetches only 1.5/3. Add **one-line importance** for full marks.
> 4. Block diagrams must be **labelled** — a plain rectangle without unit names gets zero diagram marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision for KTU Board Exam**

- [x] **Definition**: Utilities = supporting services; not part of product.
- [x] **Six major utilities**: Water, Steam, Air, Refrigeration, Electricity, Fuel/Inert.
- [x] **Water is most consumed**; cooling water is recycled via cooling towers.
- [x] **Steam carries latent heat = 2,256 kJ/kg at 1 atm** — key numerical.
- [x] **Steam tiers**: LP (3–5 bar), MP (10–15 bar), HP (40+ bar).
- [x] **Instrument air** must be oil-free, dry, dew point **−40 °C**.
- [x] **C_p of water = 4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** — used in all CW calculations.
- [x] **Utility cost = 15–30%** of operating cost; up to 60% in energy-intensive plants.
- [x] **Utility piping = 40–60%** of total plant piping length.
- [x] **Refrigeration COP** = Q_cooling / W_input (always > 1).
- [x] **Inert gas (N₂)** is critical for fire/explosion prevention in flammable storage.
- [x] **Formula**: $Q = mC_p\Delta T$ (sensible), $Q = m\lambda$ (latent).
- [x] **UCI (Utility Cost Index)** = Total utility cost / Product output (lower is better).
- [x] Always convert **°C to K** in thermodynamic formulas.
- [x] Loss of electricity → cascading loss of CW, IA, and control → **plant trip**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water as a utility in process industries: sources of water, hard and soft water, methods of water treatment, recycle and conservation of water
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT636 - PROCESS UTILITY AND PIPING ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Water as a utility in process industries: sources of water, hard and soft water, methods of water treatment, recycle and conservation of water # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Water — The "Universal Solvent" as a Process Utility **Formal KTU Definition:** > Water is the most widely used **process utility** in chemical, petrochemical, fertilizer, food, pharmaceutical, and power-generation industries. It is required in **massive volumetric quantities** (often **200–500 m³ per tonne** of product in a typical chemical plant) for cooling, heating, steam generation, washing, dilution, reaction medium, and as a mass separating agent. In KTU's outcome-based framework (PECHT636), water is classified as a **primary utility** alongside steam, electricity, compressed air, and fuel. The syllabus treats water as the **single largest consumable raw material** in any process plant, even though it does not appear in the product's molecular formula. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECHT636 Module 1):** > The examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between: > (a) **Process water** (becomes part of the product or reactant), > (b) **Utility water** (cooling, boiler feed, washing), and > (c) **Domestic/Potable water** (drinking, sanitation). > This three-way classification is a **favourite 3-mark question**. ### Quantitative Footprint of Water in Industry | Industry Sector | Approx. Water Use (m³/tonne of product) | Major End Use | |---|---|---| | Petroleum Refining | **0.5 – 4** | Desalter, cooling, H₂S scrubbing | | Fertilizer (Urea/Ammonia) | **5 – 20** | Process & cooling | | Pulp & Paper | **100 – 250** | Pulp washing | | Steel Industry | **20 – 100** | Cooling, descaling | | Synthetic Fibres | **200 – 500** | Washing, spinning bath | | Thermal Power Plant | **1500 – 3000 (per MWh)** | Boiler + cooling tower | ### Conceptual Analogy — "Water is the Blood of a Process Plant" Think of a process plant as a **living human body**: - **Arteries** carry fresh water (intake lines) to where it is needed (reactors, exchangers). - **Veins** carry used water (effluent) back for treatment. - **Kidneys** (Effluent Treatment Plant, ETP) filter and recycle it. - **Heart** (water reservoir/pump house) provides the required pressure and flow. If the "blood" contains too much "calcium" or "magnesium" (hardness), it starts depositing inside the "arteries" (boiler tubes, heat exchangers) — causing **scaling, choking, and eventual failure**. Hence, **water treatment is preventive medicine for the plant**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Quality Metric (Remember this number!):** > - **Boiler feed water hardness:** < **0.5 ppm** (essentially zero). > - **Cooling water hardness:** **50–200 ppm** (controlled). > - **Drinking water hardness (WHO):** < **500 ppm** CaCO₃ equivalent. --- ## 1.2 Sources of Water — Where Does Industry Get Its Water? Process industries draw water from **four broad natural and reclaimed sources**, ranked by typical purity: ### A. Surface Water (Rivers, Lakes, Reservoirs) - **Characteristics:** High turbidity (suspended solids), color, organic matter, low dissolved minerals, microbial contamination. - **Treatment Required:** Sedimentation → Filtration → Disinfection. - **Typical Use:** Cooling towers, general plant washing. ### B. Ground Water (Wells, Boreholes, Springs) - **Characteristics:** Low turbidity, **high hardness** (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺), higher dissolved solids, generally free of microbes. - **Treatment Required:** Softening, iron removal, sometimes demineralization. - **Typical Use:** Boiler feed (after softening), potable water. ### C. Sea / Brackish Water (Coastal Plants) - **Characteristics:** Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) up to **35,000–45,000 ppm** (vs. <500 ppm for fresh water). - **Treatment Required:** Multi-Stage Flash (MSF), Reverse Osmosis (RO), or Multi-Effect Distillation (MED). - **Typical Use:** Cooling (single-pass), desalination for boiler feed in coastal power plants. ### D. Reclaimed / Recycled Water (Treated Effluent) - **Characteristics:** Variable quality; depends on upstream ETP. - **Treatment Required:** Tertiary treatment — RO, UV, polishing. - **Typical Use:** Cooling tower make-up, gardening, fire-fighting. > [!TIP] > **KTU Memory Trick — "S-G-S-R" (Sea, Ground, Surface, Reclaimed):** > Memorize this sequence to never confuse sources in the exam. --- ## 1.3 Hard Water vs. Soft Water — The Core Distinction > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner phrasing):** > **Hard water** is water that **does not produce lather readily with soap** because of the presence of dissolved **divalent cations**, primarily Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ (and to a lesser extent Fe²⁺, Mn²⁺, Sr²⁺). > **Soft water** is water that **lathers freely with soap** because it contains negligible concentrations of these hardness-causing cations. ### Intuition: The Soap Wasting Reaction $$ \text{Ca}^{2+}(aq) + 2\,\text{C}_{17}\text{H}_{35}\text{COO}^{-}\text{Na}^{+}(aq) \longrightarrow (C_{17}H_{35}COO)_2Ca \downarrow + 2\,Na^{+} $$ The insoluble calcium stearate ("scum") is what forms the **grey ring in your bathtub** instead of lather. Each 1 ppm of Ca²⁺ "kills" about **~0.16 ppm of soap** — this is why hard water is economically wasteful. ### Classification of Hardness **By Source of Cation:** 1. **Calcium Hardness:** Ca²⁺ (also called temporary + permanent). 2. **Magnesium Hardness:** Mg²⁺ (also called temporary + permanent). 3. **Total Hardness (TH):** Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺. **By Anion (which is the more useful classification for KTU!):** | Type | Cations | Anions | Removed by Boiling? | |---|---|---|---| | **Carbonate Hardness (Temporary)** | Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ | HCO₃⁻ | ✅ Yes | | **Non-carbonate Hardness (Permanent)** | Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ | Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, NO₃⁻ | ❌ No | ### Why the Name "Temporary"? Bicarbonates decompose on boiling: $$ \text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow $$ The CaCO₃ scale falls to the bottom of the kettle — hence the hardness "temporarily" disappears. Permanent hardness (CaSO₄, CaCl₂) cannot be removed by boiling because these salts are soluble even at 100 °C. ### Units of Hardness (KTU Frequently Asked!) Hardness is **always expressed as the equivalent concentration of CaCO₃** (M = 100 g/mol), even when the actual ion is Mg²⁺ or Fe²⁺. **Conversion Formula:** $$ \text{Hardness (as CaCO}_3, \text{ppm)} = \text{Actual mass of ion (ppm)} \times \frac{50}{\text{Equivalent weight of ion}} $$ | Ion | Atomic/Molar Mass | Equivalent Weight | Multiplier → CaCO₃ | |---|---|---|---| | Ca²⁺ | 40 | 20 | **2.5** | | Mg²⁺ | 24 | 12 | **4.17** | | Fe²⁺ | 56 | 28 | **1.79** | | HCO₃⁻ | 61 | 61 | **0.82** | | CO₃²⁻ | 60 | 30 | **1.67** | | SO₄²⁻ | 96 | 48 | **1.04** | | Cl⁻ | 35.5 | 35.5 | **1.41** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Industry MUST Remove Hardness — Engineering Consequences Hard water causes **four major operational problems** that every KTU student must be able to name: | Problem | Mechanism | Industrial Impact | |---|---|---| | **Scaling** | CaCO₃, CaSO₄ deposit on hot surfaces | Boiler tube failure, reduced heat transfer | | **Corrosion** | Dissolved O₂, CO₂, Cl⁻ attack metal | Pipe leakages, EDC (Equipment Damage Cost) | | **Foaming** | Na₂SO₄, organic matter in boilers | Carryover of water into steam, turbine blade erosion | | **Microbial fouling** | Nutrients for bacteria/algae in cooling water | Biofilm, Legionella, reduced cooling efficiency | > [!NOTE] > **Threshold numbers (must memorize):** > - Langelier Saturation Index (LSI) must be between **−0.5 and +0.5** for cooling water (slightly scaling to slightly corrosive). > - Boiler feed water: silica < **0.02 ppm** (to prevent turbine blade deposition). --- ## 2.2 Internal Treatment vs. External Treatment (Key KTU Distinction) This distinction is worth **7 marks** in a typical KTU ESE. ### A. External Treatment (Before water enters the plant) Performed at the **central water treatment plant (WTP)**. Examples: - Lime-soda softening - Ion exchange - Reverse osmosis - Deaeration ### B. Internal Treatment (Chemicals dosed inside boilers/cooling systems) Examples: - Phosphate dosing (forms Ca₃(PO₄)₂ sludge instead of hard scale). - Sodium sulphite (O₂ scavenger). - Chelants like EDTA (sequesters Ca²⁺). --- ## 2.3 Methods of Water Treatment — The Master Flow A complete industrial water treatment scheme follows this **logical sequence**: 1. **Pre-chlorination** → kills algae & microbes. 2. **Screening** → removes large debris. 3. **Aeration** → removes Fe²⁺, H₂S, CO₂; oxidizes metals. 4. **Coagulation & Flocculation** → alum [Al₂(SO₄)₃] or FeCl₃ + polyelectrolyte. 5. **Sedimentation** → gravity settling in clarifier (residence time 2–4 h). 6. **Filtration** → rapid sand filter (RSF) or multimedia filter. 7. **Softening (Lime-Soda or Zeolite)** → removes Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺. 8. **Disinfection** → Cl₂, O₃, or UV. 9. **Demineralization (if boiler-grade)** → cation + anion exchanger → mixed bed. 10. **Storage & Distribution** → to cooling tower, boiler, process. > [!TIP] > **KTU Mnemonic — "S-A-C-S-F-S-D-D-S-D":** > **S**creening → **A**eration → **C**oagulation → **S**edimentation → **F**iltration → **S**oftening → **D**isinfection → **D**eionization → **S**torage → **D**istribution. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Table for Problem-Solving > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are commonly tested in numerical problems (Part B, 14 marks).** > Practice applying each at least once. | # | Concept | Formula | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Hardness as CaCO₃ equivalent | $\text{CaCO}_3 \text{ (ppm)} = \dfrac{m \times 50}{E}$ | ppm | | 2 | Molarity relationship | $\text{Molarity} = \dfrac{\text{Strength (g/L)}}{\text{Molar Mass}}$ | mol/L | | 3 | Normality of hardness | $N = \dfrac{\text{Strength (g/L of CaCO}_3\text{)}}{\text{Equivalent weight (50)}}$ | eq/L | | 4 | Lime requirement (Carbonate hardness) | $\text{Lime (mg/L)} = \dfrac{74}{100}\bigl[\text{CO}_2 + \text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2 + \text{Mg(HCO}_3)_2 + \text{MgCO}_3\bigr]$ (all as CaCO₃) | mg/L | | 5 | Lime requirement (Total) | $\text{Lime} = \dfrac{74}{100}\bigl[\text{CO}_2 + \text{CO}_3^{2-} + \text{HCO}_3^- + \text{Mg}^{2+}\bigr]$ (as CaCO₃) | mg/L | | 6 | Soda-ash requirement | $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 = \dfrac{106}{100}\bigl[\text{Non-carbonate hardness} + \text{Mg}^{2+}\bigr]$ (as CaCO₃) | mg/L | | 7 | Zeolite exchange capacity | $E = \dfrac{(V \times H)}{W}$ | meq/g | | 8 | Langelier Saturation Index | $\text{LSI} = \text{pH}_{actual} - \text{pH}_s$ | dimensionless | | 9 | Recycle ratio | $R = \dfrac{\text{Recycled flow}}{\text{Total feed flow}}$ | fraction | | 10 | Cycles of concentration (COC) | $\text{COC} = \dfrac{\text{C}_\text{blowdown}}{\text{C}_\text{make-up}} = \dfrac{\text{Total dissolved solids (cycles)}}{\text{Make-up TDS}}$ | — | ### Key Explanations **Formula 4 — Lime Requirement:** - **74** = molecular weight of Ca(OH)₂. - **100** = molecular weight of CaCO₃ (the reference). - The expression in brackets is the **CO₂ + alkalinity + Mg²⁺** expressed in CaCO₃ equivalents. **Formula 6 — Soda Ash Requirement:** - **106** = molecular weight of Na₂CO₃. - Soda ash is needed to precipitate the **non-carbonate hardness** (CaSO₄, MgSO₄, CaCl₂) AND to remove the Mg²⁺ left over after lime treatment. **Formula 10 — COC:** - Higher COC ⇒ less water consumption BUT more scaling potential. - Typical cooling tower: COC = **3 to 6**. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **In Refineries:** Demineralized water is used for hydrocracker reactors where trace chloride can cause **SCC (Stress Corrosion Cracking)** of stainless steel. - **In Pharmaceuticals:** Water for Injection (WFI) requires conductivity < **1.3 µS/cm** at 25 °C — achieved only by **multi-effect distillation + RO**. - **In Fertilizer Plants:** Boiler feed water must be silica-free; otherwise silica volatilizes and deposits on **turbine blades** at 500+ °C, causing imbalance. - **In Power Plants:** A 500 MW plant uses **~30,000 m³/day** of demineralized water — a single day's outage due to poor water = **₹1+ crore revenue loss**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Equivalent Weight of CaCO₃ and Why It Is the Reference **Step 1: Definition of Equivalent Weight** $$ E = \dfrac{\text{Molar Mass (M)}}{\vert \text{Valency factor} \vert} $$ **Step 2: Apply to CaCO₃** - Molar mass of CaCO₃ = 40 (Ca) + 12 (C) + 3×16 (O) = **100 g/mol**. - Valency factor = 2 (Ca²⁺ is balanced by CO₃²⁻). - Therefore: $E_{CaCO_3} = 100 / 2 = \mathbf{50 \text{ g/eq}}$ **Step 3: Why CaCO₃ is the universal reference** - It has a **convenient equivalent weight of 50**. - Most hardness-causing ions have much smaller equivalent weights (Ca²⁺ = 20, Mg²⁺ = 12), so expressing hardness "as CaCO₃" inflates the numerical value but keeps stoichiometry consistent. - It is the **standard reference** recommended by APHA, AWWA, and IS 3025. --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Conversion of Mg²⁺ Hardness to CaCO₃ Equivalent **Given:** 12 mg/L of Mg²⁺ present in water. **Step 1:** Equivalent weight of Mg²⁺ $$ E_{Mg^{2+}} = \dfrac{24}{2} = 12 \text{ g/eq} $$ **Step 2:** Equivalents per litre $$ \dfrac{12 \text{ mg/L}}{12 \text{ g/eq}} = 1 \text{ meq/L} $$ **Step 3:** Express as CaCO₃ (1 meq of any ion = 1 meq of CaCO₃) $$ \text{CaCO}_3 \text{ equivalent} = 1 \text{ meq/L} \times 50 \text{ g/eq} = 50 \text{ mg/L} $$ **Step 4:** Alternative shortcut using the multiplier $$ \text{Hardness as CaCO}_3 = 12 \times \dfrac{50}{12} = 12 \times 4.17 = \mathbf{50 \text{ mg/L}} $$ ✅ Both methods agree — confirms the **multiplier table (Section 1.3)**. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example — Complete Water Analysis Report **Problem (KTU Model):** A water sample analysis reports the following concentrations: - Ca(HCO₃)₂ = 81 mg/L - Mg(HCO₃)₂ = 73 mg/L - MgSO₄ = 60 mg/L - CaSO₄ = 68 mg/L - NaCl = 50 mg/L **Find:** (i) Total Hardness, (ii) Carbonate Hardness, (iii) Non-carbonate Hardness, (iv) Alkalinity, all in **ppm as CaCO₃**. ### Solution — Step by Step | Salt | MW | Conc (mg/L) | Ion Causing Hardness | As CaCO₃ (mg/L) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Ca(HCO₃)₂ | 162 | 81 | Ca²⁺ = 81 × (40/162) = 20 mg/L | 20 × (100/80) = **25** | | Mg(HCO₃)₂ | 146 | 73 | Mg²⁺ = 73 × (24/146) = 12 mg/L | 12 × (100/48) = **25** | | MgSO₄ | 120 | 60 | Mg²⁺ = 60 × (24/120) = 12 mg/L | 12 × (100/48) = **25** | | CaSO₄ | 136 | 68 | Ca²⁺ = 68 × (40/136) = 20 mg/L | 20 × (100/80) = **25** | | NaCl | 58.5 | 50 | No hardness (Na⁺) | — | **Step 1: Total Hardness (TH)** $$ TH = 25 + 25 + 25 + 25 = \mathbf{100 \text{ ppm as CaCO}_3} $$ **Step 2: Carbonate (Temporary) Hardness** - Only salts with HCO₃⁻ contribute. - Ca(HCO₃)₂ = 25 ppm, Mg(HCO₃)₂ = 25 ppm. $$ CH = 25 + 25 = \mathbf{50 \text{ ppm}} $$ **Step 3: Non-carbonate (Permanent) Hardness** $$ NCH = TH - CH = 100 - 50 = \mathbf{50 \text{ ppm}} $$ **Step 4: Alkalinity (due to HCO₃⁻ only — no CO₃²⁻ or OH⁻ here)** $$ \text{Alkalinity} = (81 + 73) \times \dfrac{50}{122} = \mathbf{63.1 \text{ ppm}} $$ (where 122 = 2 × equivalent weight of HCO₃⁻ = 61 × 2). > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** Always tabulate — examiners reward **systematic presentation** with **0.5–1 bonus mark** above the rubric. --- ## 3.4 Derivation — Lime & Soda Ash Dosage **Problem:** A raw water has the following analysis (all as CaCO₃): - CO₂ = 20 mg/L - Ca(HCO₃)₂ = 60 mg/L - Mg(HCO₃)₂ = 50 mg/L - MgSO₄ = 40 mg/L - CaSO₄ = 30 mg/L **Calculate:** (a) Lime [Ca(OH)₂] dose, (b) Soda ash [Na₂CO₃] dose. ### Reactions Taking Place (Memorize!) **Lime reactions:** $$ \text{CO}_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ $$ \text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ $$ \text{Mg(HCO}_3)_2 + 2\,\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mg(OH)}_2\downarrow + 2\,\text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ $$ \text{MgCO}_3 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mg(OH)}_2\downarrow + \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow $$ $$ \text{MgSO}_4 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mg(OH)}_2\downarrow + \text{CaSO}_4 $$ **Soda ash reactions:** $$ \text{CaSO}_4 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 $$ $$ \text{MgSO}_4 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{MgCO}_3\downarrow + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 $$ (then MgCO₃ reacts with lime to give Mg(OH)₂). ### Step 1 — Lime Requirement (as CaCO₃ equivalents) $$ \text{Lime (as CaCO}_3\text{)} = \text{CO}_2 + \text{CO}_3^{2-} + \text{HCO}_3^- + \text{Mg}^{2+} \text{ (all as CaCO}_3\text{)} $$ $$ = 20 + 0 + (60 + 50) + (50 + 40) = 20 + 0 + 110 + 90 = \mathbf{220 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3} $$ ### Step 2 — Convert Lime to Actual Ca(OH)₂ $$ \text{Lime} = 220 \times \dfrac{74}{100} = 220 \times 0.74 = \mathbf{162.8 \text{ mg/L of Ca(OH)}_2} $$ ### Step 3 — Soda Ash Requirement $$ \text{Soda (as CaCO}_3\text{)} = \text{Non-carbonate hardness} + \text{Mg}^{2+} \text{ (all as CaCO}_3\text{)} $$ $$ = (\text{CaSO}_4 + \text{MgSO}_4) + \text{Mg from MgSO}_4 = 30 + 40 + 40 = \mathbf{110 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3} $$ (Note: Mg²⁺ from Mg(HCO₃)₂ is **already removed by lime** as Mg(OH)₂, so it's not double-counted.) ### Step 4 — Convert Soda to Actual Na₂CO₃ $$ \text{Soda} = 110 \times \dfrac{106}{100} = 110 \times 1.06 = \mathbf{116.6 \text{ mg/L of Na}_2\text{CO}_3} $$ ### Final Answer - **Lime (Ca(OH)₂) needed = 162.8 mg/L** - **Soda ash (Na₂CO₃) needed = 116.6 mg/L** --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Softening Methods | Method | Removes | Capital | Operating | Treated Water Hardness | Pros | Cons | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Lime-Soda** | Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ (partially) | Medium | Low (chemicals) | 30–50 ppm | Cheap, removes silica & Fe | Sludge disposal, manual operation | | **Zeolite (Ion Exchange)** | Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ | Low | Low (salt regeneration) | <10 ppm | Continuous, compact | Cannot remove Fe, oil, turbidity | | **Strong Acid Cation (SAC) + Strong Base Anion (SBA)** | All ions (demineralization) | High | High (acid + caustic) | <1 ppm | Boiler-grade water | Skilled operation | | **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** | 95–99% of all ions | High | High (membrane replacement, energy) | <10 ppm | Removes bacteria, organics | Membrane fouling | | **EDR (Electrodialysis)** | Ionic species | High | Medium | 10–50 ppm | No chemicals | Limited removal | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Process Flow — Industrial Water Treatment Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Water Source
River / Lake / Borewell] --> B[Raw Water Storage Tank] B --> C[Screening
Bar Screen] C --> D[Aeration Tower
CO2 and Fe removal] D --> E[Coagulation Flocculation Tank
Alum + Polyelectrolyte] E --> F[Clarifier Sedimentation Basin] F --> G[Rapid Sand Filter RSF] G --> H[Chlorination Contact Tank] H --> I[Softening Unit
Lime-Soda or Zeolite] I --> J[Treated Water Storage Tank] J --> K{Distribution Decision} K --> L[Boiler Feed DM Plant
SAC + SBA + Mixed Bed] K --> M[Cooling Tower Make-up] K --> N[Process Water Header] L --> O[Deaerator] O --> P[Boiler] M --> Q[Cooling Tower Basin] Q --> R[Recycled to Plant] P --> S[Steam Header] R --> B ``` ## 4.2 Hardness Removal Logic Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hard Water Input
Ca2+ Mg2+ Fe2+] --> B{Softening Method?} B --> C[Lime-Soda Process] B --> D[Zeolite Ion Exchange] B --> M[RO Reverse Osmosis] C --> C1[CaCO3 Sludge] C --> C2[Soft Water 30-50 ppm] D --> D1[Brine Regeneration NaCl] D --> D2[Soft Water 10 ppm] M --> M1[Reject Brine] M --> M2[Permeate 10 ppm] C2 --> E[Soft Water Outlet] D2 --> E M2 --> E E --> F{Distribution} F --> G[Boiler] F --> H[Cooling Tower] F --> I[Process Vessel] ``` ## 4.3 Water Recycle & Conservation Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Process Plant] --> W1[Wastewater Stream 1] P --> W2[Wastewater Stream 2] P --> W3[Cooling Tower Blowdown] W1 --> ETP[Effluent Treatment Plant ETP] W2 --> ETP W3 --> ETP ETP --> TR[Tertiary Treatment RO UV] TR --> R1[Recycled Water Header] R1 --> CT[Cooling Tower Make-up] R1 --> FW[Fire Water Storage] R1 --> GW[Garden and Toilet Flush] R1 --> BFW[Boiler Make-up after polishing] BFW --> P CT --> P ``` ## 4.4 Equivalent Weight & Hardness Conversion Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw ppm of Ion X] --> B{Multiply by 50 / EqWt of X} B --> C[Hardness as CaCO3 ppm] C --> D{Total of all ions} D --> E[Total Hardness ppm] C --> F{Anion is HCO3-?} F -->|Yes| G[Carbonate Temporary Hardness] F -->|No| H[Non-carbonate Permanent Hardness] G --> I[Removable by Boiling] H --> J[Removable only by Softening] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** **Q:** Differentiate between **temporary hardness** and **permanent hardness**. Mention one industrial method to remove each. **Model Answer:** - **Temporary hardness** is caused by bicarbonates of calcium and magnesium [Ca(HCO₃)₂, Mg(HCO₃)₂]. It can be removed simply by **boiling** because bicarbonates decompose to insoluble carbonates that settle out. - **Permanent hardness** is caused by chlorides and sulphates of calcium and magnesium (CaCl₂, MgSO₄, etc.) which are soluble even at 100 °C. It can only be removed by **chemical treatment** such as the **lime-soda process**, **ion exchange (zeolite)**, or **reverse osmosis**. --- ### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]** **Q:** List **any three sources of water** used in process industries and state one characteristic of each. **Model Answer:** 1. **Surface water** (river, lake): High turbidity and microbial content; requires sedimentation and disinfection. 2. **Ground water** (borewell): High mineral content (hardness); requires softening before boiler use. 3. **Sea water** (coastal plants): Very high TDS (~35,000 ppm); requires desalination via MSF or RO. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU ESE – Model Paper Style]** **(a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, describe the **complete industrial water treatment scheme** from raw water intake to boiler feed, mentioning the **purpose of each unit** (7 marks). **(b)** A water sample analysis (all values in mg/L) is as follows: - Ca(HCO₃)₂ = 162, Mg(HCO₃)₂ = 73, CaSO₄ = 136, MgSO₄ = 120, MgCl₂ = 95. **Calculate:** - (i) Total Hardness, (ii) Carbonate Hardness, (iii) Non-carbonate Hardness, all as **CaCO₃ equivalent in mg/L** (7 marks). --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks** **Flowchart description (verbal equivalent):** 1. **Intake & Raw Water Storage:** Captures water from source; provides buffer; doses alum + chlorine. 2. **Screening:** Removes leaves, twigs, large debris (bar spacing 25–100 mm). 3. **Aeration:** Removes dissolved CO₂, H₂S; oxidizes Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ (which precipitates). 4. **Coagulation–Flocculation:** Alum [Al₂(SO₄)₃·18H₂O] hydrolyses to Al(OH)₃ floc that captures colloidal impurities. 5. **Sedimentation (Clarifier):** 2–4 h retention; gravity settling reduces turbidity from ~100 NTU to ~10 NTU. 6. **Filtration (RSF):** Sand + gravel media; brings turbidity down to <1 NTU. 7. **Softening (Lime-Soda or Zeolite):** Removes Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺. 8. **Disinfection:** Chlorine (1–2 ppm residual) kills pathogens. 9. **Demineralization (SAC + SBA + Mixed Bed) for boiler feed:** Reduces TDS to <1 ppm. 10. **Storage & Distribution:** Treated water routed to boiler, cooling tower, and process headers. > [!NOTE] > Each step in the above carries **0.7 mark**, totaling **7 marks**. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks** **Tabulated analysis:** | Salt | MW | Conc (mg/L) | Cation of interest | mg/L as Ca²⁺ or Mg²⁺ | Multiplier | as CaCO₃ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Ca(HCO₃)₂ | 162 | 162 | Ca²⁺ | 162 × 40/162 = 40 | × 2.5 | **100** | | Mg(HCO₃)₂ | 146 | 73 | Mg²⁺ | 73 × 24/146 = 12 | × 4.17 | **50** | | CaSO₄ | 136 | 136 | Ca²⁺ | 136 × 40/136 = 40 | × 2.5 | **100** | | MgSO₄ | 120 | 120 | Mg²⁺ | 120 × 24/120 = 24 | × 4.17 | **100** | | MgCl₂ | 95 | 95 | Mg²⁺ | 95 × 24/95 = 24 | × 4.17 | **100** | **(i) Total Hardness:** $$ TH = 100 + 50 + 100 + 100 + 100 = \mathbf{450 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3} $$ **(ii) Carbonate (Temporary) Hardness:** Only HCO₃⁻ salts. $$ CH = 100 + 50 = \mathbf{150 \text{ mg/L}} $$ **(iii) Non-carbonate (Permanent) Hardness:** $$ NCH = TH - CH = 450 - 150 = \mathbf{300 \text{ mg/L}} $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU ESE Style]** **(a)** Explain the **lime-soda process** of water softening with **all chemical reactions**. Compute the **lime and soda ash requirements** for a water sample containing the following (all as CaCO₃, mg/L): CO₂ = 10, Ca(HCO₃)₂ = 100, Mg(HCO₃)₂ = 70, MgSO₄ = 30, CaSO₄ = 20 (7 marks). **(b)** Discuss **methods of water conservation and recycle** in process industries with **two specific case studies** (cooling tower blowdown recycle and boiler steam condensate recovery) (7 marks). --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks** **Lime-Soda Process Reactions (3 marks):** | Reaction | Equation | |---|---| | 1 | $\text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | | 2 | $\text{Mg(HCO}_3)_2 + 2\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mg(OH)}_2\downarrow + 2\text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | | 3 | $\text{MgSO}_4 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mg(OH)}_2\downarrow + \text{CaSO}_4$ | | 4 | $\text{CaSO}_4 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ | | 5 | $\text{CO}_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | **Numerical Computation (4 marks):** **Step 1:** Lime requirement (as CaCO₃) $$ \text{Lime} = \text{CO}_2 + \text{HCO}_3^- + \text{Mg}^{2+} = 10 + (100 + 70) + (70 + 30) = 10 + 170 + 100 = \mathbf{280 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3} $$ **Step 2:** Lime (Ca(OH)₂) in mg/L $$ \text{Lime} = 280 \times \dfrac{74}{100} = \mathbf{207.2 \text{ mg/L of Ca(OH)}_2} $$ **Step 3:** Soda ash requirement (as CaCO₃) $$ \text{Soda} = \text{NCH} + \text{Mg}^{2+}\text{ from NCH} = (30 + 20) + 30 = \mathbf{80 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3} $$ **Step 4:** Soda ash in mg/L $$ \text{Soda} = 80 \times \dfrac{106}{100} = \mathbf{84.8 \text{ mg/L of Na}_2\text{CO}_3} $$ **Final Answer:** Lime = 207.2 mg/L, Soda = 84.8 mg/L. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks** **A. Cooling Tower Blowdown Recycle (3.5 marks):** - A cooling tower continuously evaporates water (~1% of circulation per 10 °C cooling range) and discharges a portion as **blowdown** to control TDS. - Blowdown (typically 3000–5000 ppm TDS) can be **recycled to the cooling tower itself** after passing through a **side-stream filter + RO** for TDS reduction. - This reduces fresh water intake by **30–50%**. - **COC optimization**: Increasing cycles from 4 to 6 cuts blowdown by 33%. **B. Boiler Steam Condensate Recovery (3.5 marks):** - Steam condensate (after heat exchange) is **almost pure water** (~10–50 ppm TDS) since it has never been in contact with the process. - Recovering it directly to the **boiler feed water tank** saves: - **60–80% of treated make-up water demand.** - Significant energy (condensate is hot — saves deaerator steam). - Reduces chemical cost (already demineralized). - Typical condensate recovery systems include: **condensate return pots, flash tanks, and pump sets.** - Modern plants achieve **>90% condensate recovery**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the 74/100 conversion factor** for lime — only writing "280 mg/L" loses 2 marks. > 2. **Double counting Mg²⁺** in lime AND soda formulas — you lose 1 mark per error. > 3. **Writing "soft water"** without specifying **which ion was removed** — vague answers lose marks in 14-mark questions. > 4. **Skipping the flowchart** in part (a) — examiners require it for full marks. > 5. **Confusing "ppm" and "mg/L"** — for dilute aqueous solutions, they are numerically equal, but for seawater (TDS = 35,000 ppm), 1 ppm ≠ 1 mg/L by density. Mention this if asked. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ⭐ **Hardness is ALWAYS expressed as CaCO₃ equivalent** — Eq. Wt. = 50. - ⭐ **Conversion multipliers** — Ca²⁺: 2.5, Mg²⁺: 4.17, HCO₃⁻: 0.82. - ⭐ **Temporary hardness** = bicarbonate hardness (removable by boiling). - ⭐ **Permanent hardness** = Cl⁻ + SO₄²⁻ + NO₃⁻ hardness (needs chemical softening). - ⭐ **Sources of water** — S-G-S-R mnemonic: Sea, Ground, Surface, Reclaimed. - ⭐ **Treatment sequence** — "S-A-C-S-F-S-D-D-S-D" (Screening → Distribution). - ⭐ **Lime formula** — 74/100 × (CO₂ + alkalinity + Mg²⁺ as CaCO₃). - ⭐ **Soda formula** — 106/100 × (NCH + Mg²⁺ as CaCO₃). - ⭐ **COC (Cycles of Concentration)** — typical 3 to 6 for cooling towers. - ⭐ **LSI** must be in range −0.5 to +0.5. - ⭐ **Recycle priorities** — Condensate > Cooling tower blowdown > Treated ETP water. - ⭐ **Three big problems of bad water** — Scaling, Corrosion, Foaming. - ⭐ **KTU hot keywords** — Demineralization, ETP, RO, MSF, SAC/SBA ion exchange.

topics.title: Cooling tower: constructional details, working, types and performance evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT636 - PROCESS UTILITY AND PIPING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cooling tower: constructional details, working, types and performance evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **cooling tower** is an open, direct-contact, evaporative heat rejection device that transfers the sensible and latent heat absorbed in a circulating water stream to the atmosphere, primarily through the evaporation of a small fraction of that water. In a process plant, it is classified as a critical *process utility* because nearly every condenser, reactor jacket, and product cooler ultimately dumps its waste heat into the cooling water loop, which must then be re-cooled before re-circulation.

In KTU terminology, the performance of a cooling tower is described by two temperature-based indicators:
- **Range** ($\Delta T$): the temperature drop of water as it passes through the tower.
- **Approach** ($T_{approach}$): how close the cold water temperature comes to the ambient wet-bulb temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition:** *“A cooling tower is a heat transfer equipment in which atmospheric air is brought into direct contact with water to cool it by sensible heat transfer and evaporative (latent) heat transfer, with the wet-bulb temperature being the theoretical lower limit of cooling.”*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a cooling tower as a **"giant controlled outdoor air conditioner for water"**. When you step out of a swimming pool on a windy day, you feel cold even if the air temperature is the same as your body — because water *evaporates* from your skin, taking away latent heat. A cooling tower does the same thing on a massive industrial scale:

- **Hot water from the plant** = the “skin” that needs cooling.
- **Atmospheric air** = the “wind” that carries the evaporated moisture away.
- **Fill (packing)** = the rough towel that spreads the water out so more surface is exposed to air.
- **Fans** = an extra “breeze” when natural wind is not enough.
- **Drift eliminators** = the wind-shield that stops salty droplets from escaping onto the neighborhood.

> [!NOTE]
