> **Why we cannot use air-cooled fin-fan exchangers everywhere:** In a tropical refinery, the wet-bulb temperature of Cochin/Kerala (~28 °C) sets a much lower cooling limit than the dry-bulb (~33 °C). Evaporative cooling allows us to push water down to within 3–5 °C of the wet-bulb — something air-cooled units physically cannot achieve economically.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value (Industrial) |
|---|---|
| Approach to wet-bulb | **3 °C to 5 °C** (good design) |
| Range | **5 °C to 12 °C** (process water duty) |
| Evaporation loss | **0.85 % per 10 °C range** |
| Drift loss | **0.005 % to 0.02 %** of circulation rate |
| L/G ratio | **1.0 to 2.5** (typical mechanical draft) |
| Cooling tower effectiveness | **55 % to 75 %** |
| Air specific volume (humid) | **0.85 to 0.95 m³/kg** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle – The Physics in 4 Steps

1. **Hot water distribution** – Warm process water (e.g., 42 °C) from condensers is pumped to the top of the tower and spread evenly over the **fill** through spray nozzles or gravity distributors.
2. **Sensible heat transfer** – As water falls through the tower, a small temperature drop occurs because warmer water transfers heat directly to the cooler incoming air (conduction/convection).
3. **Latent heat transfer (evaporation)** – A small fraction (~1–2 %) of the water evaporates into the air stream. Because the latent heat of vaporization of water is very large (**~2,430 kJ/kg** at 30 °C), this phase change removes the majority of the heat.
4. **Cool water collection** – The cooled water (e.g., 32 °C) falls into the **cold-water basin/sump** and is pumped back to the process.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The wet-bulb temperature ($T_{wb}$) is the *theoretical minimum* exit temperature of water. Real towers always operate 3–5 °C *above* the wet-bulb due to finite contact time and finite air–water interface.

### 2.2 Role of Each Constructional Component

| Component | Function | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Casing / Shell** | Houses the fill, supports structure, prevents air bypass | FRP, RCC, Galvanized steel, Wood |
| **Fill (Packing)** | Increases air–water contact area & contact time | PVC (splash & film types) |
| **Water Distributor** | Spreads water uniformly over fill | Spray nozzles / gravity troughs |
| **Drift Eliminators** | Capture entrained water droplets (>0.005 %) | PVC cellular or zig-zag plates |
| **Air Inlet Louvers** | Allow uniform air entry, prevent water splash-out | FRP, PVC |
| **Fan** | Move air (in induced/forced draft) | FRP blades, cast aluminium hub |
| **Fan Stack / Cylinder** | Discharge humid air above structure | FRP, RCC |
| **Cold Water Basin** | Collects cooled water, holds the inventory | RCC with anti-algae coating |
| **Strainer / Make-up line** | Removes debris; replenishes evaporation losses | — |

### 2.3 Classification of Cooling Towers (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

**A. Based on air movement:**
1. **Natural Draft** (Atmospheric / Hyperbolic chimney) – relies on density difference.
2. **Mechanical Draft** – uses fans.
   - **Forced Draft** (fan pushes air in from below).
   - **Induced draft** (fan pulls air out from above).
3. **Atmospheric / Natural draft with wind** – no fan.

**B. Based on water–air flow direction:**
1. **Counter-flow** (water down, air up) – highest performance.
2. **Cross-flow** (water down, air horizontal) – lower height, easier maintenance.
3. **Parallel-flow** (rare).

**C. Based on heat transfer mode:**
1. **Wet (evaporative)** – common.
2. **Dry (air-cooled, finned coils)** – water conservation.
3. **Hybrid / Wet-Dry** – combination for arid regions.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam ready. Memorize the units, they are checked in Part A.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Range** | $\Delta T = T_{hot\,water} - T_{cold\,water}$ | °C |
| 2 | **Approach** | $T_{app} = T_{cold\,water} - T_{wb}$ | °C |
| 3 | **Cooling tower effectiveness** | $\eta = \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_{hot} - T_{wb}} \times 100$ | % |
| 4 | **Evaporation loss** | $E = 0.00085 \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ (% of circulation) | % |
| 5 | **Drift loss** | $D = 0.005$ to $0.02$ % of circulation | % |
| 6 | **Blowdown** | $B = \dfrac{E}{Cycles - 1}$ | % |
| 7 | **Make-up water** | $M = E + D + B$ | % |
| 8 | **Cycles of concentration** | $Cycles = \dfrac{C_{cyc}}{C_{makeup}}$ | — |
| 9 | **L/G ratio** | $\dfrac{L}{G} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{dry\,air}}$ | kg/kg |
| 10 | **Merkel number (NTU)** | $NTU = \dfrac{K \cdot a \cdot V}{L} = \displaystyle\int_{T_1}^{T_2} \dfrac{dT}{h'' - h}$ | — |
| 11 | **Evaporative heat duty** | $Q = L \cdot c_p \cdot (T_1 - T_2)$ | kW |
| 12 | **Sensible heat ratio** | $\dfrac{Q_{sensible}}{Q_{evaporative}} \approx \dfrac{1}{50}$ | — |

> **Note on $C_p$ in evaporation formula:** $C_p \approx 1.8$ for normal cooling applications.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical refineries (BPCL Kochi, CPCL Chennai):** Cooling water from hydrocracker overhead condensers is recycled through induced-draft counter-flow cooling towers.
- **Thermal power plants (NTPC Kayamkulam):** Hyperbolic natural-draft cooling towers handle ~100,000 m³/h circulation.
- **HVAC systems (airports, malls):** Use cross-flow induced-draft packaged units.
- **Pharma/Food plants (Kerala):** Hybrid wet–dry towers conserve water during summer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Cooling Tower Effectiveness

**Starting principle:** Effectiveness is defined as the ratio of *actual temperature drop* to the *maximum possible temperature drop* (which is bounded by the wet-bulb temperature).

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Actual cooling achieved}}{\text{Maximum possible cooling}}
$$

**Step 1:** Write actual cooling achieved (the range):
$$
\Delta T = T_{hot\,water} - T_{cold\,water}
$$

**Step 2:** Write the maximum possible cooling (limited by ambient wet-bulb):
$$
\Delta T_{max} = T_{hot\,water} - T_{wb}
$$

**Step 3:** Take the ratio and express as a percentage:
$$
\eta = \frac{T_{hot\,water} - T_{cold\,water}}{T_{hot\,water} - T_{wb}} \times 100\,\%
$$

This is the formula board examiners expect.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Evaporation Loss Coefficient

**Assumption:** For every 1 kg of water evaporated, ~2,430 kJ of heat is removed. The mass of evaporation is therefore obtained from an energy balance on the water side.

**Step 1:** Energy balance per kg of circulating water:
$$
L \cdot c_p \cdot (T_1 - T_2) = E \cdot \lambda_{fg}
$$

where:
- $L$ = mass of circulating water (kg/h)
- $c_p$ = specific heat of water = **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**
- $\lambda_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization ≈ **2,430 kJ/kg** at 30 °C
- $E$ = mass of water evaporated (kg/h)

**Step 2:** Solve for $E/L$ (fractional evaporation):
$$
\frac{E}{L} = \frac{c_p \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{\lambda_{fg}} = \frac{4.186 \cdot \Delta T}{2430}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate numerically:
$$
\frac{E}{L} = 0.00172 \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to percentage and introduce the empirical constant $C_p \approx 1.8$ (a correction factor used by industry for drift, sensible heat, and ambient humidity variations):
$$
E\,(\%) = 0.00085 \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

This is the standard **Kern’s rule of thumb** used in all KTU numericals.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Merkel Equation (Population of the Integral)

**Objective:** To relate the height of the packing (z) to the heat transfer process.

**Step 1:** For a small differential height $dz$, write the heat transferred from water to air:
$$
dQ = h \cdot a \cdot (T_{water} - T_{wb}) \cdot dV
$$

where $h$ = enthalpy transfer coefficient, $a$ = interfacial area per unit volume, $V$ = volume element.

**Step 2:** Equate it to the change in water enthalpy (approx. $c_p dT$ for water):
$$
L \cdot c_p \cdot dT = h \cdot a \cdot (h'' - h) \cdot dV
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to enthalpy driving force instead of temperature, then separate variables:
$$
\frac{h \cdot a \cdot V}{L} = \int_{h_1}^{h_2} \frac{dT}{h'' - h} \cdot \text{(constant correction)}
$$

**Step 4:** Final Merkel equation (for design rating):
$$
NTU_{Mer} = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{C_p \cdot dT}{h'' - h}
$$

> **KTU Examiner Note:** Merkel’s equation is *evaluated graphically* by plotting $\dfrac{1}{h'' - h}$ vs. $T$ between the inlet and outlet water temperatures. The area under the curve = Merkel number.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical (KTU Sample Problem Style)

> **Problem:** A cooling tower handles 100,000 kg/h of circulating water. Hot water enters at 42 °C, leaves at 32 °C. The ambient wet-bulb is 28 °C. Calculate: (i) range, (ii) approach, (iii) effectiveness, (iv) evaporation loss (% and kg/h), (v) blowdown if cycles of concentration = 3.

**Step (i) Range:**
$$
\Delta T = 42 - 32 = 10\,°C
$$

**Step (ii) Approach:**
$$
T_{app} = 32 - 28 = 4\,°C
$$

**Step (iii) Effectiveness:**
$$
\eta = \frac{42 - 32}{42 - 28} \times 100 = \frac{10}{14} \times 100 = 71.43\,\%
$$

**Step (iv) Evaporation loss (take $C_p = 1.8$):**
$$
E\,(\%) = 0.00085 \times 1.8 \times 10 = 0.0153\,\% \text{ of circulation}
$$

In kg/h:
$$
E = 0.000153 \times 100{,}000 = 15.3\,\text{kg/h}
$$

**Step (v) Blowdown:**
$$
B = \frac{E}{Cycles - 1} = \frac{0.0153}{3 - 1} = 0.00765\,\% \text{ of circulation} = 7.65\,\text{kg/h}
$$

**Step (vi) Make-up water:**
$$
M = E + D + B \approx 15.3 + 5 + 7.65 = 27.95\,\text{kg/h} \text{ (taking drift = 0.005 %)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake to Avoid in Exam:** Students often use $C_p = 1$ instead of $1.8$. Always quote both the percentage and the absolute value in kg/h.

---

### 3.5 Comparative Performance Matrix of Cooling Tower Types

| Parameter | Natural Draft (Hyperbolic) | Forced Draft | Induced Draft (Cross-flow) | Induced Draft (Counter-flow) | Atmospheric |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Air movement | Buoyancy | Fan (push) | Fan (pull) | Fan (pull) | Wind |
| Approach to wb | 4–6 °C | 3–4 °C | 3–5 °C | 2–3 °C | 5–8 °C |
| Water flow direction | Counter | Counter | Cross | Counter | Counter |
| Recirculation of humid air | Low | **High** (problem) | Moderate | Lowest | Low |
| Power consumption | Zero | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Zero |
| Typical application | Large power plants | Small-medium plants | Medium industrial | **Refineries/petrochem** | Small HVAC |
| Initial cost | **Very high** | Low | Moderate | Moderate–High | Low |
| Maintenance | Low | Moderate | **High** (fan at bottom) | High | Low |
| Height | **100–180 m** | 6–12 m | 8–15 m | 8–18 m | 4–8 m |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Point to Remember:** In *forced-draft* towers, the fan sits at the air inlet, so **recirculation of hot moist air back into the inlet** is a chronic problem — induced draft towers are preferred for refineries and are the most commonly asked type in KTU papers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Counter-Flow Induced Draft Cooling Tower

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Hot Water from Process Condensers 42C] --> B[Inlet Header Pipe at Top]
    B --> C[Water Distribution Nozzles / Spray System]
    C --> D[Fill or Packing PVC Splash Film Type]
    D --> E[Drift Eliminators Zig-Zag PVC Plates]
    E --> F[Induced Draft Fan Axial FRP Blades]
    F --> G[Humid Air Discharge to Atmosphere]
    H[Atmospheric Air Inlet Louvers] --> D
    D --> I[Cold Water Basin RCC Sump]
    I --> J[Cooled Water Pump 32C]
    J --> K[Return to Plant Process Loop]
    L[Make-up Water Replenishment] --> I
    M[Drift and Blowdown Drain] --> I
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Process Flow of an Industrial Cooling Water Circuit

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Process Plant Heat Sources] --> P2[Hot Return Header 42C]
    P2 --> P3[Cooling Tower Top Distribution]
    P3 --> P4[Evaporative Heat Transfer with Air]
    P4 --> P5[Cold Water Basin 32C]
    P5 --> P6[Cooling Water Pumps]
    P6 --> P7[Return to Plant 32C]
    P5 --> P8[Makeup Water City or Treated]
    P5 --> P9[Blowdown to ETP]
    A1[Atmospheric Air Inlet] --> P4
    P4 --> A2[Humid Air to Atmosphere]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Type Diagram — Forced Draft vs Induced Draft

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph FD[Forced Draft Tower]
        FD1[Fan Below Fill] --> FD2[Pushes Air Upward]
        FD2 --> FD3[Air Exits Through Top Stack]
        FD4[Water Falls Down Through Fill]
    end
    subgraph ID[Induced Draft Tower]
        ID1[Air Enters Louvers at Bottom]
        ID1 --> ID2[Rises Through Fill Counter to Water]
        ID2 --> ID3[Fan on Top Pulls Air Out]
    end
    classDef towerStyle fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    class FD,ID towerStyle
```

### 4.4 Merkel Plot Reference (Graphical Concept for KTU 2024)

```mermaid
graph LR
    X[Water Temperature T C on X axis] --> Y[Inverse Driving Force 1 over h double prime minus h on Y axis]
    Y --> Z[Area Under Curve equals Merkel Number NTU]
    Z --> W[Determines Required Tower Height Z]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** A physical sketch of hyperbolic tower and forced-draft fan arrangement is often asked for 7-mark questions. Use clear labels for *fan, fill, water distributor, eliminator, basin, air direction* — partial credit is awarded for each label.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the terms 'Range' and 'Approach' of a cooling tower. Why is approach never equal to zero?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Range** is the difference between the temperature of hot water entering the tower and the cold water leaving the tower: $\Delta T = T_{in} - T_{out}$ (1 mark).
- **Approach** is the difference between the cold water leaving temperature and the ambient wet-bulb temperature: $T_{app} = T_{out} - T_{wb}$ (1 mark).
- Approach can never be zero because the wet-bulb temperature is the *theoretical thermodynamic limit*; to achieve it would require *infinite* contact area, infinite air flow, and zero exit-air temperature driving force — all physically impossible. (1 mark).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Distinguish between forced draft and induced draft cooling towers. Which is preferred for refinery duty?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Feature | Forced Draft | Induced Draft |
|---|---|---|
| Fan location | At the **bottom** (air inlet) | At the **top** (air exit) |
| Pressure | Tower is under **positive** pressure | Tower is under **negative** pressure |
| Recirculation | **High** (humid air re-enters) | **Low** (humid air is thrown out upward) |
| Fan maintenance | Difficult (in hot humid air) | Easy (in hot dry air) |

Induced draft counter-flow towers are preferred for refinery duty due to **low approach, less recirculation, and uniform air distribution** (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-1 Internal Choice Questions

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 style]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of an **induced-draft counter-flow cooling tower**. List the function of each major component. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A cooling tower circulates 50,000 kg/h of water. Hot water enters at 45 °C and leaves at 30 °C. The ambient wet-bulb temperature is 25 °C. Calculate (i) range, (ii) approach, (iii) effectiveness, (iv) evaporation loss in % and kg/h, (v) blowdown for cycles of concentration = 4. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) – 7 marks:**

**Working Principle (2 marks):** Hot water from the plant (heat exchangers, condensers) is pumped to the top header and distributed uniformly over the **fill** through spray nozzles. Atmospheric air is drawn in through the **louvers** at the base by the **induced-draft fan** at the top. As water trickles down through the PVC fill, it meets the rising air in **counter-current**. Heat is transferred from the water to the air by:
- **Sensible heat transfer** (≈ 1/50 of total).
- **Latent heat transfer** (≈ 49/50 of total) — through evaporation of a small fraction of water.

Cooled water collects in the **basin** at the bottom and is pumped back to the plant.

**Major components & functions (5 marks, 0.5 each):**
1. **Casing/Shell** – structural enclosure, prevents air bypass.
2. **Water distribution system** – uniform spread of water over fill.
3. **Fill/Packing** – enhances air–water contact area and time.
4. **Drift eliminators** – capture entrained water droplets.
5. **Air inlet louvers** – allow uniform air entry, prevent splash-out.
6. **Induced-draft fan** – creates negative pressure to pull air through tower.
7. **Fan stack** – discharges humid air above the structure.
8. **Cold water basin** – collects and stores cooled water.
9. **Make-up water line** – replaces evaporated water.
10. **Blowdown line** – removes concentrated solids.

**Part (b) – 7 marks:**

**Given:** $L = 50{,}000$ kg/h; $T_1 = 45$ °C; $T_2 = 30$ °C; $T_{wb} = 25$ °C; $Cycles = 4$; $C_p = 1.8$.

**(i) Range (1 mark):**
$$
\Delta T = 45 - 30 = 15\,°C
$$

**(ii) Approach (1 mark):**
$$
T_{app} = 30 - 25 = 5\,°C
$$

**(iii) Effectiveness (1 mark):**
$$
\eta = \frac{45 - 30}{45 - 25} \times 100 = \frac{15}{20} \times 100 = 75\,\%
$$

**(iv) Evaporation loss (2 marks):**
$$
E\,(\%) = 0.00085 \times 1.8 \times 15 = 0.02295\,\%
$$
$$
E = 0.0002295 \times 50{,}000 = 11.475\,\text{kg/h}
$$

**(v) Blowdown (2 marks):**
$$
B\,(\%) = \frac{0.02295}{4 - 1} = 0.00765\,\%
$$
$$
B = 0.0000765 \times 50{,}000 = 3.825\,\text{kg/h}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 style]*

**(a)** Explain the **Merkel equation** for cooling tower performance. State its significance and describe a **graphical method** of evaluating it. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **natural-draft, mechanical-draft forced, and induced-draft** cooling towers on the basis of (i) air movement mechanism, (ii) approach to wet-bulb, (iii) recirculation tendency, (iv) typical application, and (v) cost. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) – 7 marks:**

**Statement of Merkel equation (3 marks):**
Merkel’s equation expresses the number of transfer units (NTU) required to achieve a given cooling of water:
$$
NTU = \int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{C_p \, dT}{h'' - h}
$$
where $h''$ is the enthalpy of air saturated at water temperature $T$ and $h$ is the enthalpy of the bulk air stream.

**Significance (2 marks):**
- It combines **sensible and latent heat transfer** into a single driving force (enthalpy difference).
- It gives the **required height of packing** ($Z = NTU \cdot L / (K \cdot a)$).
- It is the **rating equation** used by CTI (Cooling Tower Institute) for performance evaluation.

**Graphical method (2 marks):**
1. Plot $\dfrac{1}{h'' - h}$ on the Y-axis vs. water temperature $T$ on the X-axis between $T_1$ and $T_2$.
2. Mark the **operating line** of the air (assuming L/G ratio and entering air condition).
3. Mark the **saturation curve** ($h''$) of the air.
4. The **area under the curve** between $T_1$ and $T_2$ = Merkel number.
5. The tower height $Z = (NTU \cdot L) / (K \cdot a)$ where $K \cdot a$ is determined experimentally for the chosen fill.

**Part (b) – 7 marks:** (1.4 marks each, compare in tabular form)

| Parameter | Natural Draft | Forced Draft | Induced Draft |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air movement | Buoyancy of warm humid air | Fan below fill pushes air | Fan above fill pulls air |
| Approach to wet-bulb | 4–6 °C | 3–4 °C | 2–3 °C |
| Recirculation | Low (very tall) | **High** (problem) | Low (fan throws air up) |
| Typical application | Thermal power plants | Small industrial / HVAC | **Refineries, petrochemicals** |
| Power consumption | **Zero** (no fan) | Moderate | Moderate |
| Capital cost | **Very high** (huge concrete shell) | Low | Moderate–High |
| Footprint area | Large | Compact | Compact |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — always write kg/h, °C, %.
> 2. **Using $C_p = 1$** in the evaporation formula — KTU answer keys strictly use **$C_p = 1.8$**.
> 3. **Not labelling the sketch** — in 7-mark sketch questions, partial credit (typically 2 marks) is awarded purely for the labelled diagram. Draw the **fan, fill, water distribution, drift eliminator, basin, air flow arrows, water flow arrows**.
> 4. **Confusing approach with range** — Approach is with respect to **wet-bulb**, not dry-bulb.
> 5. **In Merkel equation** — students write $T_{wb}$ in the denominator; it must be **enthalpy difference** $(h'' - h)$.
> 6. **For blowdown** — formula is $B = E / (Cycles - 1)$, not $B = E / Cycles$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A cooling tower cools water by **evaporative (latent) heat transfer**, not by refrigeration.
- The **wet-bulb temperature** is the absolute thermodynamic lower limit; approach is always > 0.
- **Range** = inlet − outlet water temperature; **Approach** = outlet − wet-bulb temperature.
- **Evaporation loss** ≈ 0.00085 × $C_p$ × $\Delta T$ (with $C_p = 1.8$).
- **Effectiveness** $\eta = (T_{hot} - T_{cold}) / (T_{hot} - T_{wb}) \times 100$ %.
- **Make-up water** = Evaporation + Drift + Blowdown.
- **Blowdown** = E / (Cycles − 1); controls scale formation in the basin.
- **Forced draft** = fan pushes air from below; high recirculation is a chronic problem.
- **Induced draft** = fan pulls air from above; preferred for refineries.
- **Natural draft (hyperbolic)** = no fan, used in large power plants, very tall (~150 m).
- **Counter-flow** = water down, air up → best performance, lowest approach.
- **Cross-flow** = water down, air horizontal → lower tower height, easier maintenance.
- **Fill types** = splash (for dirty water), film (for clean water, higher performance).
- **Drift eliminators** are mandatory in all modern towers (regulatory + water-conservation).
- **Merkel equation** uses *enthalpy driving force* $(h'' - h)$, integrated graphically.
- **L/G ratio** is the primary design variable — optimum for counter-flow ≈ 1.0 to 2.5.
- **Drift loss** is in the range 0.005 %–0.02 % — do not confuse it with evaporation.
- In KTU 2024 exams, always draw a **neat labelled sketch** for the 7-mark question — 2 marks are for the figure alone.
- For numerical problems, **memorize**: range, approach, effectiveness, evaporation %, blowdown, make-up — these are the most frequently asked.
- **L/G ratio** high → water in excess → tower acts like a sensible cooler (less evaporation).
- **L/G ratio** low → air in excess → high evaporation, high drift, tower may freeze in cold climates.
- The **latent heat of vaporization** of water at 30 °C is **2,430 kJ/kg** — useful in derivations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to fuels: properties of fuel oil, coal and gas, storage, handling and preparation of fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT636 - PROCESS UTILITY AND PIPING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to fuels: properties of fuel oil, coal and gas, storage, handling and preparation of fuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — FUEL FUNDAMENTALS FOR PROCESS UTILITIES

## 1.1 Core Definition & Scope (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **fuel** is any combustible substance which on combustion in air (or oxygen) liberries a significant amount of heat that can be harnessed economically for domestic, industrial, or process heating purposes. In the context of Process Utility Engineering, fuels are the **primary thermal energy carriers** that drive boilers, furnaces, fired heaters, reformers, gas turbines, and incinerators.

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes industrial fuels into three physical states:

| State | Examples | Typical Industrial Role |
|---|---|---|
| Solid | Coal, lignite, bagasse, biomass | Utility boilers, cement kilns |
| Liquid | Fuel oil (Furnace oil FO, LSHS), diesel, naphtha | Process heaters, furnaces |
| Gaseous | Natural gas (NG), LPG, biogas, refinery off-gases | Fired heaters, GT combustors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Process utility engineers must master (i) **properties** that dictate selection, (ii) **storage** that determines safety footprint, (iii) **handling** that controls logistics, and (iv) **preparation** that ensures complete and clean combustion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Industrial Food Chain"

Think of a chemical plant as a living organism. **Fuels are the food** that keeps its thermal organs (boilers, fired heaters) alive. Just as the human body rejects greasy or contaminated food, a boiler rejects:
- *High-viscosity* fuel (cannot be atomized),
- *Wet* coal (steals combustion heat for evaporation),
- *Sour* gas (corrodes tubes via SO₂/SO₃).

Hence, **fuel specification → storage → handling → preparation** mirrors **purchase → cold-storage → transport to kitchen → cooking (atomization/pulverization)** in a household.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** All fuel properties converge toward ONE design target — **reliable, complete, and clean combustion at the rated firing rate with minimum excess air**.

### 1.3 Primary vs. Secondary Fuels

- **Primary (Natural) Fuels**: Coal, crude oil, natural gas — extracted directly from nature.
- **Secondary (Derived) Fuels**: Coke, fuel oil, LPG, producer gas, biogas — obtained by processing primary fuels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Secondary fuels dominate industrial use because their **fixed composition** allows predictable boiler design and stable heat release.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Process Utilities

| Process Utility | Fuel Used | Why That Fuel? |
|---|---|---|
| Steam Boiler (Utility) | FO / NG / PC coal | High GCV, bulk availability |
| Catalytic Reformer Heater | NG / LPG | Clean, soot-free radiant heat |
| Rotary Kiln (Cement) | Pet coke / coal | Solid + continuous duty |
| Gas Turbine | NG / syngas | Gaseous → instant mixing |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Properties of Fuel Oil (Furnace Oil / LSHS)

Fuel oil is the residual fraction from crude refining, characterized by the following engineering properties:

| Property | Definition | Typical Range | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Specific Gravity** (at 15 °C) | Ratio of oil density to water density at 15 °C | **0.94 – 0.98** | Determines tank sizing and pump head |
| **API Gravity** | Inverse scale of specific gravity | 10 – 22 °API | Trade metric; lower API ⇒ heavier oil |
| **Kinematic Viscosity** | Resistance to flow at given temperature | 30 – 380 cSt at 50 °C | Governs atomizer selection |
| **Flash Point** | Lowest temp at which vapor ignites | **66 °C (FO)** – 130 °C | Storage safety & IMDG classification |
| **Pour Point** | Temp at which oil ceases to flow | 10 – 30 °C | Heated storage/piping required |
| **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** | Heat released by complete combustion | **10 200 – 10 800 kcal/kg** | Boiler efficiency baseline |
| **Sulfur Content** | Mass fraction of S in oil | 1 – 4 % | SO₂ emissions; corrosion |
| **Water & Sediment** | Entrained water + solids | < 1 % | Combustion disruption, burner damage |
| **Ash** | Incombustible residue | < 0.1 % | Slagging, convection pass fouling |
| **Carbon Residue (Conradson)** | Coke-forming tendency | 2 – 12 % | Indicates burner tip fouling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **API Gravity Conversion (KTU must-know):**
> $$\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{SG_{15}} - 131.5$$
> $$SG_{15} = \frac{141.5}{131.5 + \text{°API}}$$

### 2.2 Properties of Coal

Coal is assessed via **two analytical pillars**:

#### (a) Proximate Analysis (engineering bench-test)

| Constituent | Definition | Effect on Combustion |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture (M) | Inherent + surface water | Latent heat loss (2260 kJ/kg) |
| Volatile Matter (VM) | Gases released at 950 °C (excl. moisture) | Flame length, ignition ease |
| Fixed Carbon (FC) | Solid combustible residue | Sustained combustion, char burnout |
| Ash (A) | Incombustible mineral residue | Slagging, fouling, erosion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Check:** $M + VM + FC + A = 100\%$ (dry basis if moisture excluded).

#### (b) Ultimate Analysis (chemical assay)

Coal composition expressed as mass fractions of **C, H, O, N, S + Ash + Moisture**. Used in stoichiometric air and emission calculations.

#### (c) Key Derived Properties

| Property | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|
| **GCV (kcal/kg)** | Total heat from combustion at constant volume |
| **NCV / LCV (kcal/kg)** | GCV − latent heat of water vapor in flue gas |
| **Hardgrove Grindability Index (HGI)** | Ease of pulverization; **> 50 → soft coal** |
| **Coking Index** | Swelling tendency; critical for stoker selection |
| **Ash Fusion Temperature** | Determines slagging limits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dulong's Formula (KTU favorite for numerical):**
> $$\boxed{\text{GCV}_{\text{dry}} \;=\; 8080\,C \;+\; 34500\!\left(H - \tfrac{O}{8}\right) \;+\; 2240\,S \quad \text{kcal/kg}}$$
> where C, H, O, S are **mass fractions on dry basis** from ultimate analysis.

#### NCV from GCV:

$$\boxed{\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9\,H \cdot \frac{L}{100} - M \cdot L \cdot 0.01}$$

where $L = 586$ kcal/kg at 25 °C (latent heat of vaporization of water), $H$ is hydrogen mass %, $M$ is moisture %.

### 2.3 Properties of Gaseous Fuels

| Property | NG Typical | LPG Typical |
|---|---|---|
| Composition | CH₄ 85–95 %, C₂H₆ 2–8 % | C₃H₈ / C₄H₆ mix |
| GCV (kcal/Nm³) | **8 500 – 9 500** | 25 000 – 28 000 |
| Specific Gravity (air = 1) | 0.55 – 0.70 | 1.5 – 2.0 |
| Wobbe Index (kcal/Nm³) | 11 000 – 13 000 | 18 000 – 20 000 |
| Flame speed (m/s) | 0.4 | 0.8 – 1.0 |
| Sulphur (as H₂S) | < 5 mg/Nm³ | < 50 mg/Nm³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wobbe Index** — the most cited property in gas fuel selection:
> $$I_W = \frac{\text{GCV}}{\sqrt{SG_{\text{gas}/\,\text{air}}}}$$
> It characterizes **interchangeability** of fuel gases in a burner — gases with equal Wobbe Index deliver the same thermal energy at the same burner pressure.

### 2.4 Master KTU Formula & Property Cheat-Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Expression | Unit | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dulong GCV | $8080C + 34500(H-O/8) + 2240S$ | kcal/kg | Coal heat duty |
| 2 | NCV | $GCV - 9H - M$ correction | kcal/kg | Boiler efficiency |
| 3 | °API | $\frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ | °API | Oil grading |
| 4 | Wobbe Index | $GCV / \sqrt{SG}$ | kcal/Nm³ | Gas interchangeability |
| 5 | Stoich. O₂ for coal | $C + 0.5H + 1.5S - O$ | kmol/kg C-basis | Air requirement |
| 6 | Stoich. air mass | $O_2 \times 4.32$ | kg/kg fuel | Combustion calc |
| 7 | Excess air | $(A_{\text{actual}} - A_{\text{stoich}})/A_{\text{stoich}}$ | % | Combustion tuning |
| 8 | CV of mixture | $\sum (y_i \cdot CV_i)$ | kcal/Nm³ | Blended gas |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU quick recall):** $1\text{ Nm}^3$ of NG ≈ $1.0\text{ L}$ of FO ≈ $1.4\text{ kg}$ of coal (on GCV basis).

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Properties

- **Viscosity & pour point** decide the **trace-heating and pumping temperature** in fuel-oil rings.
- **HGI** selects the **pulverizer type** (ball mill vs. bowl mill) in a coal-fired utility boiler.
- **Wobbe Index** validates whether refinery off-gas can substitute NG without re-tuning the burner.
- **Sulphur content** dictates the **FGD (Flue Gas Desulfurization)** design or selection of low-S fuel for clean-area plants.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Dulong's Formula Application

**Problem [KTU Pattern]:** A coal sample has the ultimate analysis (dry basis):
$C = 70\%$, $H = 5\%$, $O = 10\%$, $S = 1\%$, $N = 2\%$, Ash = $12\%$.
Calculate GCV and NCV (with 5 % inherent moisture).

### Step 1 — Verify Mass Balance

$$C + H + O + S + N + \text{Ash} = 70 + 5 + 10 + 1 + 2 + 12 = 100\,\% \;\; \checkmark$$

### Step 2 — Apply Dulong's Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV}_{\text{dry}} &= 8080\,C \;+\; 34500\!\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) \;+\; 2240\,S \\[4pt]
&= 8080 \times 0.70 \;+\; 34500 \times \left(0.05 - \frac{0.10}{8}\right) \;+\; 2240 \times 0.01 \\[4pt]
&= 5656 \;+\; 34500 \times (0.05 - 0.0125) \;+\; 22.4 \\[4pt]
&= 5656 \;+\; 34500 \times 0.0375 \;+\; 22.4 \\[4pt]
&= 5656 \;+\; 1293.75 \;+\; 22.4 \\[4pt]
\text{GCV}_{\text{dry}} &= \mathbf{6972.15 \;\text{kcal/kg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to As-Received (5 % Moisture) GCV

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV}_{\text{ar}} &= \text{GCV}_{\text{dry}} \times (1 - M) - 6\,M \\
&= 6972.15 \times 0.95 - 6 \times 5 \\
&= 6623.54 - 30 \\
\text{GCV}_{\text{ar}} &= \mathbf{6593.54 \;\text{kcal/kg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute NCV

Water of combustion: $9H = 9 \times 5 = 45$ kg water per 100 kg fuel
Latent heat loss: $45 \times 586 = 26370$ kcal per 100 kg = 263.7 kcal/kg
Moisture latent loss: $5 \times 586/100 = 29.3$ kcal/kg

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NCV}_{\text{ar}} &= \text{GCV}_{\text{ar}} - 263.7 - 29.3 \\
&= 6593.54 - 293.0 \\
\text{NCV}_{\text{ar}} &= \mathbf{6300.5 \;\text{kcal/kg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Stoichiometric Air for Methane

**Problem:** Calculate theoretical air required for complete combustion of 1 Nm³ of pure CH₄ at STP.

### Step 1 — Write Combustion Equation

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### Step 2 — Mole Balance

1 Nm³ CH₄ → 2 Nm³ O₂ (stoichiometric)

### Step 3 — Convert O₂ to Air

Air contains 21 % O₂ by volume → Air needed

$$
A_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{2}{0.21} = \mathbf{9.524 \;\text{Nm}^3\; \text{air per Nm}^3\;\text{CH}_4}
$$

### Step 4 — Account for 20 % Excess Air

$$
A_{\text{actual}} = A_{\text{stoich}} \times 1.20 = \mathbf{11.43 \;\text{Nm}^3\;\text{air/Nm}^3\;\text{CH}_4}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Wobbe Index for Blended Gas

**Problem:** A refinery blends **60 % NG** (GCV = 9 000 kcal/Nm³, SG = 0.6) with **40 % off-gas** (GCV = 4 500 kcal/Nm³, SG = 0.9). Calculate the Wobbe Index of the blend.

### Step 1 — Composite GCV by Kay's Rule

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV}_{\text{blend}} &= \sum y_i \, \text{GCV}_i = (0.6)(9000) + (0.4)(4500) \\
&= 5400 + 1800 = \mathbf{7200 \;\text{kcal/Nm}^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Composite SG by Kay's Mixing Rule

$$
\begin{aligned}
SG_{\text{blend}} &= \sum y_i \, SG_i = (0.6)(0.6) + (0.4)(0.9) \\
&= 0.36 + 0.36 = \mathbf{0.72}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Wobbe Index

$$
I_W = \frac{\text{GCV}_{\text{blend}}}{\sqrt{SG_{\text{blend}}}} = \frac{7200}{\sqrt{0.72}} = \frac{7200}{0.8485} = \mathbf{8485 \;\text{kcal/Nm}^3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The blend's $I_W = 8485$ falls within the **NG interchangeability band (11 000–13 000)** → the burner must be **re-tilted** to operate safely.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Fuel Oil | Coal | Natural Gas |
|---|---|---|---|
| Storage form | Bulk liquid (tank farm) | Stockpile / bunkers / silos | Gas holders / bullets / pipeline |
| Preparation intensity | Moderate (heating + atomization) | High (crushing, drying, pulverizing) | Low (filtration, pressure regulation) |
| Atomization / Feeding | Pressure / steam-assisted | Pulverized fuel burners | Gas nozzles (no atomizer) |
| Combustion cleanliness | Medium (SO₂, particulates) | Low (SO₂, NOₓ, ash, PM) | High (only NOₓ concern) |
| Capital cost of handling | Medium | High (mills, conveyors) | Low |
| Operating flexibility | Limited by viscosity | Limited by HGI / moisture | Excellent |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Industrial Fuel Cycle (Solid + Liquid + Gas)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Coal Stockpile] --> A2[Crusher Primary]
    A2 --> A3[Crusher Secondary]
    A3 --> A4[Coal Dryer]
    A4 --> A5[Pulverizer Mill]
    A5 --> A6[Pneumatic Conveyor]
    A6 --> A7[Pulverized Coal Burner]
    A7 --> A8[Utility Boiler]

    B1[Crude Oil Storage Tank] --> B2[FO Pre-Heater 90C]
    B2 --> B3[Oil Pump House]
    B3 --> B4[Steam Atomizer]
    B4 --> B5[Furnace Oil Burner]
    B5 --> A8

    C1[Natural Gas Pipeline] --> C2[Pressure Regulating Skid]
    C2 --> C3[Gas Filter / Scrubber]
    C3 --> C4[Gas Pre-Heater]
    C4 --> C5[Gas Burner Nozzle]
    C5 --> A8

    A8 --> D1[Steam Turbine / Process Header]
    A8 --> D2[Flue Gas Treatment ESP FGD]
    D2 --> D3[Stack Emission]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Fuel Oil Storage & Forwarding System

```mermaid

    S1[Tank Truck / Barge Receipt] --> S2[Inlet Sump Strainer]
    S2 --> S3[Receiving Tank Fixed Roof]

    S4 --> S5[Day Tank with Heated Coil]
    S5 --> S6[Steam Heated Oil Pre-Heater]
    S6 --> S7[Viscosity Governor]
    S7 --> S8[Burner Assembly with Steam Atomizer]
    S8 --> S9[Furnace Boiler]

```

## 4.3 Process Block — Coal Preparation Train

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Coal Wagon Tippler] --> P2[Conveyor to Crusher House]
    P2 --> P3[Vibrating Screen 25 mm]
    P3 --> P4[Ring Granulator Crusher]
    P4 --> P5[Coal Bunker 2000 Tonnes]
    P5 --> P6[Bunker Discharge Feeder]
    P6 --> P7[Pulverizer Ball Mill Bowl Mill]
    P7 --> P8[Classifier Return Oversize]
    P8 --> P9[PF Bin 70 Percent thru 200 Mesh]
    P9 --> P10[Coal Pipe to Burners]
    P10 --> P11[Furnace Flame Zone]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Gas Fuel Conditioning Unit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[Gas Inlet from Pipeline] --> G2[Coalescing Filter]
    G2 --> G3[Pressure Reducing Station 2 x 100 percent]
    G3 --> G4[Gas Heater for Dew Point]
    G4 --> G5[Knockout Drum for Condensate]
    G5 --> G6[Pressure Regulating Valve]
    G6 --> G7[Gas Burner Manifold]
    G7 --> G8[Fired Heater Combustion Zone]
    G2 --> G9[Filter Drain LP Flare]
    G5 --> G10[Condensate Pump to Slop]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Gross Calorific Value (GCV) and Net Calorific Value (NCV) of a fuel. Why is NCV used in boiler efficiency calculations?**

**Model Answer:**
- **GCV** is the total heat liberated by complete combustion of unit mass (or volume) of fuel, with the products of combustion cooled back to the original fuel temperature, **including** the latent heat of condensation of water vapor formed during combustion.
- **NCV** is the heat liberated by complete combustion, **excluding** the latent heat of water vapor (i.e., water leaves as vapor).
- **Boiler efficiency uses NCV** because in actual boiler operation, **flue gases leave at 150–200 °C**, well above the dew point, so the latent heat of water vapor is **not recovered** — it constitutes a real heat loss.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Explain the significance of Wobbe Index for gaseous fuels.**

**Model Answer:**
The Wobbe Index $I_W = \text{GCV}/\sqrt{SG}$ characterizes the **heat output** delivered by a gas burner at a **constant supply pressure**. Two gases with equal Wobbe Index are **interchangeable** in the same burner without re-tuning. In process utilities, this is critical when switching between NG, LPG, or refinery off-gas in **fired heaters** — the Wobbe Index must remain within the burner's specified band (±5 %) to ensure flame stability and complete combustion.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — Fuel Oil Systems (14 Marks)

**[a] [7 Marks] Discuss the important properties of fuel oil that govern its selection as a furnace fuel in a process plant.**

**Model Answer:**

The properties that govern fuel-oil selection are:

1. **Calorific Value (GCV/NCV)** — Determines the volume of fuel to be stored and the firing rate for a given heat duty. FO typically delivers **10 200–10 800 kcal/kg**.
2. **Viscosity** — Atomization quality depends on viscosity. At the burner tip viscosity should be **≤ 15 cSt**; therefore FO is heated to **100–120 °C** before atomization.
3. **Flash Point & Fire Point** — Dictate storage code (IMDG). Flash point > **66 °C** classifies FO as Class "C" petroleum.
4. **Pour Point** — Below pour point, oil ceases to flow → storage tanks, transfer lines, and pumps must be **steam-traced**.
5. **Sulphur Content** — High S → SO₂ emission, acid dew point corrosion in economizers.
6. **Water & Sediment** — Centrifuged out to < 1 %; protects burner nozzles and pumps.
7. **Carbon Residue (Conradson)** — Indicates fouling tendency on burner tips and convection tubes.
8. **Specific Gravity / API Gravity** — Used for volume-mass conversions and tank calibration.

**[b] [7 Marks] With the aid of a block diagram, describe the storage, handling and preparation of fuel oil in a process plant.**

**Model Answer:**

| Stage | Equipment | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Receipt | Tank-truck gantry / jetty | Receiving FO by road or marine tanker |
| Bulk Storage | Fixed-roof steel tanks | Atmospheric storage at ambient; floating roof for lighter grades |
| Heating | Internal steam coils in tanks | Maintain oil at **50–60 °C** for pumpability |
| Transfer | Centrifugal pumps (Duty + Standby) | Move oil from bulk tank to day tank |
| Day Tank | Heated tank with level control | Buffer storage close to burner house |
| Pre-Heating | Shell-and-tube steam heaters | Raise oil to atomization temperature (**100–120 °C**) |
| Viscosity Control | Viscosity governor + RTD | Maintains **10–15 cSt** for atomizer |
| Atomization | Pressure / steam / air atomizer | Disperses oil into fine droplets |
| Combustion | Refractory-lined burner | Releases thermal energy |

**Block Diagram — see Section 4.2 above.**

The system ensures **continuous firing**, **safe storage (flash point compliant)**, and **clean, complete combustion** at the rated firing rate.

---

### QUESTION B — Coal and Gas (14 Marks)

**[a] [7 Marks] Explain proximate and ultimate analysis of coal. What is the role of Hardgrove Grindability Index (HGI)?**

**Model Answer:**

**Proximate Analysis** is an empirical decomposition of coal into four fractions (on % by mass):

| Fraction | Method | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture | Heating at 105–110 °C | Surface + inherent water |
| Volatile Matter | Heating at 950 °C for 7 min (excl. moisture) | Gases + tars released |
| Fixed Carbon | By difference, after subtracting VM, M, Ash | Solid combustible char |
| Ash | Combustion residue at 815 °C | Non-combustible mineral matter |

**Ultimate Analysis** is the chemical assay expressed as **mass % of C, H, N, S, O + Ash + Moisture**. It is essential for **stoichiometric combustion calculations** and emission estimates.

**Role of HGI:**
- The **Hardgrove Grindability Index** measures the **ease of pulverizing coal** — higher HGI = softer coal = lower energy required in the mill.
- HGI is critical for **mill selection and capacity sizing** in PF-fired utility boilers. Typical design bands: **HGI > 50 → bowl mills**, **HGI < 50 → ball mills**.

**[b] [7 Marks] Describe the storage, handling and preparation of natural gas used as fuel in a process plant.**

**Model Answer:**

**Storage:** Natural gas is stored as:
1. **Pipeline gas** — most common; continuous supply from grid.
2. **Underground storage** — depleted gas reservoirs, salt caverns.
3. **LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas)** — cryogenic storage at **−162 °C** for peak-demand or import terminals.
4. **Gas holders** — low-pressure dry-seal holders for refinery off-gas.

**Handling:**
- **Pressure regulation** — city gate station reduces pipeline pressure (typically 35–70 bar) to distribution pressure (4–7 bar).
- **Filtration & Heating** — gas heaters raise temperature above the **hydrocarbon dew point** to prevent condensate formation.
- **Metering** — orifice / ultrasonic / Coriolis meters quantify flow and Wobbe Index.

**Preparation:**
- **Scrubbing** — removal of H₂S (if any) and entrained liquids.
- **Odorization** — injection of mercaptan for leak detection (city gas).
- **Pressure trimming** — final pressure reduction at burner skid.

**Combustion:** Gas enters the **burner nozzle** where it mixes with preheated combustion air and burns with a **short, luminous, stable flame**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfalls where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert** Dulong's formula result to **as-received** basis before applying in boiler efficiency.
> 2. Using **GCV instead of NCV** in efficiency computation → inflates efficiency by 4–6 %.
> 3. Confusing **°API with specific gravity** — °API is the **inverse** of SG.
> 4. **Mixing dry and as-fired** percentages of coal composition in stoichiometric air calculation.
> 5. In Wobbe Index calculation, using **molar volume basis inconsistently** — always stick to **Nm³ at STP**.
> 6. Writing **"lower calorific value = GCV − 585 × 9H"** without specifying that **9 kg water per kg fuel** is produced for each 1 kg of H burned (×9 = 9 moles H₂O per kg H = 9 kg).

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Fuel**: combustible substance liberating usable heat on combustion.
- **Classification**: Solid (coal) / Liquid (FO, LSHS) / Gaseous (NG, LPG).
- **API Gravity**: °API = 141.5/SG − 131.5.
- **Flash Point** governs fuel-oil storage safety code.
- **Pour Point** decides heating & tracing requirements.
- **Proximate Analysis**: M + VM + FC + Ash = 100 %.
- **Ultimate Analysis**: C, H, O, N, S + Ash + Moisture.
- **Dulong's Formula**: GCV = 8080 C + 34 500 (H − O/8) + 2240 S kcal/kg.
- **NCV** = GCV − latent heat of water vapor (used in boiler efficiency).
- **Wobbe Index** $I_W$ = GCV/√SG — gas interchangeability parameter.
- **HGI** → governs coal pulverizer selection.
- **Fuel Oil Storage**: Bulk tank → Day tank → Pre-heater → Atomizer → Burner.
- **Coal Preparation**: Tippler → Crusher → Bunker → Pulverizer → PF bin → Burner.
- **Gas Conditioning**: Pipeline → Filter → PRS → Heater → KOD → Burner.
- **Stoichiometric Air Rule**: For CH₄, 9.52 Nm³ air / Nm³ CH₄; 20 % excess air for complete combustion.
- **Property Trade-off**: Cleaner fuel → higher cost but lower handling/preparation capital.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of combustion: combustion of oil, coal and gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT636 - PROCESS UTILITY AND PIPING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principles of combustion: combustion of oil, coal and gas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Combustion? (KTU Board Definition)

**Combustion** is a rapid, exothermic chemical reaction between a **fuel** (hydrocarbon-based — solid, liquid, or gas) and an **oxidant** (typically atmospheric oxygen, O₂), accompanied by the release of **heat**, **light**, and **flame**. From a process utility standpoint, combustion is the primary means of converting chemical energy stored in fossil fuels into thermal energy used to generate steam, drive process heaters, fire reformers, and power furnaces across chemical process industries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A combustion engineer must master *three* fuel classes — **oil**, **coal**, and **gas** — because each requires fundamentally different burner design, stoichiometric air calculations, and combustion control philosophy. The 2024 Scheme places heavy emphasis on this triad under Module 2.

The generalized stoichiometric equation for a hydrocarbon fuel $C_x H_y S_z O_w$ burning in oxygen is:

$$C_x H_y S_z O_w + \left(x + \frac{y}{4} + z\right) O_2 \longrightarrow x\, CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\, H_2O + z\, SO_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sulfur** in fuels produces $SO_2$ (sulfur dioxide) and minor $SO_3$, both of which are flue-gas pollutants and contributors to acid rain. Modern combustion systems must account for $SO_x$ formation in their mass balances.

### 1.2 The Combustion Triangle (Intuitive Foundation)

Any combustion process requires **three** simultaneous conditions — the famous **Fire Triangle**:

```
        FUEL
         /\
        /  \
       /    \
      / FLAME \  
     /________\
   OXYGEN   HEAT (Activation Energy)
```

1. **Fuel** — the reducing agent (hydrocarbon supply).
2. **Oxygen** — the oxidizing agent (≥ 21 vol % in air).
3. **Heat / Activation Energy** — the ignition temperature must be reached to sustain the chain reaction.

> [!NOTE]
> A fourth element — **uninhibited chain propagation** — is sometimes added in modern combustion theory. If you remove *any one* of these three, the flame extinguishes. This is the basis for all flame-safety interlocks in fired heaters (e.g., BMS / Burner Management System).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bonfire vs. The Furnace

Imagine lighting a backyard **wood bonfire** versus an industrial **fired heater**:

- In the bonfire, you toss logs haphazardly — excess air leaks in uncontrolled, smoke billows, and much of the carbon escapes as soot and CO (inefficient, smoky combustion).
- In the **process furnace**, the engineer precisely meters **fuel** and **air** through engineered burners, maintains a target **excess air** (typically 10–25 %), and instruments the flue gas to measure $O_2$, $CO$, and $CO_2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industrial Intuition:** Combustion engineering is essentially the discipline of *controlling the chemistry of the bonfire*. By managing fuel-air mixing, residence time, and temperature, the chemical engineer converts uncontrolled fire into a precise thermal-energy release system with predictable heat flux.

### 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics in Combustion Engineering

- **Gross Calorific Value (GCV) / Higher Heating Value (HHV)** — Heat released assuming all water vapour in products condenses to liquid (latent heat recovered). Units: **kJ/kg** or **kJ/Nm³**.
- **Net Calorific Value (NCV) / Lower Heating Value (LHV)** — Heat released assuming water leaves as vapour. **NCV = GCV − 9 × H₂ × 2442 kJ/kg of H₂**.
- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F)ₛₜ** — Minimum air required for complete combustion per kg (or Nm³) of fuel.
- **Excess Air (%)** — Extra air supplied beyond stoichiometric requirement.
- **Flue Gas** — The combustion product mixture leaving the stack.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stoichiometry of Common Fuels

#### A. Combustion of Gaseous Fuel (Methane — CH₄)

$$CH_4 + 2\, O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2\, H_2O$$

Per kmol CH₄ → 2 kmol O₂ → 2 × 4.76 = **9.52 kmol air**.

#### B. Combustion of Liquid Fuel (n-Heptane C₇H₁₆ — kerosene proxy)

$$C_7H_{16} + 11\, O_2 \longrightarrow 7\, CO_2 + 8\, H_2O$$

#### C. Combustion of Coal (Carbon proxy — C)

$$C + O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2$$

Sulfur in coal:

$$S + O_2 \longrightarrow SO_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-coal combustion** is never just carbon. A typical bituminous coal proximate analysis: Fixed Carbon = 50–70 %, Volatile Matter = 20–35 %, Moisture = 5–15 %, Ash = 5–15 %. Each component combusts at a different rate and temperature.

### 2.2 The Three Combustion Regimes — How & Why They Differ

#### 2.2.1 Combustion of Oil (Atomization-Dependent)

**How it works:**
1. Pre-heated fuel oil (**150–200 °C** for heavy fuel oil to reduce viscosity below **15–25 cSt**) is delivered under pressure.
2. **Atomization** breaks the oil into **10–100 µm droplets**, dramatically increasing surface-area-to-volume ratio.
3. Droplets vaporize, mix with air, and burn in a diffusion flame.

**Why atomization matters:**
- A 1 mm droplet has surface/volume ratio = 6 mm⁻¹.
- A 50 µm droplet has surface/volume ratio = **120 mm⁻¹** — a 20× enhancement.
- Faster vaporization → faster mixing → more complete combustion → less soot.

**Burner Types for Oil:**
- **Pressure-jet (Pressure-atomizing) burner** — fuel at 7–30 bar.
- **Steam-assisted (Y-jet) burner** — uses steam at 3–10 bar for atomization.
- **Air-assisted burner** — uses compressed air at 1–4 bar.
- **Rotary-cup burner** — uses spinning cup to centrifugally atomize oil.

#### 2.2.2 Combustion of Coal (Stage-Dependent)

Coal combustion proceeds through **four overlapping stages**:

| Stage | Phenomenon | Temperature Range | Duration |
|-------|-----------|-------------------|----------|
| 1. Drying | Moisture evaporation | 100–150 °C | Seconds |
| 2. Devolatilization / Pyrolysis | Volatile matter (VM) released as tar + gases | 300–700 °C | 1–10 s |
| 3. Char combustion | Fixed carbon + O₂ → CO₂/CO | 700–1500 °C | Minutes |
| 4. Ash formation | Mineral matter melts/sinters | > 1200 °C | Continuous |

**Combustion Technologies:**
- **Fixed-bed (Stoker firing)** — coal on a moving grate; limited to < 50 MW; in decline.
- **Fluidized-bed combustion (FBC)** — coal particles suspended in air-fluidized bed of inert sand at 800–900 °C; excellent for high-ash / high-sulfur coals.
- **Pulverized coal (PC) firing** — coal ground to < 75 µm; burned in suspension; dominant utility-scale technology.
- **Cyclone furnace** — crushed coal + primary air tangentially fired; high heat release.

#### 2.2.3 Combustion of Gas (Mixing-Dependent)

Gas combustion is governed by **premixed vs. diffusion flame** architecture:

- **Premixed flame** — fuel and air mixed before burner; flame speed controlled; risk of flashback. Example: Bunsen burner.
- **Diffusion flame** — fuel and air mixed *at* and *after* burner port; longer, luminous yellow flame; soot-prone with heavy hydrocarbons.
- **Partially premixed** — pilot for stability + main flame (industrial standard).

**Burner Types for Gas:**
- **Nozzle-mix (raw gas) burner** — gas + air meet at burner tip; short, intense flame.
- **Pre-mix burner** — gas + air mixed in venturi before nozzle; lower flame temperature.
- **Atmospheric burner** — natural draft air via venturi; domestic use.
- **Power burner (forced-draft)** — fan-driven air; high capacity.

### 2.3 Air-Fuel Ratio & Excess Air

**Theoretical (stoichiometric) air required** for a fuel $C_x H_y S_z O_w$:

$$\boxed{O_{2,\,st} = x + \frac{y}{4} + z \quad \text{(kmol O}_2\text{/kmol fuel)}}$$

$$\boxed{Air_{st} = 4.76 \times O_{2,\,st} \quad \text{(kmol air/kmol fuel)}}$$

**Actual air supplied:**

$$Air_{act} = Air_{st} \times (1 + \text{excess air fraction})$$

**% Excess Air (EA):**

$$\boxed{\% EA = \frac{Air_{act} - Air_{st}}{Air_{st}} \times 100}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical KTU Rule-of-Thumb:** Industrial practice uses **10–25 % excess air** for gaseous fuels, **15–30 %** for oil, and **20–35 %** for coal. Below ~5 %, CO rises sharply; above ~30 %, stack losses rise without proportional efficiency gain.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Stoichiometric O₂ | $O_{2,st} = x + \dfrac{y}{4} + z$ | kmol O₂/kmol fuel | For $C_xH_yS_z$ fuel |
| 2 | Stoichiometric Air | $A_{st} = 4.76\, O_{2,st}$ | kmol air/kmol fuel | 4.76 = 1/0.21 |
| 3 | % Excess Air | $\%EA = \dfrac{A_{act} - A_{st}}{A_{st}} \times 100$ | % | Always positive in practice |
| 4 | Air-Fuel Ratio (mass) | $A/F = \dfrac{A_{act}\, M_{air}}{M_{fuel}}$ | kg air/kg fuel | For mass-based systems |
| 5 | NCV from GCV | $NCV = GCV - 9\,H_2 \cdot h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | $h_{fg}$ = 2442 kJ/kg at 25 °C |
| 6 | % Theoretical Air | $\%TA = 100 + \%EA$ | % | Used in combustion control |
| 7 | Orsat $CO_2\%$ max | $CO_{2,max} = \dfrac{CO_2\, vol}{dry\, products} \times 100$ | % | Orsat absorbs $SO_2$ with water + CO₂ |
| 8 | Heat loss in dry flue gas | $Q_{dry} = m_{fg}\, c_{p,fg}\, (T_{fg} - T_{ref})$ | kW | Major stack loss |
| 9 | Heat loss due to H₂O | $Q_{H_2O} = m_{H_2O}\,(h_{fg} + c_{p,v}(T_{fg}-T_{ref}))$ | kW | Latent loss — basis difference GCV/NCV |
| 10 | Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_c = 100 - \%L_{dry} - \%L_{H_2O} - \%L_{CO+unb}$ | % | KTU board favorite |
| 11 | Dew point of flue gas | $P_{H_2O} = y_{H_2O}\, P_{total}$ | bar | Corrosion control |
| 12 | Combustion equation: oil | $C_xH_y + (x+y/4)\, O_2 \rightarrow xCO_2 + (y/2)\, H_2O$ | — | Per kmol fuel |

> [!NOTE]
> All above formulas are **KTU-high-yield** — at least one appears in almost every KTU Process Utility exam paper. Memorize the **boxed** entries first.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Combustion mastery is the cornerstone of **process plant utility systems**. Applications include:

- **Fired heaters / process furnaces** in oil refineries (crude distillation, visbreakers).
- **Steam boilers** — the heart of every chemical plant.
- **Gas turbines & internal combustion engines** — power generation.
- **Incinerators** — hazardous waste treatment.
- **Reformer furnaces** — hydrogen and ammonia plants.
- **Thermal oxidizers** — VOC abatement in chemical industry.

Each of these depends on correct **stoichiometry, excess-air control, and heat-release management** to deliver predictable energy while meeting emission norms ($NO_x$, $SO_x$, CO).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air for a Generic Hydrocarbon

**Given:** A gaseous fuel of composition **CH₄ = 80 %, C₂H₆ = 15 %, N₂ = 5 %** by volume. Air composition is 21 % O₂ + 79 % N₂ (mole basis).

**Find:** Stoichiometric air required per Nm³ of fuel and per kg of fuel.

#### Step 1 — Write balanced equations for each combustible component

For methane:

$$CH_4 + 2\, O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2\, H_2O$$

For ethane:

$$C_2H_6 + \tfrac{7}{2}\, O_2 \rightarrow 2\, CO_2 + 3\, H_2O$$

Nitrogen is inert — it does not consume oxygen.

#### Step 2 — Compute O₂ demand per component per Nm³ of fuel

| Component | Volume Fraction | O₂ Required (Nm³/Nm³ component) | O₂ per Nm³ fuel |
|-----------|----------------|----------------------------------|-----------------|
| CH₄ | 0.80 | 2.00 | $0.80 \times 2.00 = 1.600$ |
| C₂H₆ | 0.15 | 3.50 | $0.15 \times 3.50 = 0.525$ |
| N₂ | 0.05 | 0.00 | $0.05 \times 0.00 = 0.000$ |

**Total O₂ (stoichiometric)** = $1.600 + 0.525 + 0.000 = 2.125$ Nm³ O₂/Nm³ fuel.

#### Step 3 — Convert O₂ to theoretical air

$$\text{Air}_{st} = \frac{O_{2,\,st}}{0.21} = \frac{2.125}{0.21} = 10.119\ \text{Nm}^3\,\text{air/Nm}^3\,\text{fuel}$$

#### Step 4 — Per-kg-fuel calculation (mass basis)

Average molecular weight of fuel:

$$M_{fuel} = (0.80 \times 16) + (0.15 \times 30) + (0.05 \times 28)$$

$$\boxed{M_{fuel} = 12.8 + 4.5 + 1.4 = 18.7\ \text{kg/kmol}}$$

Per kg fuel basis, the volume occupied (at STP) is:

$$V_{fuel,kg} = \frac{22.4}{18.7} = 1.198\ \text{Nm}^3/\text{kg}$$

Hence per kg fuel:

$$\text{Air}_{st,\,kg} = \frac{10.119}{1.198} = 8.45\ \text{Nm}^3\,\text{air/kg fuel}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Air}_{st,\,kg} = 8.45\ \text{Nm}^3\,\text{air/kg fuel} \;\;(\approx 10.4\ \text{kg air/kg fuel})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **3-Derivation Mastery**: a KTU exam favorite. Many students drop the mass-basis conversion and lose 4 marks. Always state your basis explicitly.

### 3.2 Full Numerical Worked Example — Coal Combustion + Orsat Analysis

**Problem (KTU Standard):** A coal has the following ultimate analysis by mass:
**C = 72 %, H₂ = 5 %, S = 1 %, O₂ = 8 %, N₂ = 1.5 %, Moisture = 7 %, Ash = 5.5 %**.

The coal is burned with **30 % excess air**. Calculate:
(a) Theoretical air required per kg coal.
(b) Actual air supplied per kg coal.
(c) Wet and dry flue gas analysis (Orsat basis).
(d) % CO₂ (dry basis) in flue gas.
(e) Combustion efficiency if NCV = 25,000 kJ/kg and stack temperature = 220 °C with ambient = 30 °C.

#### (a) Theoretical Air

**Per kg coal, oxygen demand by each combustible (kg of O₂ per kg of element):**

| Element | Mass in 1 kg coal | O₂ demanded (kg/kg element) | O₂ demanded (kg) |
|---------|------------------|-----------------------------|------------------|
| C | 0.72 | $\dfrac{32}{12} = 2.667$ | $0.72 \times 2.667 = 1.920$ |
| H₂ | 0.05 | $\dfrac{32}{4} = 8.000$ | $0.05 \times 8.000 = 0.400$ |
| S | 0.01 | $\dfrac{32}{32} = 1.000$ | $0.01 \times 1.000 = 0.010$ |
| O₂ in fuel | 0.08 | −1.000 (subtract) | $-0.080$ |
| **Total O₂ needed** | | | **2.250 kg O₂** |

Convert kg O₂ to Nm³:

$$O_{2,\,st,\,Nm^3} = \frac{2.250}{32/22.4} = \frac{2.250}{1.429} = 1.575\ \text{Nm}^3\,\text{O}_2/\text{kg coal}$$

Theoretical air:

$$\boxed{A_{st} = \frac{1.575}{0.21} = 7.50\ \text{Nm}^3\,\text{air/kg coal}}$$

#### (b) Actual Air with 30 % Excess

$$A_{act} = A_{st} \times (1 + 0.30) = 7.50 \times 1.30 = 9.75\ \text{Nm}^3\,\text{air/kg coal}$$

#### (c) Flue Gas Composition (per kg coal)

**Step C1 — Products of combustion (theoretical component-by-component):**

From **C**: $0.72 \times \dfrac{22.4}{12} = 1.344$ Nm³ CO₂.
From **H₂**: $0.05 \times \dfrac{22.4}{2} = 0.560$ Nm³ H₂O (vapour).
From **S**: $0.01 \times \dfrac{22.4}{32} = 0.007$ Nm³ SO₂.

**Step C2 — Air-supplied components:**

N₂ from theoretical air = $7.50 \times 0.79 = 5.925$ Nm³ N₂.
N₂ from 30 % excess air = $0.30 \times 7.50 \times 0.79 = 1.778$ Nm³ N₂.

O₂ in excess air (unreacted) = $0.30 \times 7.50 \times 0.21 = 0.473$ Nm³ O₂.

**Step C3 — Total flue gas (wet basis):**

| Component | Nm³/kg coal | % Wet |
|-----------|-------------|-------|
| CO₂ | 1.344 | 13.32 |
| SO₂ | 0.007 | 0.07 |
| H₂O (vapour) | 0.560 | 5.55 |
| N₂ | $5.925 + 1.778 = 7.703$ | 76.34 |
| O₂ (excess) | 0.473 | 4.69 |
| **Total wet** | **10.087** | **100.00** |

**Step C4 — Dry basis (subtract H₂O):**

Dry flue gas = $10.087 - 0.560 = 9.527$ Nm³/kg coal.

| Component | Nm³/kg coal (dry) | % Dry (Orsat) |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------|
| CO₂ | 1.344 | 14.11 |
| SO₂ | 0.007 | 0.07 |
| N₂ | 7.703 | 80.86 |
| O₂ | 0.473 | 4.96 |
| **Total dry** | **9.527** | **100.00** |

#### (d) % CO₂ (dry)

$$\boxed{\%CO_2 = \frac{1.344}{9.527} \times 100 = 14.11\ \%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In **Orsat apparatus**, $SO_2$ is absorbed along with $CO_2$ by KOH solution, so practical Orsat reading reports $CO_2 + SO_2 \approx 14.18\%$. This is a frequently-tested nuance.

#### (e) Combustion Efficiency

Mass of dry flue gas per kg coal:

$$m_{dry} = \frac{9.527 \times 28.97}{22.4} = 12.32\ \text{kg/kg coal (approx)}$$

Using $c_{p,fg} \approx 1.05$ kJ/kg·K and $\Delta T = 220 - 30 = 190$ K:

$$Q_{dry} = 12.32 \times 1.05 \times 190 = 2457\ \text{kJ/kg coal}$$

Mass of water vapour from fuel + combustion H₂O:

$$m_{H_2O} = \frac{0.560 \times 18}{22.4} = 0.450\ \text{kg/kg coal}$$

$$Q_{H_2O} = 0.450 \times (2442 + 1.88 \times 190) = 0.450 \times 2799 = 1260\ \text{kJ/kg coal}$$

Heat input (NCV basis):

$$Q_{in} = 25{,}000\ \text{kJ/kg coal}$$

$$\%L_{dry} = \frac{2457}{25000} \times 100 = 9.83\ \%$$

$$\%L_{H_2O} = \frac{1260}{25000} \times 100 = 5.04\ \%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_c = 100 - 9.83 - 5.04 = 85.13\ \%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** If the question gives **GCV** instead of NCV, then latent heat of water formation is already included → **do NOT add 2442 kJ/kg × H₂O mass** in stack loss; only sensible heating of water vapour. Read the question word-by-word.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Fuel Class

| Criterion | Coal | Oil | Gas |
|-----------|------|-----|-----|
| Calorific value (kJ/kg) | 20,000–30,000 | 40,000–45,000 | 35,000–55,000 (per Nm³) |
| Ease of storage | Bulk piles | Tanks | Cylinders / pipelines |
| Pre-combustion prep | Pulverization / drying | Heating + atomization | Minimal |
| Burner capital cost | High | Medium | Low |
| SOₓ emissions | High | Medium | Negligible |
| NOₓ control | Difficult | Moderate | Easier (low-NOx burners) |
| Start-up time | Hours | 30 min–2 h | Seconds–minutes |
| Soot formation | Moderate–high | Moderate | Negligible (clean) |
| KTU exam weightage | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Combustion System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Storage and Handling] --> B[Fuel Preparation]
    B --> C[Burner Assembly]
    D[Air Intake and Filtration] --> E[Forced Draft Fan]
    E --> F[Air Preheater]
    F --> C
    C --> G[Combustion Chamber]
    G --> H[Heat Transfer Surfaces]
    H --> I[Flue Gas Path]
    I --> J[Economizer]
    J --> K[Air Preheater Secondary]
    K --> L[Baghouse or ESP]
    L --> M[Stack]
    M --> N[Atmosphere]
    H --> O[Steam or Process Output]
    G -.-> P[Burner Management System BMS]
    P -.-> C
```

**Reading Guide:**
- **Solid arrows** = material/energy flow.
- **Dashed arrows** = control signal (BMS trip & modulation).
- The **air preheater (F)** recovers waste heat from flue gas (I) to boost thermal efficiency.

### 4.2 Coal Combustion Subsystem Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Coal Stockpile] --> B[Crusher]
    B --> C[Pulverizer Mill 75 micron]
    C --> D[Classifier Oversize Recycle]
    D --> C
    C --> E[Primary Air Drying Carrier]
    E --> F[Coal Burner]
    G[Forced Draft Fan] --> H[Secondary Air Preheater]
    H --> F
    F --> I[Combustion Chamber 1500 C]
    I --> J[Furnace Bottom Ash Hopper]
    I --> K[Radiant and Convective Sections]
    K --> L[Fly Ash Electrostatic Precipitator]
    L --> M[ID Fan Induced Draft]
    M --> N[Stack]
```

### 4.3 Oil Burner Functional Block (Pressure-Jet Type)

flowchart LR
    A[Heavy Fuel Oil Storage 80 C] --> B[Oil Preheater 150 to 200 C]
    B --> C[Oil Strainers]
    C --> D[Oil Pump 7 to 30 bar]
    D --> E[Burner Tip Atomizer]
    F[Combustion Air Fan] --> G[Air Register]
    G --> H[Refractory Throat]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Flame Envelope Diffustion Flame]
    I --> J[Furnace Radiant Zone]
```

flowchart LR
    A[Gas Pressure Regulator] --> B[Safety Shutoff Valve SSOV]

    F --> G[Burner Nozzle Tip]

    G --> H[Premixed Flame Cone]
    H --> I[Furnace]
    B -.-> J[BMS Flame Scanner UV or IR]
    J -.-> B
```

### 4.5 Combustion Chemistry Comparison — Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Type] --> B[Solid Coal]

    A --> D[Gaseous Fuel]
    B --> B1[Stage 1 Drying 100 C]
    B1 --> B2[Stage 2 Devolatilization 300 to 700 C]
    B2 --> B3[Stage 3 Char Combustion 700 to 1500 C]
    B3 --> B4[Stage 4 Ash Sintering]
    C --> C1[Preheating 150 to 200 C]
    C1 --> C2[Atomization 10 to 100 micron]
    C2 --> C3[Vaporization]
    C3 --> C4[Diffusion Flame]
    D --> D1[Premixed or Diffusion]
    D1 --> D2[Short Intense Flame]
    B4 --> E[Flue Gas CO2 H2O SO2 N2 O2]
    C4 --> E
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Stack to Atmosphere]
```
