## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1. Define the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio. Why is excess air always supplied in practice?**

**Model Answer:**
The stoichiometric air-fuel ratio is the *minimum* mass (or volume) of air required for **complete combustion** of a unit mass (or volume) of fuel, such that all carbon becomes CO₂, all hydrogen becomes H₂O, and all sulfur becomes SO₂ — with no leftover fuel and no free oxygen. It is computed from the fuel's ultimate analysis and the balanced combustion equations.

In practice, **excess air (typically 10–35 %)** is always supplied because: (i) imperfect fuel-air mixing in real burners requires extra oxygen to approach complete combustion, (ii) it suppresses CO and soot formation by ensuring oxidizer-rich local zones, and (iii) it limits peak flame temperatures (especially for NOₓ control in gas firing). The trade-off is increased flue-gas mass flow and higher stack losses.

---

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between premixed and diffusion flames with one industrial example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Premixed Flame | Diffusion Flame |
|--------|---------------|-----------------|
| Mixing point | Fuel + air mixed **before** burner | Fuel + air mixed **at/after** burner port |
| Flame character | Short, sharp, blue | Long, luminous, yellow |
| Combustion stability | Risk of flashback | Stable, no flashback |
| Soot tendency | Low (clean) | Higher for heavy fuels |
| Industrial example | **Bunsen burner**, gas stove | **Oil gun burner**, candle |

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently lose 4–5 marks by **forgetting to convert dry-basis Orsat percentages to wet basis** (or vice-versa) when computing dew points or humidity ratios. Always read carefully whether "wet" or "dry" is specified. Also, **NCV vs GCV** confusion costs another 3 marks. Show the latent heat correction explicitly.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**A coal has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 70 %, H₂ = 5 %, N₂ = 1.5 %, S = 2 %, O₂ = 6 %, Moisture = 8 %, Ash = 7.5 %. The coal is burned with 25 % excess air.**

**(a)** Calculate the stoichiometric air required per kg of coal.
**(b)** Determine the actual flue-gas composition (both wet and dry) in volumetric percentage.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Stoichiometric air per kg coal:**

Per kg coal, mass of O₂ demanded by each combustible:

$$O_{2,\,C} = 0.70 \times \frac{32}{12} = 1.867\ \text{kg}$$

$$O_{2,\,H_2} = 0.05 \times \frac{32}{4} = 0.400\ \text{kg}$$

$$O_{2,\,S} = 0.02 \times \frac{32}{32} = 0.020\ \text{kg}$$

$$O_{2,\,fuel} = -0.060\ \text{kg (subtract)}$$

$$O_{2,\,net,\,st} = 1.867 + 0.400 + 0.020 - 0.060 = 2.227\ \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg coal}$$

In volumetric terms:

$$V_{O_2,\,st} = \frac{2.227}{32/22.4} = \frac{2.227}{1.429} = 1.559\ \text{Nm}^3\,\text{O}_2/\text{kg coal}$$

$$\boxed{A_{st} = \frac{1.559}{0.21} = 7.42\ \text{Nm}^3\,\text{air/kg coal}}$$

**(b) Wet and dry flue gas analysis:**

Volume of products per kg coal:

$$V_{CO_2} = 0.70 \times \frac{22.4}{12} = 1.307\ \text{Nm}^3$$

$$V_{H_2O} = 0.05 \times \frac{22.4}{2} = 0.560\ \text{Nm}^3$$

$$V_{SO_2} = 0.02 \times \frac{22.4}{32} = 0.014\ \text{Nm}^3$$

N₂ from theoretical air: $7.42 \times 0.79 = 5.862$ Nm³.
N₂ from 25 % excess air: $7.42 \times 0.25 \times 0.79 = 1.466$ Nm³.
Total N₂ = 7.328 Nm³.

Excess O₂ = $7.42 \times 0.25 \times 0.21 = 0.390$ Nm³.

**Wet basis totals:** $1.307 + 0.560 + 0.014 + 7.328 + 0.390 = 9.599$ Nm³.

| Component | Nm³ | % Wet |
|-----------|-----|-------|
| CO₂ | 1.307 | 13.62 |
| H₂O | 0.560 | 5.83 |
| SO₂ | 0.014 | 0.15 |
| N₂ | 7.328 | 76.34 |
| O₂ | 0.390 | 4.06 |
| **Total** | **9.599** | **100.00** |

**Dry basis:** Total dry = $9.599 - 0.560 = 9.039$ Nm³.

| Component | Nm³ (dry) | % Dry |
|-----------|-----------|-------|
| CO₂ | 1.307 | 14.46 |
| SO₂ | 0.014 | 0.15 |
| N₂ | 7.328 | 81.07 |
| O₂ | 0.390 | 4.32 |
| **Total** | **9.039** | **100.00** |

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**A furnace burns furnace oil (assumed C₁₂H₂₆) with 20 % excess air. The oil has NCV = 42,000 kJ/kg. Stack gas leaves at 280 °C and ambient is 30 °C.**

**(a)** Calculate the stoichiometric air required per kg of oil.
**(b)** If the flue-gas analysis (dry basis) shows 12 % CO₂, 4 % O₂, 84 % N₂, determine the actual % excess air used and check consistency with the given 20 %.
**(c)** Estimate the dry flue-gas heat loss as a percentage of NCV if mean $c_p$ of dry gas = 1.05 kJ/kg·K.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Stoichiometric air:**

$$C_{12}H_{26} + \tfrac{71}{2}\, O_2 \rightarrow 12\, CO_2 + 13\, H_2O$$

Per kg fuel (M = 170 kg/kmol):

$$O_{2,\,st} = \frac{35.5}{170} \times 22.4 = 4.676\ \text{Nm}^3\,\text{O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

$$\boxed{A_{st} = \frac{4.676}{0.21} = 22.27\ \text{Nm}^3\,\text{air/kg fuel}}$$

$$\boxed{A_{st,\,mass} = 22.27 \times 1.293 = 28.79\ \text{kg air/kg fuel}}$$

**(b) Actual excess air from Orsat:**

For a hydrocarbon burning in excess air, the relation between dry flue-gas O₂ and excess air is:

$$\% EA = \frac{O_{2,\,dry}}{0.21\, N_{2,\,dry} - O_{2,\,dry}} \times 100$$

Substituting 4 % O₂ and 84 % N₂:

$$\% EA = \frac{4}{0.21 \times 84 - 4} \times 100 = \frac{4}{17.64 - 4} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% EA = \frac{4}{13.64} \times 100 = 29.3\ \%}$$

The actual flue gas indicates **~29 % excess air**, *not* 20 % — meaning the operator is admitting more air than the design intent (perhaps due to faulty damper setting). This is a **diagnostic insight** of Orsat analysis — a KTU examiner loves this real-world application.

**(c) Dry flue gas heat loss:**

Per Nm³ of dry flue gas, mass = $\frac{29}{22.4} = 1.295$ kg (using mean M ≈ 29 kg/kmol).

Per kg fuel, dry flue-gas volume = $\frac{22.27 \times (1+0.293) \times 0.79}{1} \approx 22.74$ Nm³ (corrected: $A_{act} = 22.27 \times 1.293 = 28.79$ Nm³; N₂ = $28.79 \times 0.79 = 22.74$ Nm³; total dry ≈ 27.4 Nm³).

Take total dry flue gas = ~27 Nm³/kg fuel:

$$m_{dry} = 27 \times 1.295 = 34.97\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$$

$$Q_{dry} = 34.97 \times 1.05 \times (280 - 30) = 34.97 \times 1.05 \times 250 = 9179\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$\%L_{dry} = \frac{9179}{42000} \times 100 = \boxed{21.85\ \%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall on Part (b):** Students often confuse the Orsat $N_2$ as **only fuel-derived nitrogen**. In air-fired systems, *all* the nitrogen in the flue gas comes from air. The relation $\%EA = \dfrac{O_{2,dry}}{0.21\,N_{2,dry} - O_{2,dry}}$ uses **flue-gas N₂**, not fuel N₂.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- 🔥 **Combustion Triangle**: Fuel + O₂ + Activation Energy → all three required simultaneously.
- ⚖️ **Stoichiometric Air (theoretical air)** = O₂ required × (1/0.21) = O₂ × **4.76**.
- 📈 **Excess air practice ranges**: Gas ~10–20 %, Oil ~15–30 %, Coal ~20–35 %.
- 💧 **NCV vs GCV**: GCV includes latent heat of water condensation; NCV does not. **NCV = GCV − 9 H₂ × 2442 kJ/kg**.
- 🛢️ **Oil combustion hinges on atomization**: Pressure / steam / air-assisted / rotary-cup burners. Droplet size 10–100 µm is the goal.
- ⛏️ **Coal combustion is staged**: Drying → Devolatilization → Char combustion → Ash. **Pulverized coal (PC)** is the dominant utility technology.
- 💨 **Gas combustion**: Premixed flame (short, blue, flashback risk) vs Diffusion flame (long, yellow, stable).
- 🧪 **Orsat analysis** gives **dry-basis** composition of CO₂ + SO₂, O₂, CO, N₂.
- 📐 **%EA from Orsat**: $\%EA = \dfrac{O_{2,dry}}{0.21\,N_{2,dry} - O_{2,dry}} \times 100$.
- 🌡️ **Combustion efficiency** = 100 % − (dry stack loss) − (H₂O stack loss) − (CO + unburned loss).
- 🚨 **Stack losses dominate** — most loss is sensible heat of dry flue gas.
- 🌍 **Sulfur in fuel** → SO₂ → absorbed with CO₂ in Orsat KOH bottle (causes reading error of +0.1–0.3 %).
- 🛡️ **BMS (Burner Management System)** uses UV/IR flame scanners, pressure switches, and permissives — every fired heater in a KTU-affiliated plant must follow **NFPA 85** logic.
- 🧮 **Always state your basis** (per kg fuel or per Nm³ fuel or per kg air) before writing any formula — examiners deduct heavily for ambiguity.
- 📊 **Memorize the KTU Formula Sheet** in Section 2.4 verbatim — these twelve equations cover ~85 % of all combustion problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In every combustion numerical, follow the **5-Step KTU Board Method**:
> 1. **Write balanced combustion equations** for each fuel component.
> 2. **Compute stoichiometric O₂** (per kg or per Nm³ fuel).
> 3. **Compute stoichiometric air** (divide by 0.21).
> 4. **Apply excess air** to get actual air and unreacted O₂.
> 5. **Tabulate flue gas** as wet-basis then dry-basis (Orsat).
>
> This sequence is what KTU examiners expect — deviate and you risk losing "method marks."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compressed air system: types of air compressors, compressor efficiency, compressed air system components, factors affecting the performance and efficiency of compressors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT636 - PROCESS UTILITY AND PIPING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Compressed air system: types of air compressors, compressor efficiency, compressed air system components, factors affecting the performance and efficiency of compressors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Compressed Air System?

A **Compressed Air System** is an integrated utility network that converts ambient atmospheric air into a higher-pressure, cleaner, and drier working fluid using mechanical work. In chemical process industries (CPI), it is classified as the **"fourth utility"** after water, steam, and electricity because nearly every plant — from instrumentation and pneumatic conveying to reactor agitation and valve actuation — depends on it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT636 – Module 2):**
> A compressed air system comprises the air intake filter, the compressor (prime mover + machine), the inter-stage/after coolers, the moisture separator, the air receiver, the dryer, the distribution piping network, and the end-use pressure regulators. The system delivers air typically at **0.7 MPa to 0.85 MPa (gauge)** for plant use, with a guaranteed dew point of **−40 °C** for instrument-quality air.

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Lung and Artery" of the Plant

Imagine a human body: the **compressor is the lung** that inhales raw air and pressurizes it; the **aftercooler and dryer are the cooling veins** that clean the blood; the **receiver tank is the heart** that stores and smooths the flow; and the **piping network is the arteries** that deliver purified fluid to every organ (actuator, valve, tool). If the lung is weak, the heart (receiver) cannot supply steady flow, and the arteries (pipes) carry impure, humid air — leading to valve sticking, instrument drift, and plant shutdowns.

## 1.2 Classification of Air Compressors — At a Glance

| Broad Class | Working Principle | Typical Capacity | Typical Discharge |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Positive Displacement (PD)** | Air trapped in a fixed volume, then mechanically reduced | Low to medium flow (< 500 m³/min) | High pressure (up to 1000 bar in reciprocating) |
| **Dynamic (Turbo)** | Air accelerated by impeller; kinetic energy → pressure | High flow (> 500 m³/min) | Moderate pressure (1–15 bar typical) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** For 80% of chemical process plants in India, **oil-flooded rotary screw compressors** dominate the low-pressure range (7–13 bar), while **centrifugal compressors** dominate large-scale continuous processes (e.g., ethylene crackers, ammonia plants) due to their oil-free discharge and high turndown capability.

### 1.2.1 Positive Displacement Compressors

- **Reciprocating (Piston) Compressor** — Uses a piston moving inside a cylinder. Available as single-stage, two-stage, or multi-stage. Best for high-pressure, low-flow duties.
- **Rotary Screw Compressor** — Two intermeshing helical rotors (male + female) trap, compress, and discharge air continuously. Smooth, vibration-free, and high reliability.
- **Rotary Vane Compressor** — An eccentric rotor carries sliding vanes; air trapped between consecutive vanes is compressed. Used in oil-injected medium-duty applications.
- **Scroll Compressor** — Two interleaving spiral scrolls; one is fixed, the other orbits. Very low noise, used in oil-free instrument air.

### 1.2.2 Dynamic (Turbo) Compressors

- **Centrifugal Compressor** — Air enters the eye of a high-speed impeller, gains kinetic energy, and is converted to pressure in the diffuser/volute. Multi-stage units can reach 50+ bar.
- **Axial Flow Compressor** — Air flows parallel to the axis through alternating rows of rotating and stationary blades. Used in very large flow rates (gas turbine intakes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Mnemonic (KTU Board Favorite):**
> **"Reciprocating for high pressure, Screw for medium, Centrifugal for huge flow."**

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value (Plant Air) |
|---|---|---|
| Discharge pressure | $P_d$ | **7.0 – 8.5 bar (g)** |
| Inlet air temperature | $T_1$ | **25 °C – 40 °C** (Indian climate) |
| Relative humidity | RH | 60 – 90% |
| Dew point (instrument air) | DPT | **−40 °C** (ISO 8573-1 Class 2) |
| Power supply | — | 415 V, 3-Phase, 50 Hz |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Work of Compression

The theoretical work required to compress a unit mass of gas from inlet state (P₁, V₁) to discharge state (P₂, V₂) depends on the **polytropic process equation**:

$$PV^n = \text{constant}$$

where **n = polytropic index**, which depends on heat exchange with the surroundings.

### 2.1.1 The Three Ideal Compression Processes

| Process | Index `n` | Heat Exchange | Work Equation (per kg) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Isothermal | $n = 1$ | Heat removed fully to keep T constant | $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ |
| Adiabatic (Isentropic) | $n = \gamma = C_p / C_v$ | No heat transfer (insulated) | $W_{adi} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$ |
| Polytropic | $1 < n < \gamma$ | Partial cooling | $W_{poly} = \dfrac{n}{n - 1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$ |

For air, the adiabatic index is **γ = 1.4** (since $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K).

### 2.1.2 Why Isothermal Work is the Minimum

In an ideal isothermal process, ALL the heat generated during compression is removed. Since $W_{iso} < W_{adi}$, real compressors try to mimic isothermal behavior by:
- **Water-jacketing** the cylinder (reciprocating)
- **Inter-stage cooling** between stages
- **Oil-injection** cooling (screw compressors)
- **External aftercoolers** after discharge

## 2.2 Compressor Efficiency — A Multi-Parameter Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Students often confuse "compressor efficiency" with "compressor effectiveness." In KTU papers, expect numericals on **isothermal, adiabatic (isentropic), mechanical, and overall (isentropic + mechanical) efficiencies** — each defined differently. Memorize the four equations below.

### 2.2.1 Isothermal Efficiency (η_iso)

Compares actual work to the theoretical isothermal (minimum possible) work:

$$\eta_{iso} = \dfrac{W_{iso,\,ideal}}{W_{actual}} \times 100\%$$

- **Application:** Reciprocating compressors with good intercooling.
- **Typical value:** 50 – 70% (single-stage), 60 – 80% (multi-stage).

### 2.2.2 Adiabatic (Isentropic) Efficiency (η_ad)

Used for dynamic (turbo) compressors where the process is nearly adiabatic:

$$\eta_{ad} = \dfrac{W_{adi,\,ideal}}{W_{actual}} \times 100\%$$

- **Application:** Centrifugal and axial compressors.
- **Typical value:** 70 – 85% for centrifugal, 85 – 92% for axial.

### 2.2.3 Mechanical Efficiency (η_m)

Accounts for friction losses in bearings, gears, and seals:

$$\eta_m = \dfrac{W_{indicated}}{W_{shaft}} \times 100\%$$

where $W_{shaft} = W_{indicated} + W_{friction}$.

- **Typical value:** 90 – 95%.

### 2.2.4 Volumetric Efficiency (η_v) — Reciprocating Only

Ratio of actual suction volume to swept volume (displacement):

$$\eta_v = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{swept}} = 1 + C - C \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$$

where **C = clearance ratio** = $V_c / V_s$ (typically 3 – 8% for industrial units).

> [!NOTE]
> The clearance volume is a "dead space" left in the cylinder to prevent the piston from striking the valve plate. A higher clearance ratio REDUCES volumetric efficiency but PROTECTS the compressor mechanically.

### 2.2.5 Overall (Brake) Efficiency (η_overall)

Combines isothermal/adiabatic with mechanical:

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{iso} \times \eta_m \quad \text{(for reciprocating)}$$

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{ad} \times \eta_m \quad \text{(for centrifugal)}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 12 equations — they cover ~80% of KTU numerical questions on this topic.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Polytropic relation | $PV^n = \text{const}$ | — | $n$ = polytropic index |
| 2 | Isothermal work | $W = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$ | kJ/kg | Minimum theoretical work |
| 3 | Adiabatic work | $W = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P_1 V_1 \left[(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$ | kJ/kg | $\gamma = 1.4$ for air |
| 4 | Polytropic work | $W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$ | kJ/kg | $1 < n < \gamma$ |
| 5 | Isothermal efficiency | $\eta_{iso} = W_{iso,ideal}/W_{actual}$ | — | Reciprocating |
| 6 | Adiabatic efficiency | $\eta_{ad} = W_{adi,ideal}/W_{actual}$ | — | Centrifugal |
| 7 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_m = W_{indicated}/W_{shaft}$ | — | Bearings + friction |
| 8 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_v = 1 + C - C(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$ | — | Reciprocating only |
| 9 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{iso} \times \eta_m$ | — | Reciprocating |
| 10 | Power required | $P_{shaft} = \dot{m} \cdot W_{actual} / \eta_m$ | kW | — |
| 11 | FAD (Free Air Delivery) | $FAD = V_{swept} \times N \times \eta_v$ | m³/min | At inlet conditions |
| 12 | Pressure ratio per stage | $r_s = (P_{final}/P_1)^{1/k}$ | — | $k$ = number of stages |

## 2.4 Compressed Air System Components — Functional Breakdown

A complete industrial compressed air system has the following components in flow order:

### 2.4.1 Air Intake Filter

- Removes atmospheric dust (typically 5–10 micron).
- Protects the compressor internals from abrasion.
- Pressure drop should not exceed **0.05 bar** (clean).

### 2.4.2 Compressor (Prime Mover + Machine)

- **Prime mover:** Electric motor (most common in CPI) or gas/diesel engine.
- **Machine:** Reciprocating / Screw / Centrifugal (selected per duty).
- Motor power: $P = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot W_{adi}}{\eta_{overall}}$ (kW)

### 2.4.3 Aftercooler (Inter-Cooler / After-Cooler)

- Reduces discharge air temperature from **~150 °C** to within **~10 °C** of ambient.
- Types: Water-cooled shell-and-tube, air-cooled finned.
- Condenses ~70% of the moisture carried as vapor.

### 2.4.4 Moisture Separator / Receiver (Air Receiver Tank)

- **Receiver:** Cylindrical pressure vessel (ASME Sec VIII), sized to give **2–5 minutes** of buffer time at full flow.
- **Sizing rule of thumb:** $V_{receiver} = 1.5 \times Q \times \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{max} - P_{min}}$ (m³) where Q is flow in m³/min.
- Provides pulsation dampening (reciprocating) and condensate drop-out.

### 2.4.5 Air Dryer

- **Refrigeration (chilled) dryer:** Dew point +3 °C; cheapest.
- **Desiccant (adsorption) dryer:** Dew point −40 °C; mandatory for instrument air.
- **Membrane dryer:** Compact, used in remote installations.

### 2.4.6 Particulate and Coalescing Filters

- **Particulate filter:** 1 micron → removes solid dust.
- **Coalescing filter:** 0.01 micron → removes oil aerosols + water droplets.
- **Activated carbon filter:** Removes oil vapor → down to 0.003 mg/m³.

### 2.4.7 Pressure Regulators and Distribution Network

- **Regulator:** Maintains downstream pressure within ±0.1 bar.
- **Piping:** Sized for **6 m/s** air velocity; drops of 0.1 bar/100 m typical.
- **Moisture traps:** Auto-drain valves at low points.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **petrochemical plant**, a single centrifugal compressor supplying **10,000 Nm³/hr** of instrument air at 7 bar (g) can power:
- ~5,000 pneumatic control valves
- ~200 pneumatic actuators
- 24/7 DCS pneumatic signal lines
- Pneumatic conveying of catalysts

A **1% reduction in compressor efficiency** at this scale translates to **₹15–20 lakhs/year** in extra electricity cost. Hence, KTU emphasizes the factors below.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Work of Polytropic Compression

We begin from the first law of thermodynamics for an open system (control volume) under steady-flow, with negligible kinetic and potential energy changes:

$$dW = -V \, dP \quad \text{(reversible flow work)}$$

The polytropic process satisfies $P V^n = C$ (constant). Differentiating:

$$V^n \, dP + n P V^{n-1} \, dV = 0$$

$$dV = -\dfrac{V}{nP} \, dP$$

Substituting back into the work expression for a unit mass:

$$W = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} V(-dP) = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \dfrac{V}{nP} P \, dP$$

Using $P_1 V_1^n = P V^n$ implies $V = V_1 (P_1/P)^{1/n}$, so:

$$W = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} V_1 \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P}\right)^{1/n} \dfrac{1}{n} \left(\dfrac{P}{P_1}\right) dP$$

$$W = \dfrac{V_1 P_1^{1/n}}{n} \int_{P_1}^{P_2} P^{(1/n) - 1} \, dP$$

Integrating with $m = (1/n) - 1$:

$$W = \dfrac{V_1 P_1^{1/n}}{n} \cdot \dfrac{1}{m+1} \left[P_2^{m+1} - P_1^{m+1}\right]$$

With $m+1 = 1/n$:

$$W = V_1 P_1 \cdot \dfrac{n}{n-1} \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting cases of the polytropic equation:**
> - $n = 1$ → $W = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$ (isothermal, using L'Hôpital's rule)
> - $n = \gamma$ → $W = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} P_1 V_1 \left[(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$ (adiabatic)

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Typical KTU Board Question

> **Question:** A two-stage reciprocating compressor compresses air from **1.013 bar and 25 °C** to a final pressure of **25 bar**. The isentropic index is **γ = 1.4** and the isentropic efficiency of each stage is **85%**. Calculate:
> (a) The intermediate (inter-cooler) pressure for minimum work.
> (b) The total work of compression per kg of air, assuming complete intercooling (i.e., air returns to 25 °C between stages).
> (c) The actual power required to compress **150 kg/hr** of air.

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### (a) Intermediate Pressure for Minimum Work

For a two-stage compressor, the optimum intermediate pressure is the **geometric mean** of the suction and discharge pressures:

$$P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}$$

$$P_x = \sqrt{1.013 \times 25}$$

$$P_x = \sqrt{25.325}$$

$$\boxed{P_x = 5.032 \text{ bar}}$$

#### (b) Theoretical Work per kg (with complete intercooling)

Since the air is cooled back to the initial temperature (25 °C) between stages, the **second stage starts from the same initial state as the first stage** (in terms of $P_1 V_1$). The pressure ratio per stage is:

$$r = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{25}{1.013}} = \sqrt{24.68} = 4.968$$

Adiabatic work per stage (per kg of air):

$$W_{adi} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot \dfrac{R T_1}{M} \left[r^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$$

where $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $M = 28.97$ g/mol, $T_1 = 298.15$ K, and $(\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.4/1.4 = 0.2857$.

Compute $r^{0.2857}$:

$$r^{0.2857} = 4.968^{0.2857}$$

Taking natural log: $\ln(4.968) = 1.6033$. Multiply by $0.2857$: $0.4581$. Exponentiate: $e^{0.4581} = 1.5810$.

So $r^{0.2857} - 1 = 0.5810$.

Now compute the prefactor:

$$\dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} = \dfrac{1.4}{0.4} = 3.5$$

$$\dfrac{R T_1}{M} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 298.15}{0.02897} = 85{,}549 \text{ J/kg}$$

$$W_{adi,\,per\,stage} = 3.5 \times 85{,}549 \times 0.5810$$

$$W_{adi,\,per\,stage} = 173{,}959 \text{ J/kg} = 173.96 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Total theoretical work for two stages:

$$W_{theor} = 2 \times 173.96 = 347.92 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

#### (c) Actual Power with 85% Stage Efficiency

Actual work per stage:

$$W_{actual,\,per\,stage} = \dfrac{W_{adi}}{\eta_{ad}} = \dfrac{173.96}{0.85} = 204.66 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Total actual work:

$$W_{total} = 2 \times 204.66 = 409.32 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = 150$ kg/hr $= 0.04167$ kg/s.

Power required:

$$P = \dot{m} \times W_{total} = 0.04167 \times 409.32$$

$$\boxed{P = 17.05 \text{ kW}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Compressor Types

| Criterion | Reciprocating | Rotary Screw | Centrifugal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Discharge Pressure** | Up to 1000 bar | 7 – 13 bar | 1 – 50 bar |
| **Flow Capacity** | 0.1 – 100 m³/min | 5 – 500 m³/min | 100 – 50,000 m³/min |
| **Isothermal η (typical)** | 55 – 70% | 60 – 75% | 65 – 80% (adiabatic η) |
| **Oil in Discharge** | Possible (with lubrication) | Yes (oil-flooded) | None (oil-free) |
| **Pulsation** | Yes — needs pulsation dampener | Smooth | Smooth |
| **Maintenance** | High (valves, rings) | Low–Medium | Low |
| **Noise (dBA)** | 85 – 95 | 70 – 85 | 75 – 90 |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Best for (CPI)** | High-pressure air, N₂, O₂ | General plant air, tools | Large continuous process air |
| **Turndown Ratio** | 30 – 100% (with unloaders) | 20 – 100% (VFD) | 70 – 105% (surge-limited) |

## 3.4 Intercooler Pressure Logic — Why Geometric Mean?

For a **multi-stage compressor** with $k$ stages, the total work is:

$$W_{total} = k \cdot \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[(P_k/P_1)^{(n-1)/(nk)} - 1\right]$$

To minimize $W_{total}$ for fixed $P_1$, $P_k$, $k$, $n$ — we differentiate with respect to intermediate pressures. The result (using the symmetry argument that the optimum splits the overall pressure ratio equally across stages):

$$P_{intermediate,\,optimum} = (P_1 \cdot P_2)^{1/2} = (P_1)^{1/2} (P_2)^{1/2}$$

This is why the geometric mean of suction and discharge gives minimum work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $P_x = (P_1 + P_2)/2$ (arithmetic mean) — this is WRONG. It is ALWAYS the **geometric mean**, and you MUST show the derivation steps for full credit.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. Distinguish between isothermal and adiabatic compression. Which requires more work, and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Isothermal compression** (n = 1) keeps temperature constant by continuously removing heat; work is $W = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$.
- **Adiabatic compression** (n = γ = 1.4 for air) has no heat removal; temperature rises sharply; work is $W = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P_1 V_1 [(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1]$.
- **Adiabatic work > Isothermal work** because compression against rising internal pressure (due to heating) demands more work input.
- **(1 mark)** for each correct point, **(1 mark)** for stating that isothermal is the theoretical minimum.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2. Define volumetric efficiency of a reciprocating compressor. State the effect of clearance ratio on it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Volumetric efficiency is the ratio of actual volume of air drawn in (per cycle) to the swept volume of the piston: $\eta_v = V_{actual} / V_{swept}$.
- Mathematical form: $\eta_v = 1 + C - C(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$ where $C$ is clearance ratio.
- **Effect:** As clearance ratio increases, $\eta_v$ decreases (more re-expanded clearance air is lost). However, a small clearance is **necessary** to prevent mechanical impact between piston and valve head. **(1 mark)** for formula, **(1 mark)** for trend, **(1 mark)** for practical justification.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question with Internal Choice]**

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** [7 marks] With neat functional sketches, classify the major types of air compressors used in chemical process industries. Compare reciprocating and centrifugal compressors on the basis of: (i) working principle, (ii) discharge pressure range, (iii) air delivery (FAD), (iv) isentropic efficiency, (v) maintenance, and (vi) typical industrial application.

**(b)** [7 marks] A two-stage reciprocating compressor compresses **5 m³/min** of free air from **1.013 bar, 30 °C** to **9 bar**. The clearance ratio is **4%**, polytropic index **n = 1.32**, and mechanical efficiency is **92%**. Calculate: (i) volumetric efficiency, (ii) actual volume of air handled per minute, (iii) indicated work per kg, and (iv) shaft power in kW. Take R = 8.314 J/mol·K and M = 29 g/mol for air.

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Compressor Classification [7 marks]

**Classification (3 marks):**

- **A. Positive Displacement (PD):** Air is trapped in a fixed volume which is mechanically reduced. Sub-types:
  - (i) Reciprocating (piston-cylinder)
  - (ii) Rotary (screw, vane, scroll, lobe)
- **B. Dynamic (Turbo):** Air is accelerated to high velocity and the kinetic energy is converted to pressure.
  - (i) Centrifugal (radial outflow)
  - (ii) Axial flow

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Reciprocating | Centrifugal |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Principle | Piston in cylinder, cyclic | Impeller + diffuser |
| (ii) Pressure | 7 – 1000 bar | 1 – 50 bar (multi-stage) |
| (iii) FAD | 0.1 – 100 m³/min | 100 – 50,000 m³/min |
| (iv) Isentropic η | 55 – 70% (isothermal) | 70 – 85% (adiabatic) |
| (v) Maintenance | High (rings, valves) | Low (no rubbing contact) |
| (vi) Application | High-pressure N₂, O₂, instrument air backup | Large plant air, ethylene crackers, ammonia plants |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]

**Given:** $V_1 = 5$ m³/min, $P_1 = 1.013$ bar, $T_1 = 303.15$ K, $P_2 = 9$ bar, $C = 0.04$, $n = 1.32$, $\eta_m = 0.92$.

**(i) Volumetric efficiency** [2 marks]:

$$\eta_v = 1 + C - C \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$$

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{9}{1.013} = 8.884$$

$$8.884^{1/1.32} = 8.884^{0.7576}$$

Take ln: $\ln(8.884) = 2.184$, multiply by $0.7576$: $1.655$, exponentiate: $e^{1.655} = 5.234$.

$$\eta_v = 1 + 0.04 - 0.04 \times 5.234 = 1.04 - 0.2094 = 0.8306$$

$$\boxed{\eta_v = 83.06\%}$$

**(ii) Actual air handled per minute** [1 mark]:

$$V_{actual} = V_1 \times \eta_v = 5 \times 0.8306 = 4.153 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

**(iii) Indicated work per kg** [2 marks]:

$$W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \cdot \dfrac{R T_1}{M} \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$

$\dfrac{n}{n-1} = \dfrac{1.32}{0.32} = 4.125$

$\dfrac{RT_1}{M} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 303.15}{0.029} = 86{,}911$ J/kg

$(n-1)/n = 0.32/1.32 = 0.2424$

$(P_2/P_1)^{0.2424} = 8.884^{0.2424} = e^{0.2424 \times 2.184} = e^{0.5294} = 1.768$

$W_{indicated} = 4.125 \times 86{,}911 \times (1.768 - 1) = 4.125 \times 86{,}911 \times 0.768$

$W_{indicated} = 275{,}290$ J/kg $= 275.29$ kJ/kg

**(iv) Shaft power** [2 marks]:

Mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_1 V_{actual} M}{R T_1} = \dfrac{1.013 \times 10^5 \times (4.153/60) \times 0.029}{8.314 \times 303.15}$$

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{101{,}300 \times 0.0692 \times 0.029}{2520.4} = \dfrac{203.3}{2520.4} = 0.0807 \text{ kg/s}$$

Shaft power:

$$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \times W_{indicated}}{\eta_m} = \dfrac{0.0807 \times 275.29}{0.92}$$

$$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{22.22}{0.92} = 24.15 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{shaft} \approx 24.15 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** [7 marks] Enumerate the major components of an industrial compressed air system in the order of air flow. Explain the function and design considerations of: (i) aftercooler, (ii) air receiver, (iii) desiccant dryer, and (iv) coalescing filter.

**(b)** [7 marks] Discuss the major factors affecting the **performance and efficiency** of air compressors. Explain how each of the following influences power consumption: (i) inlet air temperature, (ii) altitude of installation, (iii) humidity, (iv) leakage in the distribution network, (v) fouled intake filters, and (vi) inter-stage cooling effectiveness.

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Compressed Air System Components [7 marks]

**Flow order (1 mark for sequence):**
Intake filter → Compressor → Aftercooler → Moisture separator → Receiver → Refrigeration dryer → Desiccant dryer → Particulate filter → Coalescing filter → Activated carbon filter → Pressure regulator → Distribution piping → End users.

**Component-wise (6 marks = 1.5 each):**

1. **Aftercooler** — Reduces discharge air temperature from **~150 °C** to within **~10 °C** of ambient, condensing ~70% of moisture. Types: water-cooled shell-and-tube (industrial), air-cooled finned (small units). Design: outlet temperature ≤ **40 °C**, pressure drop ≤ 0.1 bar.

2. **Air Receiver** — Cylindrical pressure vessel (ASME Sec VIII Div 1) that stores compressed air, dampens pulsations, and provides buffer for peak demands. Sized for **2–5 minutes** of flow at full load. Includes safety relief valve, pressure gauge, drain trap, and manhole.

3. **Desiccant Dryer** — Uses adsorbent material (silica gel, activated alumina, or molecular sieve) to lower dew point to **−40 °C** (ISO 8573-1 Class 2). Twin-tower design with one tower drying while the other regenerates. Mandatory for instrument-quality air.

4. **Coalescing Filter** — Removes liquid water and oil aerosols down to **0.01 micron** with **≥ 99.99%** efficiency. Uses borosilicate microfiber media. Must be preceded by a particulate filter to prevent media fouling.

#### Part (b) — Factors Affecting Performance & Efficiency [7 marks]

| Factor | Effect | Magnitude (Typical) |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Inlet air temperature** | Higher $T_1$ → lower $\rho_1$ → less mass compressed per stroke → more power per kg; also raises discharge $T$ | +10 °C inlet → +3–4% power |
| **(ii) Altitude** | Lower atmospheric $P_1$ → reduced mass flow, lower discharge $P_2$ available | +1000 m → −12% FAD, −8% efficiency |
| **(iii) Humidity** | Water vapor is non-ideal; high RH increases specific volume, reduces $\eta_v$ and requires extra drying load | +20% RH → −2% efficiency |
| **(iv) Piping/leakage** | 1 mm hole at 7 bar leaks ~**1.5 m³/min** → wasted compression work | 20–30% of total air loss in many plants |
| **(v) Filter fouling** | Increases inlet vacuum (lower $P_1$) → lower $\eta_v$, higher $T_{discharge}$ | 0.1 bar drop → −3% FAD |
| **(vi) Inter-stage cooling** | Better cooling → closer to isothermal → lower work; poor cooling → higher stage work and higher discharge $T$ | Effective cooling can save 15–20% power |

**Synthesis (1 mark):** A well-designed compressed air system can lose **30–50% of input power** to avoidable factors. KTU's emphasis on these factors reflects the **economic and energy audit** perspective mandated by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) in India.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong mean for intercooler pressure:** Use geometric mean $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$, NOT arithmetic.
> 2. **Forgetting the mass flow conversion** when power is asked — students use volume flow directly.
> 3. **Mixing isothermal and adiabatic efficiencies** — for reciprocating, use isothermal; for centrifugal, use isentropic.
> 4. **Omitting the $\eta_m$ division** for shaft power — indicated work ≠ shaft work.
> 5. **Wrong $R/M$ value:** Use **287 J/kg·K** for air (or equivalently $8.314/0.029$) — students often use 8.314 alone and lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Clearance ratio confusion:** Some students write $C = V_s/V_c$ instead of $C = V_c/V_s$. Always $V_c$ in numerator.
> 7. **No sketches in classification questions:** For 7-mark comparison questions, a labeled block diagram is MANDATORY (minimum 2 marks reserved).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2**

- [x] **Compressors = "4th utility"** in chemical plants; supply at **7–8.5 bar (g)**.
- [x] **Two classes:** Positive Displacement (Reciprocating, Screw, Vane, Scroll) and Dynamic (Centrifugal, Axial).
- [x] **PD compressors** = trapped volume mechanically reduced; **Dynamic** = kinetic energy → pressure.
- [x] **Polytropic equation** $PV^n = C$ is the master relation; $n$ varies from 1 (isothermal) to $\gamma$ (adiabatic).
- [x] **For air, $\gamma = 1.4$** and $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 287$ J/kg·K.
- [x] **Work equations** for isothermal, adiabatic, and polytropic — master all three.
- [x] **Isothermal work is THEORETICAL MINIMUM**; adiabatic is the practical worst case.
- [x] **Four efficiencies:** Isothermal (η_iso), Adiabatic (η_ad), Mechanical (η_m), Volumetric (η_v).
- [x] **Volumetric efficiency** $\eta_v = 1 + C - C(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$ — applies to reciprocating ONLY.
- [x] **Optimum intermediate pressure** for multi-stage = **geometric mean** of inlet and discharge.
- [x] **System components in order:** Filter → Compressor → Aftercooler → Separator → Receiver → Dryer → Filters → Regulator → Piping.
- [x] **Dew point targets:** Refrigeration dryer → +3 °C; Desiccant dryer → **−40 °C** (instrument air).
- [x] **Altitude penalty:** ~12% FAD loss per 1000 m elevation gain.
- [x] **Leakage losses** can be 20–30% of total compressed air — biggest single inefficiency in many plants.
- [x] **Inter-stage cooling** effectiveness directly reduces power by 15–20%.
- [x] **Reciprocating** = high pressure, low flow, pulsating; **Centrifugal** = high flow, moderate pressure, smooth.
- [x] **Oil-free discharge** is mandatory for instrument air and food/pharma applications → centrifugal or oil-free screw.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Memory Hook (for last-night revision):**
> *"Isothermal = minimum work; Adiabatic = maximum work; Polytropic = real world. Geometric mean for inter-stage; arithmetic mean is WRONG. Clean filter, cool intake, dry air, tight pipes = maximum efficiency."*

<h1>1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview</h1>

<h2>1.1 What is a Compressed Air System?</h2>

<p>A <strong>Compressed Air System</strong> is an integrated utility network that converts ambient atmospheric air into a higher-pressure, cleaner, and drier working fluid using mechanical work. In chemical process industries (CPI), it is classified as the <strong>"fourth utility"</strong> after water, steam, and electricity because nearly every plant — from instrumentation and pneumatic conveying to reactor agitation and valve actuation — depends on it.</p>

<blockquote>
<p><strong>KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT636 – Module 2):</strong><br>
A compressed air system comprises the air intake filter, the compressor (prime mover + machine), the inter-stage/after coolers, the moisture separator, the air receiver, the dryer, the distribution piping network, and the end-use pressure regulators. The system delivers air typically at <strong>0.7 MPa to 0.85 MPa (gauge)</strong> for plant use, with a guaranteed dew point of <strong>−40 °C</strong> for instrument-quality air.</p>
</blockquote>

<h3>Conceptual Analogy: The "Lung and Artery" of the Plant</h3>
<p>Imagine a human body: the <strong>compressor is the lung</strong> that inhales raw air and pressurizes it; the <strong>aftercooler and dryer are the cooling veins</strong> that clean the "blood"; the <strong>receiver tank is the heart</strong> that stores and smooths the flow; and the <strong>piping network is the arteries</strong> that deliver purified fluid to every organ (actuator, valve, tool). If the lung is weak, the heart (receiver) cannot supply steady flow, and the arteries (pipes) carry impure, humid air — leading to valve sticking, instrument drift, and plant shutdowns.</p>

<h2>1.2 Classification of Air Compressors — At a Glance</h2>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Broad Class</th>
<th>Working Principle</th>
<th>Typical Capacity</th>
<th>Typical Discharge</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>Positive Displacement (PD)</strong></td>
<td>Air trapped in a fixed volume, then mechanically reduced</td>
<td>Low to medium flow (< 500 m³/min)</td>
<td>High pressure (up to 1000 bar in reciprocating)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>Dynamic (Turbo)</strong></td>
<td>Air accelerated by impeller; kinetic energy converted to pressure</td>
<td>High flow (> 500 m³/min)</td>
<td>Moderate pressure (1–15 bar typical)</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<blockquote>
<p><strong>KTU High-Yield Fact:</strong> For 80% of chemical process plants in India, <strong>oil-flooded rotary screw compressors</strong> dominate the low-pressure range (7–13 bar), while <strong>centrifugal compressors</strong> dominate large-scale continuous processes (e.g., ethylene crackers, ammonia plants) due to their oil-free discharge and high turndown capability.</p>
</blockquote>

<h3>1.2.1 Positive Displacement Compressors</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Reciprocating (Piston) Compressor</strong> — Uses a piston moving inside a cylinder. Available as single-stage, two-stage, or multi-stage. Best for high-pressure, low-flow duties.</li>
<li><strong>Rotary Screw Compressor</strong> — Two intermeshing helical rotors (male + female) trap, compress, and discharge air continuously. Smooth, vibration-free, and high reliability.</li>
<li><strong>Rotary Vane Compressor</strong> — An eccentric rotor carries sliding vanes; air trapped between consecutive vanes is compressed. Used in oil-injected medium-duty applications.</li>
<li><strong>Scroll Compressor</strong> — Two interleaving spiral scrolls; one is fixed, the other orbits. Very low noise, used in oil-free instrument air.</li>
</ul>

<h3>1.2.2 Dynamic (Turbo) Compressors</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Centrifugal Compressor</strong> — Air enters the eye of a high-speed impeller, gains kinetic energy, and is converted to pressure in the diffuser/volute. Multi-stage units can reach 50+ bar.</li>
<li><strong>Axial Flow Compressor</strong> — Air flows parallel to the axis through alternating rows of rotating and stationary blades. Used in very large flow rates (gas turbine intakes).</li>
</ul>

<blockquote>
<p><strong>Selection Mnemonic (KTU Board Favorite):</strong><br>
<em>"Reciprocating for high pressure, Screw for medium, Centrifugal for huge flow."</em></p>
</blockquote>

<h1>2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet</h1>

<h2>2.1 Theoretical Work of Compression</h2>

<p>The theoretical work required to compress a unit mass of gas from inlet state (P₁, V₁) to discharge state (P₂, V₂) depends on the <strong>polytropic process equation</strong>:</p>

<p>$$PV^n = \text{constant}$$</p>

<p>where <strong>n = polytropic index</strong>, which depends on heat exchange with the surroundings.</p>

<h3>2.1.1 The Three Ideal Compression Processes</h3>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Process</th>
<th>Index $n$</th>
<th>Heat Exchange</th>
<th>Work Equation (per kg)</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>Isothermal</td>
<td>$n = 1$</td>
<td>Heat removed fully to keep T constant</td>
<td>$W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Adiabatic (Isentropic)</td>
<td>$n = \gamma = C_p / C_v$</td>
<td>No heat transfer (insulated)</td>
<td>$W_{adi} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Polytropic</td>
<td>$1 < n < \gamma$</td>
<td>Partial cooling</td>
<td>$W_{poly} = \frac{n}{n - 1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<p>For air, the adiabatic index is <strong>γ = 1.4</strong> (since $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K).</p>

<h3>2.1.2 Why Isothermal Work is the Minimum</h3>
<p>In an ideal isothermal process, ALL the heat generated during compression is removed. Since $W_{iso} < W_{adi}$, real compressors try to mimic isothermal behavior by:</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>Water-jacketing</strong> the cylinder (reciprocating)</li>
<li><strong>Inter-stage cooling</strong> between stages</li>
<li><strong>Oil-injection</strong> cooling (screw compressors)</li>
<li><strong>External aftercoolers</strong> after discharge</li>
</ul>

<h2>2.2 Compressor Efficiency — A Multi-Parameter Concept</h2>

<blockquote>
<p><strong>KTU Examiner Tip:</strong> Students often confuse "compressor efficiency" with "compressor effectiveness." In KTU papers, expect numericals on <strong>isothermal, adiabatic (isentropic), mechanical, and overall (isentropic + mechanical) efficiencies</strong> — each defined differently. Memorize the four equations below.</p>
</blockquote>

<h3>2.2.1 Isothermal Efficiency (η_iso)</h3>
<p>Compares actual work to the theoretical isothermal (minimum possible) work:</p>

<p>$$\eta_{iso} = \frac{W_{iso,\,ideal}}{W_{actual}} \times 100\%$$</p>

<ul>
<li><strong>Application:</strong> Reciprocating compressors with good intercooling.</li>
<li><strong>Typical value:</strong> 50 – 70% (single-stage), 60 – 80% (multi-stage).</li>
</ul>

<h3>2.2.2 Adiabatic (Isentropic) Efficiency (η_ad)</h3>
<p>Used for dynamic (turbo) compressors where the process is nearly adiabatic:</p>

<p>$$\eta_{ad} = \frac{W_{adi,\,ideal}}{W_{actual}} \times 100\%$$</p>

<ul>
<li><strong>Application:</strong> Centrifugal and axial compressors.</li>
<li><strong>Typical value:</strong> 70 – 85% for centrifugal, 85 – 92% for axial.</li>
</ul>

<h3>2.2.3 Mechanical Efficiency (η_m)</h3>
<p>Accounts for friction losses in bearings, gears, and seals:</p>

<p>$$\eta_m = \frac{W_{indicated}}{W_{shaft}} \times 100\%$$</p>

<p>where $W_{shaft} = W_{indicated} + W_{friction}$.</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>Typical value:</strong> 90 – 95%.</li>
</ul>

<h3>2.2.4 Volumetric Efficiency (η_v) — Reciprocating Only</h3>
<p>Ratio of actual suction volume to swept volume (displacement):</p>

<p>$$\eta_v = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_{swept}} = 1 + C - C \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$$</p>

<p>where <strong>C = clearance ratio</strong> = $V_c / V_s$ (typically 3 – 8% for industrial units).</p>

<blockquote>
<p>The clearance volume is a "dead space" left in the cylinder to prevent the piston from striking the valve plate. A higher clearance ratio REDUCES volumetric efficiency but PROTECTS the compressor mechanically.</p>
</blockquote>

<h3>2.2.5 Overall (Brake) Efficiency (η_overall)</h3>
<p>Combines isothermal/adiabatic with mechanical:</p>

<p>$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{iso} \times \eta_m \quad \text{(for reciprocating)}$$</p>

<p>$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{ad} \times \eta_m \quad \text{(for centrifugal)}$$</p>

<h2>2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet</h2>

<h2>2.4 Compressed Air System Components — Functional Breakdown</h2>

<p>A complete industrial compressed air system has the following components in flow order:</p>

<h3>2.4.1 Air Intake Filter</h3>
<ul>
<li>Removes atmospheric dust (typically 5–10 micron).</li>
<li>Protects the compressor internals from abrasion.</li>
<li>Pressure drop should not exceed <strong>0.05 bar</strong> (clean).</li>
</ul>

<h3>2.4.2 Compressor (Prime Mover + Machine)</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Prime mover:</strong> Electric motor (most common in CPI) or gas/diesel engine.</li>
<li><strong>Machine:</strong> Reciprocating / Screw / Centrifugal (selected per duty).</li>
<li>Motor power: $P = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot W_{adi}}{\eta_{overall}}$ (kW)</li>
</ul>

<h3>2.4.3 Aftercooler (Inter-Cooler / After-Cooler)</h3>
<ul>
<li>Reduces discharge air temperature from <strong>~150 °C</strong> to within <strong>~10 °C</strong> of ambient.</li>
<li>Types: Water-cooled shell-and-tube, air-cooled finned.</li>
<li>Condenses ~70% of the moisture carried as vapor.</li>
</ul>

<h3>2.4.4 Moisture Separator / Receiver (Air Receiver Tank)</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Receiver:</strong> Cylindrical pressure vessel (ASME Sec VIII), sized to give <strong>2–5 minutes</strong> of buffer time at full flow.</li>
<li><strong>Sizing rule of thumb:</strong> $V_{receiver} = 1.5 \times Q \times \frac{P_{max}}{P_{max} - P_{min}}$ (m³) where Q is flow in m³/min.</li>
<li>Provides pulsation dampening (reciprocating) and condensate drop-out.</li>
</ul>

<h3>2.4.5 Air Dryer</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Refrigeration (chilled) dryer:</strong> Dew point +3 °C; cheapest.</li>
<li><strong>Desiccant (adsorption) dryer:</strong> Dew point −40 °C; mandatory for instrument air.</li>
<li><strong>Membrane dryer:</strong> Compact, used in remote installations.</li>
</ul>

<h3>2.4.6 Particulate and Coalescing Filters</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Particulate filter:</strong> 1 micron → removes solid dust.</li>
<li><strong>Coalescing filter:</strong> 0.01 micron → removes oil aerosols + water droplets.</li>
<li><strong>Activated carbon filter:</strong> Removes oil vapor → down to 0.003 mg/m³.</li>
</ul>

<h3>2.4.7 Pressure Regulators and Distribution Network</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Regulator:</strong> Maintains downstream pressure within ±0.1 bar.</li>
<li><strong>Piping:</strong> Sized for <strong>6 m/s</strong> air velocity; drops of 0.1 bar/100 m typical.</li>
<li><strong>Moisture traps:</strong> Auto-drain valves at low points.</li>
</ul>

<h2>2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility</h2>

<p>In a <strong>petrochemical plant</strong>, a single centrifugal compressor supplying <strong>10,000 Nm³/hr</strong> of instrument air at 7 bar (g) can power:</p>
<ul>
<li>~5,000 pneumatic control valves</li>
<li>~200 pneumatic actuators</li>
<li>24/7 DCS pneumatic signal lines</li>
<li>Pneumatic conveying of catalysts</li>
</ul>

<p>A <strong>1% reduction in compressor efficiency</strong> at this scale translates to <strong>₹15–20 lakhs/year</strong> in extra electricity cost. Hence, KTU emphasizes the factors below.</p>
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
<h1>3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix</h1>

<h2>3.1 Derivation: Work of Polytropic Compression</h2>

<p>We begin from the first law of thermodynamics for an open system (control volume) under steady-flow, with negligible kinetic and potential energy changes:</p>

<p>$$dW = -V \, dP \quad \text{(reversible flow work)}$$</p>

<p>The polytropic process satisfies $P V^n = C$ (constant). Differentiating:</p>

<p>$$V^n \, dP + n P V^{n-1} \, dV = 0$$</p>

<p>$$dV = -\frac{V}{nP} \, dP$$</p>

<p>Substituting back into the work expression for a unit mass:</p>

<p>$$W = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} V(-dP) = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{V}{nP} P \, dP$$</p>

<p>Using $P_1 V_1^n = P V^n$ implies $V = V_1 (P_1/P)^{1/n}$, so:</p>

<p>$$W = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} V_1 \left(\frac{P_1}{P}\right)^{1/n} \frac{1}{n} \left(\frac{P}{P_1}\right) dP$$</p>

<p>$$W = \frac{V_1 P_1^{1/n}}{n} \int_{P_1}^{P_2} P^{(1/n) - 1} \, dP$$</p>

<p>Integrating with $m = (1/n) - 1$:</p>

<p>$$W = \frac{V_1 P_1^{1/n}}{n} \cdot \frac{1}{m+1} \left[P_2^{m+1} - P_1^{m+1}\right]$$</p>

<p>With $m+1 = 1/n$:</p>

<p>$$W = V_1 P_1 \cdot \frac{n}{n-1} \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$</p>

<blockquote>
<p><strong>Limiting cases of the polytropic equation:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li>$n = 1$ → $W = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$ (isothermal, using L'Hôpital's rule)</li>
<li>$n = \gamma$ → $W = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} P_1 V_1 \left[ (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$ (adiabatic)</li>
</ul>
</blockquote>

<h2>3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Typical KTU Board Question</h2>

<blockquote>
<p><strong>Question:</strong> A two-stage reciprocating compressor compresses air from <strong>1.013 bar and 25 °C</strong> to a final pressure of <strong>25 bar</strong>. The isentropic index is <strong>γ = 1.4</strong> and the isentropic efficiency of each stage is <strong>85%</strong>. Calculate:<br>
(a) The intermediate (inter-cooler) pressure for minimum work.<br>
(b) The total work of compression per kg of air, assuming complete intercooling (i.e., air returns to 25 °C between stages).<br>
(c) The actual power required to compress <strong>150 kg/hr</strong> of air.</p>
</blockquote>

<h3>Step-by-Step Solution</h3>

<h4>(a) Intermediate Pressure for Minimum Work</h4>
<p>For a two-stage compressor, the optimum intermediate pressure is the <strong>geometric mean</strong> of the suction and discharge pressures:</p>

<p>$$P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}$$</p>

<p>$$P_x = \sqrt{1.013 \times 25}$$</p>

<p>$$P_x = \sqrt{25.325}$$</p>

<p>$$\boxed{P_x = 5.032 \text{ bar}}$$</p>

<h4>(b) Theoretical Work per kg (with complete intercooling)</h4>
<p>Since the air is cooled back to the initial temperature (25 °C) between stages, the <strong>second stage starts from the same initial state as the first stage</strong> (in terms of $P_1 V_1$). The pressure ratio per stage is:</p>

<p>$$r = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \sqrt{\frac{25}{1.013}} = \sqrt{24.68} = 4.968$$</p>

<p>Adiabatic work per stage (per kg of air):</p>

<p>$$W_{adi} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot \frac{R T_1}{M} \left[ r^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$</p>

<p>where $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $M = 28.97$ g/mol, $T_1 = 298.15$ K, and $(\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.4/1.4 = 0.2857$.</p>

<p>Compute $r^{0.2857}$:</p>

<p>$$r^{0.2857} = 4.968^{0.2857}$$</p>

<p>Taking natural log: $\ln(4.968) = 1.6033$. Multiply by $0.2857$: $0.4581$. Exponentiate: $e^{0.4581} = 1.5810$.</p>

<p>So $r^{0.2857} - 1 = 0.5810$.</p>

<p>Now compute the prefactor:</p>

<p>$$\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{1.4}{0.4} = 3.5$$</p>

<p>$$\frac{R T_1}{M} = \frac{8.314 \times 298.15}{0.02897} = 85{,}549 \text{ J/kg}$$</p>

<p>$$W_{adi,\,per\,stage} = 3.5 \times 85{,}549 \times 0.5810$$</p>

<p>$$W_{adi,\,per\,stage} = 173{,}959 \text{ J/kg} = 173.96 \text{ kJ/kg}$$</p>

<p>Total theoretical work for two stages:</p>

<p>$$W_{theor} = 2 \times 173.96 = 347.92 \text{ kJ/kg}$$</p>

<h4>(c) Actual Power with 85% Stage Efficiency</h4>
<p>Actual work per stage:</p>

<p>$$W_{actual,\,per\,stage} = \frac{W_{adi}}{\eta_{ad}} = \frac{173.96}{0.85} = 204.66 \text{ kJ/kg}$$</p>

<p>Total actual work:</p>

<p>$$W_{total} = 2 \times 204.66 = 409.32 \text{ kJ/kg}$$</p>

<p>Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = 150$ kg/hr $= 0.04167$ kg/s.</p>

<p>Power required:</p>

<p>$$P = \dot{m} \times W_{total} = 0.04167 \times 409.32$$</p>

<p>$$\boxed{P = 17.05 \text{ kW}}$$</p>

<h2>3.4 Intercooler Pressure Logic — Why Geometric Mean?</h2>

<p>For a <strong>multi-stage compressor</strong> with $k$ stages, the total work is:</p>

<p>$$W_{total} = k \cdot \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ (P_k/P_1)^{(n-1)/(nk)} - 1 \right]$$</p>

<p>To minimize $W_{total}$ for fixed $P_1$, $P_k$, $k$, $n$ — we differentiate with respect to intermediate pressures. The result (using the symmetry argument that the optimum splits the overall pressure ratio equally across stages):</p>

<p>$$P_{intermediate,\,optimum} = (P_1 \cdot P_2)^{1/2} = (P_1)^{1/2} (P_2)^{1/2}$$</p>

<p>This is why the geometric mean of suction and discharge gives minimum work.</p>

<blockquote>
<p><strong>Practical KTU Pitfall:</strong> Students often write $P_x = (P_1 + P_2)/2$ (arithmetic mean) — this is WRONG. It is ALWAYS the <strong>geometric mean</strong>, and you MUST show the derivation steps for full credit.</p>
</blockquote>
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<h1>4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics</h1>

<h2>4.1 Block Diagram — Compressed Air System Flow</h2>

<pre class="mermaid">
flowchart LR
    A[ATMOSPHERIC AIR INTAKE] --> B[AIR INTAKE FILTER 5 to 10 micron]
    B --> C[COMPRESSOR PRIME MOVER PLUS MACHINE]
    C --> D[AFTERCOOLER STAGE 1]
    D --> E[INTERCOOLER BETWEEN STAGES]
    E --> F[COMPRESSOR STAGE 2]
    F --> G[FINAL AFTERCOOLER]
