    I --> J[PARTICULATE FILTER 1 micron]
    J --> K[COALESCING FILTER 0.01 micron]
    K --> L[ACTIVATED CARBON FILTER]
    L --> M[PRESSURE REGULATOR]
    M --> N[DISTRIBUTION PIPING NETWORK]
    N --> O[END USERS VALVES ACTUATORS TOOLS]
    O --> P[CONDENSATE DRAIN AT LOW POINTS]
</pre>

<h2>4.2 Functional Architecture — Reciprocating Compressor Internal Cycles</h2>

<pre class="mermaid">
flowchart TD
    A1[SUCTION STROKE PISTON MOVES DOWN] --> A2[LOW PRESSURE AIR ENTERS CYLINDER]
    A2 --> A3[INLET VALVE OPENS]
    A3 --> A4[COMPRESSION STROKE PISTON MOVES UP]
    A4 --> A5[AIR COMPRESSED POLYTROPICALLY]
    A5 --> A6[INLET VALVE CLOSES]
    A6 --> A7[DISCHARGE VALVE OPENS AT P EQUALS P2]
    A7 --> A8[EXHAUST STROKE AIR PUSHED TO RECEIVER]
    A8 --> A9[REEXPANSION OF CLEARANCE VOLUME]
    A9 --> A1
</pre>

<h2>4.3 Functional Architecture — Centrifugal Compressor Energy Path</h2>

<pre class="mermaid">
flowchart LR
    B1[AXIAL INLET GUIDE VANES] --> B2[ROTATING IMPELLER KINETIC ENERGY ADDED]
    B2 --> B3[DIFFUSER OR VOLUTE VELOCITY DECELERATED]
    B3 --> B4[PRESSURE RECOVERY STATIC ENTHALPY RISE]
    B4 --> B5[DELTA T RISE TO NEXT STAGE]
    B5 --> B6[REPEAT FOR MULTIPLE STAGES]
    B6 --> B7[FINAL DISCHARGE TO AFTERCOOLER]
</pre>

<h2>4.4 Component Dependency Matrix</h2>

<pre class="mermaid">
flowchart TD
    C1[AMBIENT CONDITIONS T RH ALTITUDE] --> C2[INTAKE AIR DENSITY]
    C2 --> C3[MASS FLOW AND POWER]
    C4[COOLING WATER TEMPERATURE] --> C5[INTERCOOLER EFFECTIVENESS]
    C5 --> C6[STAGE DISCHARGE TEMP]
    C6 --> C7[VOLUMETRIC EFFICIENCY]
    C7 --> C8[FREE AIR DELIVERY FAD]
    C9[PIPING LEAKAGE] --> C10[APPARENT FAD LOSS]
    C11[FILTER FOULING] --> C12[INLET PRESSURE DROP]
    C12 --> C3
    C3 --> C13[SHAFT POWER KW]
    C13 --> C14[ELECTRICITY COST RS PER YEAR]
</pre>
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
<h1>5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap</h1>

<h2>5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)</h2>

<blockquote>
<p><strong>[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]</strong></p>
</blockquote>

<p><strong>Q1. Distinguish between isothermal and adiabatic compression. Which requires more work, and why?</strong></p>

<p><strong>Model Answer (3 marks):</strong></p>
<ul>
<li><strong>Isothermal compression</strong> (n = 1) keeps temperature constant by continuously removing heat; work is $W = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$.</li>
<li><strong>Adiabatic compression</strong> (n = γ = 1.4 for air) has no heat removal; temperature rises sharply; work is $W = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$.</li>
<li><strong>Adiabatic work > Isothermal work</strong> because compression against rising internal pressure (due to heating) demands more work input.</li>
<li><strong>(1 mark)</strong> for each correct point, <strong>(1 mark)</strong> for stating that isothermal is the theoretical minimum.</li>
</ul>

<hr>

<blockquote>
<p><strong>[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]</strong></p>
</blockquote>

<p><strong>Q2. Define volumetric efficiency of a reciprocating compressor. State the effect of clearance ratio on it.</strong></p>

<p><strong>Model Answer (3 marks):</strong></p>
<ul>
<li>Volumetric efficiency is the ratio of actual volume of air drawn in (per cycle) to the swept volume of the piston: $\eta_v = V_{actual} / V_{swept}$.</li>
<li>Mathematical form: $\eta_v = 1 + C - C(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$ where $C$ is clearance ratio.</li>
<li><strong>Effect:</strong> As clearance ratio increases, $\eta_v$ decreases (more re-expanded clearance air is lost). However, a small clearance is <strong>necessary</strong> to prevent mechanical impact between piston and valve head. <strong>(1 mark)</strong> for formula, <strong>(1 mark)</strong> for trend, <strong>(1 mark)</strong> for practical justification.</li>
</ul>

<hr>

<h2>5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)</h2>

<blockquote>
<p><strong>[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question with Internal Choice]</strong></p>
</blockquote>

<h3>Question A — 14 Marks</h3>

<p><strong>(a)</strong> [7 marks] With neat functional sketches, classify the major types of air compressors used in chemical process industries. Compare reciprocating and centrifugal compressors on the basis of: (i) working principle, (ii) discharge pressure range, (iii) air delivery (FAD), (iv) isentropic efficiency, (v) maintenance, and (vi) typical industrial application.</p>

<p><strong>(b)</strong> [7 marks] A two-stage reciprocating compressor compresses <strong>5 m³/min</strong> of free air from <strong>1.013 bar, 30 °C</strong> to <strong>9 bar</strong>. The clearance ratio is <strong>4%</strong>, polytropic index <strong>n = 1.32</strong>, and mechanical efficiency is <strong>92%</strong>. Calculate: (i) volumetric efficiency, (ii) actual volume of air handled per minute, (iii) indicated work per kg, and (iv) shaft power in kW. Take R = 8.314 J/mol·K and M = 29 g/mol for air.</p>

<h4>Model Solution — Question A</h4>

<h5>Part (a) — Compressor Classification [7 marks]</h5>

<p><strong>Classification (3 marks):</strong></p>
<ul>
<li><strong>A. Positive Displacement (PD):</strong> Air is trapped in a fixed volume which is mechanically reduced. Sub-types:
  <ul>
    <li>(i) Reciprocating (piston-cylinder)</li>
    <li>(ii) Rotary (screw, vane, scroll, lobe)</li>
  </ul>
</li>
<li><strong>B. Dynamic (Turbo):</strong> Air is accelerated to high velocity and the kinetic energy is converted to pressure.
  <ul>
    <li>(i) Centrifugal (radial outflow)</li>
    <li>(ii) Axial flow</li>
  </ul>
</li>
</ul>

<p><strong>Comparison Table (4 marks):</strong></p>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Parameter</th>
<th>Reciprocating</th>
<th>Centrifugal</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>(i) Principle</td>
<td>Piston in cylinder, cyclic</td>
<td>Impeller + diffuser</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>(ii) Pressure</td>
<td>7 – 1000 bar</td>
<td>1 – 50 bar (multi-stage)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>(iii) FAD</td>
<td>0.1 – 100 m³/min</td>
<td>100 – 50,000 m³/min</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>(iv) Isentropic η</td>
<td>55 – 70% (isothermal)</td>
<td>70 – 85% (adiabatic)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>(v) Maintenance</td>
<td>High (rings, valves)</td>
<td>Low (no rubbing contact)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>(vi) Application</td>
<td>High-pressure N₂, O₂, instrument air backup</td>
<td>Large plant air, ethylene crackers, ammonia plants</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<h5>Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]</h5>

<p><strong>Given:</strong> $V_1 = 5$ m³/min, $P_1 = 1.013$ bar, $T_1 = 303.15$ K, $P_2 = 9$ bar, $C = 0.04$, $n = 1.32$, $\eta_m = 0.92$.</p>

<p><strong>(i) Volumetric efficiency</strong> [2 marks]:</p>

<p>$$\eta_v = 1 + C - C \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$$</p>

<p>$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{9}{1.013} = 8.884$$</p>

<p>$$8.884^{1/1.32} = 8.884^{0.7576}$$</p>

<p>Take ln: $\ln(8.884) = 2.184$, multiply by $0.7576$: $1.655$, exponentiate: $e^{1.655} = 5.234$.</p>

<p>$$\eta_v = 1 + 0.04 - 0.04 \times 5.234 = 1.04 - 0.2094 = 0.8306$$</p>

<p>$$\boxed{\eta_v = 83.06\%}$$</p>

<p><strong>(ii) Actual air handled per minute</strong> [1 mark]:</p>

<p>$$V_{actual} = V_1 \times \eta_v = 5 \times 0.8306 = 4.153 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$</p>

<p><strong>(iii) Indicated work per kg</strong> [2 marks]:</p>

<p>$$W = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot \frac{R T_1}{M} \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$</p>

<p>$\frac{n}{n-1} = \frac{1.32}{0.32} = 4.125$</p>

<p>$\frac{RT_1}{M} = \frac{8.314 \times 303.15}{0.029} = 86{,}911$ J/kg</p>

<p>$(n-1)/n = 0.32/1.32 = 0.2424$</p>

<p>$(P_2/P_1)^{0.2424} = 8.884^{0.2424} = e^{0.2424 \times 2.184} = e^{0.5294} = 1.768$</p>

<p>$W_{indicated} = 4.125 \times 86{,}911 \times (1.768 - 1) = 4.125 \times 86{,}911 \times 0.768$</p>

<p>$W_{indicated} = 275{,}290$ J/kg $= 275.29$ kJ/kg</p>

<p><strong>(iv) Shaft power</strong> [2 marks]:</p>

<p>Mass flow rate:</p>

<p>$$\dot{m} = \frac{P_1 V_{actual} M}{R T_1} = \frac{1.013 \times 10^5 \times (4.153/60) \times 0.029}{8.314 \times 303.15}$$</p>

<p>$$\dot{m} = \frac{101{,}300 \times 0.0692 \times 0.029}{2520.4} = \frac{203.3}{2520.4} = 0.0807 \text{ kg/s}$$</p>

<p>Shaft power:</p>

<p>$$P_{shaft} = \frac{\dot{m} \times W_{indicated}}{\eta_m} = \frac{0.0807 \times 275.29}{0.92}$$</p>

<p>$$P_{shaft} = \frac{22.22}{0.92} = 24.15 \text{ kW}$$</p>

<p>$$\boxed{P_{shaft} \approx 24.15 \text{ kW}}$$</p>

<hr>

<h3>Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)</h3>

<p><strong>(a)</strong> [7 marks] Enumerate the major components of an industrial compressed air system in the order of air flow. Explain the function and design considerations of: (i) aftercooler, (ii) air receiver, (iii) desiccant dryer, and (iv) coalescing filter.</p>

<p><strong>(b)</strong> [7 marks] Discuss the major factors affecting the <strong>performance and efficiency</strong> of air compressors. Explain how each of the following influences power consumption: (i) inlet air temperature, (ii) altitude of installation, (iii) humidity, (iv) leakage in the distribution network, (v) fouled intake filters, and (vi) inter-stage cooling effectiveness.</p>

<h4>Model Solution — Question B</h4>

<h5>Part (a) — Compressed Air System Components [7 marks]</h5>

<p><strong>Flow order (1 mark for sequence):</strong><br>
Intake filter → Compressor → Aftercooler → Moisture separator → Receiver → Refrigeration dryer → Desiccant dryer → Particulate filter → Coalescing filter → Activated carbon filter → Pressure regulator → Distribution piping → End users.</p>

<p><strong>Component-wise (6 marks = 1.5 each):</strong></p>

<ol>
<li><strong>Aftercooler</strong> — Reduces discharge air temperature from <strong>~150 °C</strong> to within <strong>~10 °C</strong> of ambient, condensing ~70% of moisture. Types: water-cooled shell-and-tube (industrial), air-cooled finned (small units). Design: outlet temperature ≤ <strong>40 °C</strong>, pressure drop ≤ 0.1 bar.</li>
<li><strong>Air Receiver</strong> — Cylindrical pressure vessel (ASME Sec VIII Div 1) that stores compressed air, dampens pulsations, and provides buffer for peak demands. Sized for <strong>2–5 minutes</strong> of flow at full load. Includes safety relief valve, pressure gauge, drain trap, and manhole.</li>
<li><strong>Desiccant Dryer</strong> — Uses adsorbent material (silica gel, activated alumina, or molecular sieve) to lower dew point to <strong>−40 °C</strong> (ISO 8573-1 Class 2). Twin-tower design with one tower drying while the other regenerates. Mandatory for instrument-quality air.</li>
<li><strong>Coalescing Filter</strong> — Removes liquid water and oil aerosols down to <strong>0.01 micron</strong> with <strong>≥ 99.99%</strong> efficiency. Uses borosilicate microfiber media. Must be preceded by a particulate filter to prevent media fouling.</li>
</ol>

<h5>Part (b) — Factors Affecting Performance & Efficiency [7 marks]</h5>

<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Factor</th>
<th>Effect</th>
<th>Magnitude (Typical)</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><strong>(i) Inlet air temperature</strong></td>
<td>Higher $T_1$ → lower $\rho_1$ → less mass compressed per stroke → more power per kg; also raises discharge $T$</td>
<td>+10 °C inlet → +3–4% power</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>(ii) Altitude</strong></td>
<td>Lower atmospheric $P_1$ → reduced mass flow, lower discharge $P_2$ available</td>
<td>+1000 m → −12% FAD, −8% efficiency</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>(iii) Humidity</strong></td>
<td>Water vapor is non-ideal; high RH increases specific volume, reduces $\eta_v$ and requires extra drying load</td>
<td>+20% RH → −2% efficiency</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>(iv) Piping/leakage</strong></td>
<td>1 mm hole at 7 bar leaks ~<strong>1.5 m³/min</strong> → wasted compression work</td>
<td>20–30% of total air loss in many plants</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>(v) Filter fouling</strong></td>
<td>Increases inlet vacuum (lower $P_1$) → lower $\eta_v$, higher $T_{discharge}$</td>
<td>0.1 bar drop → −3% FAD</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><strong>(vi) Inter-stage cooling</strong></td>
<td>Better cooling → closer to isothermal → lower work; poor cooling → higher stage work and higher discharge $T$</td>
<td>Effective cooling can save 15–20% power</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<p><strong>Synthesis (1 mark):</strong> A well-designed compressed air system can lose <strong>30–50% of input power</strong> to avoidable factors. KTU's emphasis on these factors reflects the <strong>economic and energy audit</strong> perspective mandated by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) in India.</p>

<blockquote>
<p><strong>KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:</strong></p>
<ol>
<li><strong>Wrong mean for intercooler pressure:</strong> Use geometric mean $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$, NOT arithmetic.</li>
<li><strong>Forgetting the mass flow conversion</strong> when power is asked — students use volume flow directly.</li>
<li><strong>Mixing isothermal and adiabatic efficiencies</strong> — for reciprocating, use isothermal; for centrifugal, use isentropic.</li>
<li><strong>Omitting the $\eta_m$ division</strong> for shaft power — indicated work ≠ shaft work.</li>
<li><strong>Wrong $R/M$ value:</strong> Use <strong>287 J/kg·K</strong> for air (or equivalently $8.314/0.029$) — students often use 8.314 alone and lose 1 mark.</li>
<li><strong>Clearance ratio confusion:</strong> Some students write $C = V_s/V_c$ instead of $C = V_c/V_s$. Always $V_c$ in numerator.</li>
<li><strong>No sketches in classification questions:</strong> For 7-mark comparison questions, a labeled block diagram is MANDATORY (minimum 2 marks reserved).</li>
</ol>
</blockquote>

<hr>

<h2>5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember</h2>

<blockquote>
<p><strong>Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2</strong></p>
</blockquote>

<ul>
<li>[x] <strong>Compressors = "4th utility"</strong> in chemical plants; supply at <strong>7–8.5 bar (g)</strong>.</li>
<li>[x] <strong>Two classes:</strong> Positive Displacement (Reciprocating, Screw, Vane, Scroll) and Dynamic (Centrifugal, Axial).</li>
<li>[x] <strong>PD compressors</strong> = trapped volume mechanically reduced; <strong>Dynamic</strong> = kinetic energy converted to pressure.</li>
<li>[x] <strong>Polytropic equation</strong> $PV^n = C$ is the master relation; $n$ varies from 1 (isothermal) to $\gamma$ (adiabatic).</li>
<li>[x] <strong>For air, $\gamma = 1.4$</strong> and $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 287$ J/kg·K.</li>
<li>[x] <strong>Work equations</strong> for isothermal, adiabatic, and polytropic — master all three.</li>
<li>[x] <strong>Isothermal work is THEORETICAL MINIMUM</strong>; adiabatic is the practical worst case.</li>
<li>[x] <strong>Four efficiencies:</strong> Isothermal ($\eta_{iso}$), Adiabatic ($\eta_{ad}$), Mechanical ($\eta_m$), Volumetric ($\eta_v$).</li>
<li>[x] <strong>Volumetric efficiency</strong> $\eta_v = 1 + C - C(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$ — applies to reciprocating ONLY.</li>
<li>[x] <strong>Optimum intermediate pressure</strong> for multi-stage = <strong>geometric mean</strong> of inlet and discharge.</li>
<li>[x] <strong>System components in order:</strong> Filter → Compressor → Aftercooler → Separator → Receiver → Dryer → Filters → Regulator → Piping.</li>
<li>[x] <strong>Dew point targets:</strong> Refrigeration dryer → +3 °C; Desiccant dryer → <strong>−40 °C</strong> (instrument air).</li>
<li>[x] <strong>Altitude penalty:</strong> ~12% FAD loss per 1000 m elevation gain.</li>
<li>[x] <strong>Leakage losses</strong> can be 20–30% of total compressed air — biggest single inefficiency in many plants.</li>
<li>[x] <strong>Inter-stage cooling</strong> effectiveness directly reduces power by 15–20%.</li>
<li>[x] <strong>Reciprocating</strong> = high pressure, low flow, pulsating; <strong>Centrifugal</strong> = high flow, moderate pressure, smooth.</li>
<li>[x] <strong>Oil-free discharge</strong> is mandatory for instrument air and food/pharma applications → centrifugal or oil-free screw.</li>
</ul>

<blockquote>
<p><strong>One-Line Memory Hook (for last-night revision):</strong><br>
<em>"Isothermal = minimum work; Adiabatic = maximum work; Polytropic = real world. Geometric mean for inter-stage; arithmetic mean is WRONG. Clean filter, cool intake, dry air, tight pipes = maximum efficiency."</em></p>
</blockquote>
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
</div>

topics.title: Vacuum pumps: types of vacuum pumps and their performance characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT636 - PROCESS UTILITY AND PIPING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vacuum pumps: types of vacuum pumps and their performance characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **vacuum pump** is a mechanical device that removes gas molecules from a sealed volume in order to leave behind a partial vacuum. In the context of **Process Utility Engineering**, a vacuum pump is classified as a process auxiliary that delivers mechanical energy to a fluid (gas) in order to move it from a lower pressure (suction) to a higher pressure (discharge), specifically when the suction pressure is **sub-atmospheric** (i.e., below **101.325 kPa** or **760 mm Hg / 1 atm**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (High-Yield):** "A vacuum pump is a positive displacement or kinetic energy transfer device capable of withdrawing non-condensable gases and vapor from a process vessel so as to maintain an internal absolute pressure lower than the prevailing atmospheric pressure."

## 1.2 Vacuum Classification by Pressure Range (KTU Standard)

| Vacuum Regime | Pressure Range | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Rough / Coarse Vacuum** | 101.3 kPa → 1.33 kPa (760 → 10 Torr) | Filtration, drying, deaeration |
| **Medium Vacuum** | 1.33 kPa → 0.133 Pa (10 Torr → 10⁻³ Torr) | Distillation, freeze drying |
| **High Vacuum** | 0.133 Pa → 1.33 × 10⁻⁶ Pa (10⁻³ → 10⁻⁸ Torr) | Metallurgy, electron tubes |
| **Ultra-High Vacuum (UHV)** | < 1.33 × 10⁻⁶ Pa (< 10⁻⁸ Torr) | Particle accelerators, space simulation |

> [!NOTE]
> **1 Torr = 1 mm Hg = 133.322 Pa**. The **ultimate vacuum** is the lowest absolute pressure a pump can theoretically achieve against zero leakage and zero vapor load (often limited by the vapor pressure of any sealing liquid, e.g., water at 20 °C = **2.34 kPa**).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Reverse Bicycle Pump":** Imagine turning a bicycle pump *upside down* so that instead of pushing air into a tire, you are *pulling* air out of a sealed jar. The pump's piston creates a low-pressure zone (suction), the higher atmospheric pressure outside the jar pushes gas inward through the pump's inlet, and that gas is expelled into the room. A vacuum pump does exactly this — it uses mechanical work to repeatedly expand a sealed cavity, draw in a small bolus of process gas, compress it, and dump it to atmosphere. The *performance characteristic* that matters is how *much* gas can be removed per unit time (pumping speed, **S**, in **m³/h**) and how *low* a pressure it can reach (ultimate vacuum, **Pᵤ**, in **Pa** or **Torr**).

**Why it matters in chemical plants:** Almost every downstream unit operation in petroleum, pharmaceutical, and food processing — vacuum distillation, vacuum drying, molecular distillation, freeze concentration, solvent recovery, deodorization — depends on a reliable vacuum utility. Selection of the wrong pump type (e.g., using a wet liquid-ring pump where a dry pump is required) causes product contamination, high operating cost, and frequent maintenance shutdowns.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Classification of Vacuum Pumps

KTU 2024 categorizes vacuum pumps into **four families** based on the *transfer mechanism* of momentum, mass, or capture.

### Family A — Positive Displacement Pumps
Trapped volumes of gas are mechanically isolated, compressed, and expelled. These dominate the **rough to medium vacuum** range.

- **A.1 Reciprocating (Piston) Pump**
- **A.2 Rotary Vane Pump**
- **A.3 Rotary Piston (Roots-type) Booster Pump**
- **A.4 Liquid Ring Vacuum Pump**

### Family B — Momentum Transfer (Kinetic) Pumps
Used to compress vapor/gas using a high-velocity fluid stream.

- **B.1 Steam Jet Ejector (SJE)**
- **B.2 Liquid Jet Ejector**
- **B.3 Oil Diffusion Pump**

### Family C — Entrapment / Capture Pumps
Gas molecules are *bound* to a surface rather than compressed. These reach the **highest vacuums**.

- **C.1 Cryogenic (Cold Trap) Pump**
- **C.2 Sorption Pump (Zeolite / Activated Charcoal)**
- **C.3 Ion (Getter) Pump**

### Family D — Hybrid / Combination Systems
Two or more pumps coupled in series to extend the operating envelope.
- **D.1 Rotary + Diffusion**
- **D.2 Steam Ejector + Liquid Ring ("ejector–liquid ring" hybrid)**

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Theory — Reciprocating Piston Pump

1. **Suction Stroke:** Piston moves outward → sealed cylinder volume ↑ → cylinder pressure drops below process pressure → inlet valve (suction) opens, gas rushes in.
2. **Compression Stroke:** Piston moves inward → cylinder volume ↓ → pressure rises above discharge pressure → outlet valve opens, gas is expelled to atmosphere.
3. **Valve Action:** Spring-loaded disc or poppet valves are *passive*; they open only when cylinder pressure exceeds discharge or falls below suction. This creates the iconic "two-stroke" volumetric cycle.
4. **Leakage Control:** Clearance volume at the end of the stroke (typically 5–10 % of swept volume) is filled with the *expanded* residual gas, which limits the *compression ratio* and therefore the *ultimate vacuum*.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Theory — Steam Jet Ejector

1. High-pressure motive steam (typically **0.6 – 1.7 MPa** saturated) expands through a **converging–diverging nozzle**, reaching supersonic velocity (Mach 2 – 4).
2. The high-velocity steam jet creates a *low static pressure* region at the **suction chamber** (per Bernoulli's principle).
3. Process vapor is entrained (entrained — dragged along) by the high-momentum stream.
4. The two-phase mixture passes through a **diffuser**, where velocity is converted back to pressure, and is discharged against a higher back-pressure (e.g., a downstream condenser or another ejector stage).
5. A **condenser** placed between ejector stages condenses the motive steam, drastically reducing the gas load on the next stage and improving overall efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why multi-staging?** A single-stage steam ejector typically achieves only 100 – 5 mbar. By placing **N stages in series with intercondensers**, the practical limit drops to 0.1 mbar (3 stages) or 0.01 mbar (4–5 stages), at the cost of higher steam and cooling-water consumption.

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Operational Theory — Liquid Ring Pump

1. A rotor (with impeller blades) is mounted *eccentrically* inside a cylindrical casing partially filled with a **service liquid** (usually water, sometimes oil or a solvent).
2. As the rotor spins, centrifugal force throws the liquid outward, forming a *liquid ring* on the casing wall.
3. The eccentric geometry causes the gap between the impeller tip and the liquid ring to *alternately expand and contract*, creating moving cells of trapped gas.
4. On the *expanding side* (suction), gas is drawn in through a port. On the *contracting side* (compression), gas is compressed and discharged through a port.
5. The service liquid absorbs heat of compression and re-seals the clearance, giving the pump excellent *isothermal compression* characteristics — ideal for handling wet, corrosive, or particulate-laden vapors.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are mandatory for KTU numericals. Memorize units and boundary conditions.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = \frac{dV}{dt}\Big\vert_{P}$ | Pumping speed (volume flow at pressure P) | m³/h, L/s | Vacuum pump selection |
| 2 | $Q = S \cdot P$ | Throughput (mass-flow equivalent at low P) | Pa·m³/s | Sizing the pump |
| 3 | $P_{u} = P_{vap,\,seal\,fluid}$ | Ultimate vacuum limit (oil/water sealed) | Pa, Torr | Reciprocating / liquid ring |
| 4 | $R = \frac{P_{d}}{P_{s}}$ | Compression ratio (discharge/suction) | dimensionless | Booster sizing |
| 5 | $\tau = \frac{V}{S}$ | Pump-down time to reach pressure P | s, min | Process design |
| 6 | $P(t) = P_{0}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{S}{V}t\right)$ | Pressure decay (isothermal, leak-free) | Pa | Process dynamics |
| 7 | $\eta_{vol} = 1 - c\!\left(\dfrac{P_{d}}{P_{s}}\right)^{1/n}$ | Volumetric efficiency (n = 1.3 for adiabatic) | dimensionless | Reciprocating |
| 8 | $W_{steam} = \dot{m}_{steam}\cdot\Delta h_{nozzle}$ | Steam consumption per stage | kW | SJE design |
| 9 | $NPSH_{a} \geq NPSH_{r} + safety$ | Net Positive Suction Head (liquid ring) | m | Cavitation check |
| 10 | $P_{back} = P_{cond} + \Delta P_{piping}$ | Maximum back-pressure on ejector | Pa | SJE stage count |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical pipe in tables is forbidden** in this KTU note system. I have used the LaTeX `\vert` symbol in formula 1 and `\dfrac` for clarity. When you write these in your answer script, prefer `\vert` for the "evaluated at" symbol.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical:** Solvent recovery, vacuum tray dryers, rotary cone vacuum dryers — all use **liquid ring** or **oil-sealed rotary vane** pumps.
- **Petrochemical:** Vacuum distillation of crude oil (vacuum tower at 5–50 mbar) uses **3- to 5-stage steam ejectors with intercondensers**.
- **Semiconductor & Solar:** Production of photovoltaic silicon requires **dry pumps + Roots boosters** to avoid oil backstreaming.
- **Food & Dairy:** Freeze drying of instant coffee, fruit — **steam ejector + ice condenser** combinations are common.
- **Steel & Metallurgy:** Vacuum degassing (VD, VOD) of molten steel uses **steam ejector trains** with multi-stage condensers.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pump-Down Time for an Isothermal Chamber

**Problem Statement:** A vacuum oven has internal volume **V = 0.5 m³**. A rotary vane pump with constant speed **S = 50 m³/h** is used to evacuate it from atmospheric pressure **P₀ = 101.3 kPa** down to a process pressure **P_f = 1.0 kPa**. The chamber is leak-free and temperature is held at 20 °C. Derive the time required.

**Derivation — Step by Step:**

Step 1: Apply the **Ideal Gas Law** differential form. For an isothermal, constant-temperature system, the mass remaining is proportional to pressure.

$$PV = nRT \quad \Rightarrow \quad P = \frac{nRT}{V}$$

Step 2: Differentiate with respect to time (T and V constant):

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{RT}{V}\cdot\frac{dn}{dt}$$

Step 3: The rate of *moles leaving* the chamber equals the moles drawn in by the pump. By definition of pumping speed, the volumetric flow at pressure P is S, so the molar flow leaving is:

$$\frac{dn}{dt} = -\frac{S \cdot P}{RT}$$

Step 4: Substitute Step 3 into Step 2:

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{RT}{V}\cdot\left(-\frac{S \cdot P}{RT}\right) = -\frac{S}{V}\,P$$

Step 5: This is a first-order linear ODE. Separate variables and integrate from P₀ to P_f:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{S}{V}\,dt$$

$$\int_{P_0}^{P_f}\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{S}{V}\int_{0}^{t}dt$$

Step 6: Evaluate the integrals:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_f}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{S}{V}\,t$$

Step 7: Solve explicitly for time:

$$t = \frac{V}{S}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{P_0}{P_f}\right)$$

Step 8: Substitute numerical values (use **V = 0.5 m³**, **S = 50 m³/h**, **P₀ = 101.3 kPa**, **P_f = 1.0 kPa**):

$$t = \frac{0.5}{50}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{101.3}{1.0}\right)\text{ h}$$

$$t = 0.01 \times \ln(101.3)\text{ h}$$

$$t = 0.01 \times 4.618\text{ h}$$

$$\boxed{t = 0.04618\text{ h} = 2.77\text{ min}}$$

**Interpretation:** Even with a relatively small 0.5 m³ chamber, a 50 m³/h pump takes only ~2.8 minutes to reach 1 kPa. However, real systems suffer from **outgassing**, **leakage**, and **declining pumping speed at low pressure** (pump speed typically falls as P → Pᵤ), so a *safety factor of 1.5–2.0* should be applied.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Volumetric Efficiency of a Reciprocating Pump

**Problem Statement:** A single-stage reciprocating vacuum pump has a swept volume **V_s = 0.005 m³/stroke**, clearance fraction **c = 0.06**, discharge pressure **P_d = 101.3 kPa**, and suction pressure **P_s = 5 kPa**. Polytropic exponent **n = 1.3** (typical for diatomic gas with heat loss). Find the volumetric efficiency.

**Derivation — Step by Step:**

Step 1: Define volumetric efficiency as the ratio of *effective* swept volume to the *geometric* swept volume:

$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{V_{effective}}{V_s}$$

Step 2: The effective volume is reduced by the **re-expansion of clearance gas** at the start of the next suction stroke. The clearance volume is **V_c = c·V_s**.

Step 3: At end of compression stroke, clearance gas is at discharge pressure **P_d**. During the next suction stroke, this gas re-expands from **P_d** down to **P_s** following a polytropic law:

$$P_d \cdot V_c^{\,n} = P_s \cdot V_e^{\,n}$$

Step 4: Solve for the re-expanded volume **V_e**:

$$V_e = V_c\left(\frac{P_d}{P_s}\right)^{1/n}$$

Step 5: The effective suction volume per stroke is:

$$V_{effective} = V_s - (V_e - V_c) = V_s - V_c\!\left[\left(\frac{P_d}{P_s}\right)^{1/n} - 1\right]$$

Step 6: Divide by V_s and substitute **V_c = c·V_s**:

$$\eta_{vol} = 1 - c\!\left[\left(\frac{P_d}{P_s}\right)^{1/n} - 1\right]$$

Step 7: Plug in numbers — P_d / P_s = 101.3 / 5 = 20.26. The exponent is 1 / 1.3 = 0.7692.

$$\left(20.26\right)^{0.7692}$$

Step 8: Use logarithms. ln(20.26) = 3.009. Multiply by 0.7692:

$$3.009 \times 0.7692 = 2.3145$$

Step 9: Exponentiate:

$$e^{2.3145} = 10.12$$

Step 10: Therefore:

$$\eta_{vol} = 1 - 0.06 \times (10.12 - 1) = 1 - 0.06 \times 9.12$$

$$\eta_{vol} = 1 - 0.5472$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{vol} = 0.4528 \;\approx\; 45.3\,\%}$$

**Engineering Insight:** A 45 % volumetric efficiency is *typical* for a single-stage reciprocating pump. By adding a **second stage** in series, the effective compression ratio per stage is roughly √20.26 ≈ 4.5, raising η_vol above 80 % and pushing the ultimate vacuum from ~5 kPa down to ~0.5 kPa. This is why **most production reciprocating vacuum pumps are two-stage**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Number of Steam Ejector Stages

**Problem Statement:** A vacuum distillation column operates at **P = 5 mbar (500 Pa)**. Available cooling water is at 25 °C, and the discharge end of the ejector train must vent to atmosphere at **P_back ≈ 80 mbar** (allowing for condenser pressure drop). For a typical single-stage ejector compression ratio of **R_max = 7**, find the minimum number of stages.

**Derivation — Step by Step:**

Step 1: Total required compression ratio:

$$R_{total} = \frac{P_{back}}{P_{suction}} = \frac{80\text{ mbar}}{5\text{ mbar}} = 16$$

Step 2: For N equal stages, the per-stage ratio is:

$$R_{stage} = (R_{total})^{1/N}$$

Step 3: For a single stage (N = 1), R_stage = 16, but a single ejector can practically deliver only R_max = 7. Therefore:

$$7 < 16 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N \geq 2$$

Step 4: Try N = 2:

$$R_{stage} = \sqrt{16} = 4$$

Step 5: Since 4 < 7, the requirement is satisfied. However, the **inter-condenser** between stages typically operates at ~30 mbar, which is the *discharge* of the first stage and the *suction* of the second.

Step 6: For N = 3 (often used when condensable vapor load is high):

$$R_{stage} = (16)^{1/3} = 2.52$$

$$\boxed{N_{min} = 2 \text{ stages (3 is preferred for higher steam economy)}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Adding stages reduces steam consumption *per kg of vapor handled* because the inter-condenser removes the bulk of the motive steam before the second stage, leaving only non-condensables to be compressed. A 3-stage train typically uses **30–50 % less steam** than a 2-stage train for the same suction pressure.

## 3.4 Analytical Performance Matrix — Comparing Pump Types

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the **single most important comparison table** for KTU 2024 viva and 14-mark questions. Memorize it.

| Criterion | Rotary Vane | Liquid Ring | Steam Ejector | Cryo / Sorption | Reciprocating |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ultimate Vacuum (Pa)** | 0.5 – 1 | 3 000 – 30 000 | 100 – 0.1 (multi-stage) | 10⁻⁷ – 10⁻¹¹ | 100 – 1 000 |
| **Pumping Speed (m³/h)** | 10 – 600 | 50 – 25 000 | 5 – 5 000 | 1 000 – 50 000 | 5 – 500 |
| **Can Handle Wet Vapor?** | No (oil damage) | **Yes** (best) | Yes (with condenser) | No | Limited |
| **Can Handle Corrosive Gas?** | Limited | **Yes** (with right liquid) | Yes (SS body) | Limited | No |
| **Can Handle Particulates?** | No | **Yes** | Limited | No | No |
| **Capital Cost** | Low–Medium | Medium | Low (no moving parts) | High | Medium |
| **Operating Cost** | Medium (oil change) | Medium (seal liquid) | High (steam + CW) | Very High (LN₂) | Medium |
| **Noise / Vibration** | High | Low | Low | Low | Very High |
| **Environmental Risk** | Oil backstreaming | Liquid discharge | Thermal pollution | None | None |
| **Best For** | Clean lab vacuum | Wet / dirty process | Petrochem vacuum tower | UHV labs | High ΔP, intermittent |

## 3.5 Worked Example — Required Pumping Speed from a Process Material Balance

**Given:** A vacuum batch dryer is loaded with a solvent that releases **50 kg/h of water vapor** at operating pressure **P = 5 kPa** and **T = 60 °C** (333 K). Required process pressure must be maintained within ±5 % of setpoint. Calculate the **minimum pumping speed** (in m³/h) for the pump.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

Step 1: Convert mass flow to molar flow. Molar mass of water M = 18 kg/kmol.

$$\dot{n} = \frac{\dot{m}}{M} = \frac{50}{18} = 2.778\text{ kmol/h}$$

Step 2: Convert kmol/h to mol/s (1 kmol/h = 1000/3600 mol/s = 0.2778 mol/s):

$$\dot{n} = 2.778 \times 0.2778 = 0.7717\text{ mol/s}$$

Step 3: Apply Ideal Gas Law to find the volumetric flow at the suction conditions (P = 5000 Pa, T = 333 K, R = 8.314 J/mol·K):

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{n}RT}{P}$$

Step 4: Substitute values:

$$\dot{V} = \frac{0.7717 \times 8.314 \times 333}{5000}$$

$$\dot{V} = \frac{2136.6}{5000}$$

$$\dot{V} = 0.4273\text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Step 5: Convert to m³/h (multiply by 3600):

$$\dot{V} = 0.4273 \times 3600 = 1538.4\text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

Step 6: Apply a safety factor of 1.25 to account for leakage, temperature variation, and pump-speed degradation at low pressure:

$$S_{design} = 1.25 \times 1538.4$$

$$\boxed{S_{design} \approx 1923\text{ m}^3/\text{h}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** A 2 000 m³/h liquid-ring or two-stage rotary vane pump would be specified. The safety factor in Step 6 is *non-negotiable* in KTU design problems — failing to include it is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction** by the examiner.

> [!TIP]
> **Reading this curve:** The flat top region is where the pump delivers its *rated* speed. Below the knee, the speed falls because the pump can no longer complete a full compression cycle in the available time/clearance. At the ultimate vacuum plateau, throughput is zero — the pump is in equilibrium with its seal fluid's vapor pressure.
