# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
**Define the term "capture velocity" as applied to local exhaust ventilation. State typical values for dust-generating and solvent-vapour operations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Capture velocity is the air velocity at the hood face (or at a defined distance from the source) required to overcome the opposing currents and reliably draw the contaminant into the hood inlet.
>
> **Typical values (1.5 marks):**
> - Dusts with low density (plastic, wood): **0.5 – 1.0 m/s**
> - Dusts with high density (metal, sandblasting): **1.0 – 2.5 m/s**
> - Solvent vapours at room temperature: **0.5 – 1.0 m/s**
> - Hot process emissions / welding fumes: **1.0 – 2.0 m/s**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**Differentiate between "general ventilation" and "local exhaust ventilation" with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | General Ventilation | Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Dilution of contaminants in the entire room | Capture at or near source |
| Air volume required | High (entire room air change) | Low (concentrated stream) |
| Energy use | High | Low (more efficient) |
| Example 1 | Supply fans in a control room | Snorkel hood at welding station |
| Example 2 | Roof extractor in a paint storage shed | Side-slot hood on a chrome plating tank |

*(1.5 marks for differentiation table; 1.5 marks for examples.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **🔥 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls that cost marks:**
> - **Forgetting units** in fan power calculation — direct 1-mark cut.
> - **Confusing static pressure with velocity pressure** — write the distinction explicitly.
> - **Ignoring hood entry loss coefficient** (Kₑ ≈ 0.25 for flanged, 0.93 for unflanged round) — fan undersized.
> - **Using GEP formula with Q in g/s instead of kg/hr** — height comes out absurdly wrong. Always convert!
> - **Not mentioning stack gas temperature effect on density** — plume rise calculation goes wrong.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the components and working of a **local exhaust ventilation (LEV) system** for a process plant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A grinding machine generates **5 g/s of metallic dust**. The hood is a **flanged circular hood of diameter 0.40 m**, placed **0.50 m away from the grinding point**. Design the duct system and calculate:
  - (i) The minimum capture velocity required and the resulting volumetric flow rate. **(3 marks)**
  - (ii) Duct diameter for a transport velocity of **18 m/s**. **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) Fan static power required assuming total system resistance = **900 Pa** and fan efficiency = **60 %**. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Components of LEV System (7 marks):**

A Local Exhaust Ventilation system consists of the following components arranged in series:

1. **Hood (Capture element)** — Flanged, unflanged, canopy, slot, or enclosing type. Designed to generate capture velocity sufficient to overcome contaminant dispersion velocity.
2. **Ductwork (Transport element)** — Galvanized iron (GI) or stainless steel (SS 316 for corrosive) ducts. Sized using the **equal friction method** to maintain a constant pressure drop per metre.
3. **Air-cleaning device** — Cyclone, bag filter, wet scrubber, electrostatic precipitator (ESP), or activated carbon adsorber.
4. **Fan / Blower** — Centrifugal (high-pressure, dust-laden) or axial (low-pressure, clean air).
5. **Stack (Discharge element)** — Vertical chimney with sufficient height (per CPCB GEP formula) and exit velocity (15–30 m/s) for proper dispersion.

*Working principle:* Contaminant released at the source is captured by the hood due to induced airflow → transported through ducts (kept above transport velocity to prevent settling) → cleaned in the air-cleaning device → pushed by the fan up the stack → discharged to atmosphere at safe elevation.

*(Sketch: Refer SECTION 4.1 functional flow diagram — marks 4, 5, 6, 7 awarded for completeness of diagram with labels.)*

---

**(b) Quantitative Design (7 marks):**

**(i) Capture velocity & flow rate:**

For a flanged hood, a typical capture velocity for grinding dust is **V_c = 1.5 m/s** (per ACGIH industrial ventilation manual).

Using Dalla Valle's centerline velocity equation:

$$
V_x = \frac{V_c \cdot 10 D}{10 X + D}
$$

We want $V_x = V_c$ (capture velocity) at $X = 0.50$ m, so we solve for the face velocity required:

$$
V_{face} = V_x \cdot \frac{10 X + D}{10 D}
$$

Plugging $V_x = 1.5$ m/s, $X = 0.50$ m, $D = 0.40$ m:

$$
V_{face} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{10 \times 0.50 + 0.40}{10 \times 0.40} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{5.40}{4.00} = 1.5 \times 1.35 = 2.025 \; \text{m/s}
$$

Volumetric flow rate:

$$
Q = V_{face} \cdot A_{hood} = V_{face} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

$$
Q = 2.025 \cdot \frac{\pi \times (0.40)^2}{4} = 2.025 \cdot 0.1257 = 0.2545 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

*(3 marks — 1 for capture velocity choice, 1 for algebra, 1 for numerical answer with units.)*

**(ii) Duct diameter:**

$$
D_{duct} = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi V_t}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.2545}{\pi \times 18}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.018}{56.55}} = \sqrt{0.01800} = 0.1342 \; \text{m}
$$

Select standard duct size: **150 mm (6 inch)** GI duct.

*(2 marks.)*

**(iii) Fan static power:**

$$
P_{fan} = \frac{Q \cdot \Delta P_{total}}{\eta_{fan}} = \frac{0.2545 \times 900}{0.60} = 381.75 \; \text{W}
$$

Select a standard motor: **0.5 hp (≈ 373 W)** with safety margin → use **0.75 hp motor**.

*(2 marks.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**(a)** Discuss the **design criteria for stack height** as per **CPCB Good Engineering Practice (GEP)**. A boiler stack emits flue gas at **1500 kg/hr** of total pollutants. Calculate the GEP stack height. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industrial paint spray booth has dimensions **6 m × 4 m × 3 m**. The booth is to be maintained at **negative pressure** with **20 air changes per hour**. The duct has equivalent length **30 m**, diameter **0.35 m**, and contains **two 90° elbows**. Compute:
  - (i) Volumetric exhaust flow rate in m³/s. **(2 marks)**
  - (ii) Air velocity in the duct. **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) Friction loss assuming f = 0.018 and air density = 1.2 kg/m³. **(2 marks)**
  - (iv) Total elbow loss assuming K = 0.30 per elbow. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) GEP Stack Design (7 marks):**

**Design criteria for stack height:**

1. **Good Engineering Practice (GEP) height** must be at least the height calculated by the CPCB empirical formula.
2. **Height must be ≥ 1.5 × the height of the tallest building within a 5× stack-height radius** (downwash avoidance).
3. **Minimum exit velocity** ≥ **15 m/s** to prevent downwash by ambient wind.
4. **Stack diameter** should be such that the volumetric flow at exit velocity matches the design flow.
5. **Materials of construction** must resist flue gas corrosion (SS 316L for SO₂-containing gases; refractory lining for > 400 °C).
6. **Sampling port** at a distance ≥ **8 stack diameters** downstream and ≥ **2 diameters** upstream of any flow disturbance.
7. **Continuous Emission Monitoring System (CEMS)** for pollutants crossing regulatory thresholds.

**Calculation:**

Given $Q = 1500$ kg/hr (already in kg/hr, no conversion needed):

$$
H_{GEP} = 14 \cdot Q^{0.333} = 14 \times (1500)^{1/3}
$$

$$
(1500)^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{1500} = 11.447
$$

$$
H_{GEP} = 14 \times 11.447 = 160.3 \; \text{m}
$$

*(7 marks — 4 for criteria + 1 for formula + 2 for numerical.)*

---

**(b) Paint Booth Exhaust Design (7 marks):**

**(i) Volumetric flow rate:**

Room volume:

$$
V_{room} = 6 \times 4 \times 3 = 72 \; \text{m}^3
$$

$$
Q = \text{ACH} \times V_{room} / 3600 = \frac{20 \times 72}{3600} = \frac{1440}{3600} = 0.4 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

*(2 marks.)*

**(ii) Air velocity in duct:**

$$
A_{duct} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.35)^2}{4} = 0.0962 \; \text{m}^2
$$

$$
V_{duct} = \frac{Q}{A_{duct}} = \frac{0.4}{0.0962} = 4.158 \; \text{m/s}
$$

*(2 marks.)*

**(iii) Friction loss:**

$$
\Delta P_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}
$$

Velocity pressure:

$$
\frac{\rho V^2}{2} = \frac{1.2 \times (4.158)^2}{2} = \frac{1.2 \times 17.29}{2} = 10.37 \; \text{Pa}
$$

L/D ratio:

$$
\frac{L}{D} = \frac{30}{0.35} = 85.71
$$

Total friction loss:

$$
\Delta P_f = 0.018 \times 85.71 \times 10.37 = 16.0 \; \text{Pa}
$$

*(2 marks.)*

**(iv) Elbow losses:**

For 2 elbows, each with K = 0.30:

$$
\Delta P_{elbows} = 2 \times K \times \frac{\rho V^2}{2} = 2 \times 0.30 \times 10.37 = 6.22 \; \text{Pa}
$$

*(1 mark.)*

**Total system pressure drop:**

$$
\Delta P_{total} = 16.0 + 6.22 = 22.2 \; \text{Pa} \quad \text{(+ hood entry loss + stack loss to be added in detailed design)}
$$

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Must-Remember Points (Last-minute revision before exam):**

- ✅ **Ventilation** = general air replacement (dilution); **Exhaust** = controlled contaminant removal.
- ✅ **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** is **always preferred** over general ventilation when source is localized and contaminant is toxic.
- ✅ **Capture velocity** depends on contaminant type — **0.5–2.5 m/s**; flanged hoods have lower entry loss than unflanged.
- ✅ **Transport velocity** in ducts must exceed settling velocity of particles — typically **10–25 m/s**.
- ✅ **Equal friction method** is the industry standard for duct sizing; **static regain method** is for HVAC.
- ✅ **Fan Laws** — flow ∝ N, pressure ∝ N², power ∝ N³.
- ✅ **Darcy-Weisbach equation** $\Delta P_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ — used for straight duct friction.
- ✅ **CPCB GEP Stack Height** = $H = 14 \cdot Q^{0.333}$ where Q is in **kg/hr**.
- ✅ **Stack exit velocity** = **15–30 m/s** to prevent downwash.
- ✅ **Air Changes Per Hour (ACH)** = 6 to 30 typical for process plants.
- ✅ Always convert **g/s → kg/hr** before applying GEP formula.
- ✅ Hood entry loss coefficient Kₑ = **0.25 (flanged)** vs **0.93 (unflanged)**.
- ✅ Cyclones handle **> 10 µm** dust; bag filters handle **1–10 µm**; ESPs handle **sub-micron**.
- ✅ **LEL safety margin** — design for vapor concentration **< 25 % LEL** in workspace.
- ✅ Always include **make-up air** provision — exhausting without make-up creates negative pressure that fights the fan.

> **End of KTU Module 4 Notes — Exhaust and Ventilation.**

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Pipe? (KTU Formal Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **pipe** is a hollow cylindrical conduit, typically manufactured from metal, plastic, or composite materials, used primarily for the conveyance of fluids (liquids, gases, slurries, or two-phase mixtures) under pressure or by gravity. The complete engineered system comprising pipes, fittings, flanges, gaskets, valves, and supports is collectively termed a **Piping System** (or *Piping*).

In the context of **Chemical Process Industries (CPI)**, piping is often called the **"arterial system of the plant"** because it transports raw materials, intermediates, utilities (steam, water, air), and finished products between unit operations.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Plant is a Human Body**
> - **Pipes** → Blood vessels (arteries & veins)
> - **Pumps/Compressors** → The heart
> - **Valves** → Gatekeepers (control flow rate and direction)
> - **Vessels/Reactors** → Organs (where processing happens)
> - **Pipe Supports** → Skeleton (holds everything in place)
> - **Insulation** → Skin (protects from environment)

Just as a blocked artery can cause systemic failure, a poorly designed or undersized pipe can lead to pressure drop failures, cavitation, water hammer, or catastrophic rupture in a chemical plant.

---

## 1.3 Why is Piping Engineering Critical?

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:**
> Piping typically constitutes **30% to 60% of the total capital cost** of a process plant. Failure to select the correct pipe material, schedule, or size leads to:
> 1. Safety hazards (toxic leaks, fires, explosions)
> 2. Excessive pumping/compression costs
> 3. Pressure drop failures
> 4. Frequent maintenance shutdowns

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Expected)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Nominal Pipe Size (NPS)** | A dimensionless designator (e.g., NPS 2, NPS 6) used to identify pipe diameter |
| **Outside Diameter (OD)** | The fixed external diameter of the pipe |
| **Inside Diameter (ID)** | OD − 2 × (wall thickness) |
| **Schedule (Sch.)** | A number that defines wall thickness |
| **Service** | The fluid being transported (e.g., steam service, acid service) |
| **Bore** | Another term for internal diameter |
| **Allowable Stress (S)** | Maximum permissible hoop stress at design temperature |
| **Corrosion Allowance (C.A.)** | Extra thickness added to account for material loss over plant life |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Pipes (KTU 2024 Module 4)

Pipes are classified on **four major bases**:

### A. Classification by Material of Construction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification:**

| Class | Material | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Carbon Steel (CS)** | ASTM A106, A53, A333 | Steam, water, oil, non-corrosive hydrocarbons |
| **Stainless Steel (SS)** | SS304, SS316 (Austenitic) | Corrosive chemicals, food, pharma |
| **Alloy Steel** | 5Cr-0.5Mo, 9Cr-1Mo (Chrome-Moly) | High-temperature/pressure (boilers, FCC units) |
| **Cast Iron** | Gray iron | Underground water mains, low-pressure |
| **Ductile Iron** | Spheroidal graphite iron | Municipal water, sewage |
| **Copper & Alloys** | Cu, Cu-Ni (90/10, 70/30) | Heat exchangers, marine, refrigeration |
| **PVC / CPVC** | Polyvinyl chloride | Cold water, dilute chemicals, drainage |
| **PP / PVDF / PTFE** | Polypropylene, Kynar, Teflon | Highly corrosive acids, HF |
| **FRP / GRE** | Fibreglass Reinforced Plastic | Chemical plants, seawater |
| **Concrete / PSC** | Reinforced concrete | Large water mains, cooling water |
| **HDPE / MDPE** | High/Mid Density Polyethylene | Gas distribution, water supply |
| **Lined Pipes** | CS pipe lined with PTFE/glass/rubber | Severe corrosion (HCl, H₂SO₄) |

---

### B. Classification by Method of Manufacture

| Type | Manufacturing Method | Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| **Seamless (SMLS)** | Hot/cold piercing of billets | No weld seam; highest pressure rating; uniform strength |
| **Electric Resistance Welded (ERW)** | Cold-formed strip with longitudinal weld | Lower cost; limited to moderate pressures |
| **Electric Fusion Welded (EFW)** | Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) for large diameters | Used for NPS 16 and above |
| **Spiral Welded** | Helical seam | Large-diameter water pipes |
| **Fabricated / Welded** | Plate rolled and welded | Special sizes and thicknesses |
| **Cast** | Sand casting | Fittings, valves (not used for long runs) |
| **Drawn / Extruded** | Cold-drawing or extrusion | Small-bore instrumentation tubing |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** "**S**eamless = **S**uperior pressure; ERW = **E**conomical for low pressure"

---

### C. Classification by Service / Function

1. **Process Pipes** — Carry raw materials & products
2. **Utility Pipes** — Steam, cooling water, air, nitrogen
3. **Drain / Vent Pipes** — Remove condensate, vent gases
4. **Sample Lines** — For analytical instruments
5. **Fire-fighting Pipes** — Water spray, deluge systems
6. **Slurry / Solid Pipes** — Catalyst, ash (often with special linings)

---

### D. Classification by Operating Pressure (IS 2825 / ASME B31.3)

| Class | Pressure Range | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | Very low (vacuum–0.7 bar) | Atmospheric tanks, vent lines |
| **Class B** | Low (0.7–10 bar) | Utility water, low-pressure air |
| **Class C** | Medium (10–30 bar) | Steam, general process |
| **Class D** | High (30–100 bar) | High-pressure reactors, boilers |
| **Class E** | Very high (>100 bar) | Compressors, critical services |

---

## 2.2 Pipe Sizing (Concept & Formulae)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Continuity Equation (MUST MEMORISE for KTU):**
> The volumetric flow rate through a pipe equals the cross-sectional area times the fluid velocity.

$$Q = A \times v = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D_i^2 \times v$$

Solving for the inside diameter (D_i):

$$D_i = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times Q}{\pi \times v}}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $v$ = Fluid velocity (m/s)
- $D_i$ = Internal diameter (m)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area (m²)

### Recommended Velocity Ranges (KTU Board Favourite)

| Service | Velocity (m/s) |
|---|---|
| Liquids (pumping) | 1.0 – 3.0 |
| Liquids (gravity) | 0.5 – 1.5 |
| Saturated steam | 20 – 40 |
| Superheated steam | 30 – 60 |
| Compressed air | 10 – 20 |
| Natural gas | 10 – 20 |
| Vent / drain | 5 – 10 |
| Boiler feed water | 1.5 – 4.0 |

### Optimization Criterion: Optimum Pipe Diameter

> [!NOTE]
> **Economic Balance Principle:**
> - **Smaller diameter** → Higher velocity → Higher pumping cost, higher pressure drop
> - **Larger diameter** → Higher pipe cost, but lower pumping cost
> - **Optimum diameter** = point of minimum **Total Annual Cost** = Pipe cost + Pumping cost

---

## 2.3 Pipe Wall Thickness Calculation (KTU Critical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Barlow's Formula (Straight Pipe under Internal Pressure — Most Asked):**

For a thin-walled cylindrical pipe under internal pressure, the **hoop stress** governs:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \times D_o}{2 \times t}$$

Rearranging for required wall thickness:

$$t = \frac{P \times D_o}{2 \times S \times E - 1.2 \times P} + C$$

Where:
- $t$ = Required wall thickness (mm)
- $P$ = Internal design pressure (MPa or psi)
- $D_o$ = Outside diameter (mm or in)
- $S$ = Allowable stress of material at design temperature
- $E$ = Longitudinal joint efficiency (1.0 for seamless, 0.85 for ERW)
- $C$ = Corrosion allowance + mechanical allowances

### ASME B31.3 Variants (Use these for the exam)

**For seamless pipe (no corrosion allowance) — straight pipe:**

$$t_m = \frac{P \times D}{2 \times S \times E + 0.8 \times P}$$

**For welded pipe:**

$$t_m = \frac{P \times D}{2 \times S \times E - 0.8 \times P}$$

Then **add corrosion allowance**:

$$t_{nom} = t_m + C.A.$$

The required schedule number is then selected so that:

$$t_{schedule} \geq t_{nom}$$

---

## 2.4 Schedule Number (Sch.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Definition (ANSI B36.10 / B36.19):**

$$Sch = \frac{P_{rated} \times 1000}{S}$$

But practically, **Schedule is a fixed set of standardized wall thicknesses** for each NPS value. Common schedules are:

- **Sch 5, 10, 20, 30, 40 (STD), 40S, 60, 80 (XS), 80S, 100, 120, 140, 160, XXS**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** "**STD = Schedule 40, XS = Schedule 80**" — These are default selections in CS/SS for most process services.

### Key Schedule Rules (KTU Favourite)

1. **Sch 5S, 10S, 40S, 80S** — Used for **Stainless Steel** (light series)
2. **Sch 10, 20, 40, 80, 120, 160** — Used for **Carbon Steel** (heavy series)
3. **Sch 40 = STD (Standard)** for all NPS values
4. **Sch 80 = XS (Extra Strong)** for all NPS values
5. **Sch 160 = XXS (Double Extra Strong)** for NPS ≤ 12

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (One-Page Revision Table)

> [!TIP]
> Save this table — questions in Part A & Part B are often direct formula applications.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pipe sizing | $D_i = \sqrt{4Q/(\pi v)}$ | $D_i$ = ID, $Q$ = flow, $v$ = velocity | m, m³/s, m/s |
| 2 | Continuity | $Q = A v = (\pi/4) D_i^2 v$ | All above | — |
| 3 | Hoop stress | $\sigma_h = P D_o / (2t)$ | $P$ = pressure, $D_o$ = OD, $t$ = thickness | MPa, mm |
| 4 | Wall thickness (seamless) | $t_m = P D / (2 S E + 0.8 P)$ | $S$ = allowable stress, $E$ = joint efficiency | MPa, mm |
| 5 | Wall thickness (welded) | $t_m = P D / (2 S E - 0.8 P)$ | Same as above | MPa, mm |
| 6 | Nominal thickness | $t_{nom} = t_m + C.A.$ | $C.A.$ = corrosion allowance | mm |
| 7 | Schedule | $Sch = (P \times 1000) / S$ | $P$ = design pressure | — |
| 8 | Reynolds number | $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ | $\rho$ = density, $\mu$ = viscosity | — |
| 9 | Friction loss (Darcy) | $h_f = f (L/D)(v^2/2g)$ | $f$ = friction factor, $L$ = length | m |
| 10 | Equivalent length (K-method) | $L_e = K \cdot D / f$ | $K$ = resistance coefficient | m |

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Refineries** | Crude oil transport at high pressure/temp → heavy schedule CS |
| **Pharma** | Ultra-pure water → SS316L sanitary piping (Sch 10S) |
| **Cryogenic** | LNG transport → SS304L with vacuum jacketing |
| **Power Plants** | Boiler tubes, high-pressure steam → Chrome-moly alloys |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Pipe Sizing Equation

### Starting from Continuity

For a fluid flowing in a pipe, the **continuity principle** (conservation of mass) states that mass flow rate is constant across any cross-section:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \times Q = \rho \times A \times v = \text{constant}$$

For an **incompressible fluid** ($\rho = \text{constant}$), this simplifies to:

$$Q = A \times v = \text{constant}$$

The cross-sectional area of a circular pipe of internal diameter $D_i$ is:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D_i^2$$

Substituting into the continuity equation:

$$Q = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D_i^2 \times v$$

Rearranging to solve for $D_i$:

$$D_i^2 = \frac{4 Q}{\pi v}$$

$$D_i = \sqrt{\frac{4 Q}{\pi v}}$$

This is the **fundamental pipe-sizing equation** used in every KTU problem.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of Wall Thickness (Barlow's Formula)

### Step 1: Force Balance on Half Pipe Element

Consider a pipe of length $L$ cut longitudinally. The internal pressure $P$ acts on the projected area $D_o \times L$, producing a bursting force:

$$F_{pressure} = P \times D_o \times L$$

The wall resists this force through its cross-sectional area:

$$A_{wall} = 2 \times t \times L$$

The resisting force at allowable stress $S$:

$$F_{resist} = S \times A_{wall} = 2 \times t \times L \times S$$

### Step 2: Equating Forces (Equilibrium)

For the pipe not to burst:

$$F_{pressure} = F_{resist}$$

$$P \times D_o \times L = 2 \times t \times L \times S$$

The $L$ terms cancel:

$$P \times D_o = 2 \times t \times S$$

### Step 3: Solving for Thickness

$$t = \frac{P \times D_o}{2 \times S}$$

This is the **ideal minimum thickness**.

### Step 4: Inclusion of Joint Efficiency & Corrosion

Since welded joints are weaker than the parent metal, introduce **joint efficiency $E$**:

$$t = \frac{P \times D_o}{2 \times S \times E}$$

For **seamless pipe**, an additional correction factor of $0.8P$ is added to the denominator (ASME B31.3 requirement to account for stress intensification):

$$t_m = \frac{P \times D}{2 \times S \times E + 0.8 \times P}$$

For **welded pipe**, the correction is *subtracted*:

$$t_m = \frac{P \times D}{2 \times S \times E - 0.8 \times P}$$

### Step 5: Adding Corrosion Allowance

The plant is designed for a 15–20 year life. The material gradually erodes. We add a margin:

$$t_{nom} = t_m + C.A.$$

The next **higher standard schedule** is selected.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Sample KTU-style):**
> A pipeline carries water at a flow rate of 200 m³/h. The recommended velocity is 1.5 m/s. Calculate the required nominal pipe size.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Convert flow rate to SI units.

$$Q = 200 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h} = \frac{200}{3600} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 0.0556 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the sizing formula.

$$D_i = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times Q}{\pi \times v}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.0556}{\pi \times 1.5}}$$

$$D_i = \sqrt{\frac{0.2222}{4.7124}} = \sqrt{0.04715}$$

$$D_i = 0.2172 \, \text{m} = 217.2 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to nominal inch size.

$$D_i = 217.2 \, \text{mm} \approx 8.55 \, \text{inches}$$

**Step 4:** Round up to next standard NPS.

From ASME B36.10 — NPS 10 (OD = 273 mm, Sch 40 ID = 254.5 mm) is selected.

**Step 5:** Verify the new velocity.

$$v_{actual} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.0556}{(\pi/4)(0.2545)^2} = \frac{0.0556}{0.0509} = 1.09 \, \text{m/s} \;\; ✓ \text{ (within 1.0–3.0 m/s)}$$

---

## 3.6 Piping Colour Code (KTU 2024 Module 4 — Critical Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Colour codes identify fluid service instantly and prevent cross-connection accidents.**

### A. ASME A13.1 / IS Standard — Colour Code Scheme

The colour code uses **two bands**:
- **Primary colour** → Identifies the *type of fluid* (general category)
- **Secondary / band colour** → Identifies the *specific service*

### B. Standard Colour Code Table (MUST memorise)

| Fluid Service | Primary Colour | Band/Text Colour | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Water** (potable, process) | **Green** | — | Cooling water, DM water |
| **Steam** | **Grey / Light Grey** | — | Process steam, MP steam |
| **Air** (instrument, process) | **Blue** | — | Plant air, IA |
| **Fuel Gas** (Natural gas, LPG) | **Orange / Yellow-Orange** | — | NG, LPG |
| **Acids** | **Yellow** | Red | H₂SO₄, HCl, HNO₃ |
| **Alkalis** | **Yellow** | Green | NaOH, KOH |
| **Oil / Lubricants** | **Brown** | — | Lube oil, fuel oil |
| **Fire-fighting Water** | **Red** | — | Sprinkler, hydrant lines |
| **Toxic / Hazardous** | **Yellow** | Black | Phosgene, chlorine |
| **Inert Gas** (N₂, Ar, CO₂) | **Light Green / Pale Green** | — | Nitrogen blanketing |
| **Cooling Tower Water** | **Dark Green** | — | CW supply/return |
| **Boiler Feed Water** | **Green** | Red | BFW |
| **Condensate** | **Grey** | Green | Steam condensate |
| **Drain / Vent** | **Black** | — | Open drain |
| **Sewage / Waste** | **Dark Grey** | — | Effluent |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Aid — "G-B-O-Y-R" = "Green-Blue-Orange-Yellow-Red" = Water-Air-Gas-Chemical-Fire"**

### C. IS 2379 / Indian Standard Colour Code Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 2379 (Piping Colour Code for Industrial Plants):**
> - **Fire-fighting** → **Signal Red**
> - **Toxic / Hazardous** → **Canary Yellow**
> - **Water (general)** → **Light Sea Green / Dark Sea Green**
> - **Steam** → **Aluminium / Silver Grey**
> - **Air** → **Sky Blue**
> - **Inert gas** → **Light Green**
> - **Acids / Alkalis** → **Yellow with red / green band**
> - **Oil** → **Dark Brown / Light Brown**
> - **Gas (Fuel)** → **Yellow-Orange**
> - **Electric line** → **Dark Blue**

### D. Practical Application & Safety

> [!WARNING]
> **Cross-Connection Hazard:**
> A mistaken connection of a **chlorine line (yellow)** into a **water line (green)** in 1984 at Bhopal led to catastrophic consequences. Colour codes exist **precisely to prevent** such failures. KTU examiners love testing this on the "safety & statutory" aspect.

---

## 3.7 Selection of Pipe Material (Decision Logic)

A flowchart logic for material selection (matrix form):

| Service | Temperature | Pressure | Recommended Material |
|---|---|---|---|
| Water < 80°C | Low–Medium | CS Sch 40 | ASTM A53 ERW |
| Steam 200–400°C | High | CS Sch 80 | ASTM A106 SMLS |
| Steam > 400°C | Very high | Alloy | ASTM A335 P11/P22 |
| Hot Oil > 300°C | High | CS / SS | A106 SMLS |
| H₂SO₄ (dilute) | Ambient | Low | SS316 / FRP / Lined |
| HCl (any) | Ambient | Low | PTFE-lined CS / FRP |
| HF Acid | Ambient | Low | Carbon steel (forms FeF₃ protective layer) |
| Cryogenic (-196°C) | Cryo | Variable | SS304L austenitic |
| Seawater | Ambient | Low | Cu-Ni 90/10 / Super Duplex |
| Food / Pharma | Sterile | Low | SS316L sanitary, polished |
| Brine / Saline | Ambient | Medium | SS316L / Super Duplex |
| Caustic (NaOH) | < 100°C | Low | CS / SS304 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case — HF Acid:** Despite HF being extremely corrosive, it is safely handled in **carbon steel** because it forms a protective FeF₃ passive layer. Using SS or Monel would cause rapid attack. This is a **classic KTU viva question**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are direct definition / explanation questions of the type asked in KTU Module Tests and the first part of University Exam papers.

---

### **Q1. (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]**
**Define the term "Schedule Number" of a pipe. What is the relationship between Schedule 40 and "Standard (STD)" wall thickness?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Schedule Number** is a numerical designation assigned to a pipe to indicate its wall thickness for a given nominal pipe size (NPS). It is a standardized classification system per **ANSI/ASME B36.10** (for carbon steel) and **B36.19** (for stainless steel).
>
> **Relationship with STD:**
> - **Sch 40 = STD (Standard)** wall thickness for **all NPS values** in carbon steel and alloy steel.
> - **Sch 80 = XS (Extra Strong)** wall thickness.
> - **Sch 160 = XXS (Double Extra Strong)** for small NPS.
>
> The Schedule Number can be related to design pressure by the formula:
>
> $$Sch = \frac{P \times 1000}{S}$$
>
> where $P$ is internal pressure and $S$ is allowable stress.

---

### **Q2. (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]**
**List any SIX standard colour codes used in process piping with their corresponding fluid service.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Colour | Fluid Service |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Signal Red** | Fire-fighting water |
| 2 | **Canary Yellow** | Toxic / Hazardous chemicals |
| 3 | **Light Sea Green** | Potable / Process water |
| 4 | **Aluminium / Silver Grey** | Steam |
| 5 | **Sky Blue** | Compressed Air |
| 6 | **Yellow-Orange** | Fuel Gas (Natural Gas, LPG) |
| 7 | **Dark Brown** | Oil / Lubricants |
| 8 | **Light Green** | Inert Gases (N₂, CO₂) |

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 scheme provides **internal choice** in Part B. Two alternatives are given below — students answer either (a)+(b) of Q-A or (b)+(a) of Q-B. Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 MARKS)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, PECHT636]**

**(a)** *Explain the classification of pipes based on (i) material of construction, (ii) method of manufacture, and (iii) service conditions. Give two examples of each. [7 Marks]*

**(b)** *A pipeline is to carry 150 m³/h of cooling water at 30°C. The recommended velocity is 1.8 m/s. The pipe material is carbon steel (ASTM A106 Grade B) with allowable stress S = 96 MPa, joint efficiency E = 1.0 (seamless), and design pressure is 4.0 MPa. Corrosion allowance is 1.5 mm. The pipe is NPS 6 (OD = 168.3 mm). Determine: (i) the internal diameter, (ii) the required wall thickness using ASME B31.3, and (iii) the schedule number. [7 Marks]*

---

#### **Solution to Q-A(a):**

> **Classification of Pipes:**

**(i) Classification by Material of Construction:**

| Material | Example & Use |
|---|---|
| Carbon Steel (CS) | ASTM A106, A53 — used for steam, water, oil |
| Stainless Steel (SS) | A312 SS304, SS316 — used for corrosive chemicals |
| Alloy Steel | 5Cr-0.5Mo, 9Cr-1Mo — high-temp boiler piping |
| Cast Iron | Gray iron — underground water mains |
| Ductile Iron | Spheroidal graphite — municipal water |
| Copper & Alloys | Cu, 90/10 Cu-Ni — heat exchangers, marine |
| Plastic (PVC, CPVC, PP, PVDF, HDPE) | PVC — water; HDPE — gas; PVDF — strong acids |
| FRP / GRP | Fibreglass Reinforced Plastic — chemical plants |
| Lined Pipes | CS + PTFE/rubber/glass lining — severe corrosion |

**(ii) Classification by Method of Manufacture:**

| Method | Characteristics & Example |
|---|---|
| Seamless (SMLS) | No weld seam; hot/cold-pierced billet; used for high-pressure |
| ERW (Electric Resistance Welded) | Longitudinal weld; cold-formed strip; economical |
| EFW (Electric Fusion Welded) / SAW | Submerged arc welding; used for large diameters (NPS ≥ 16) |
| Spiral Welded | Helical seam; large-diameter water transmission |
| Fabricated (Plate-welded) | Plate rolled and welded; special sizes |
| Cast | Sand casting; used for fittings and valves |
| Drawn / Extruded | Cold-drawn tubes; instrumentation tubing |

**(iii) Classification by Service Conditions:**

| Service Type | Description |
|---|---|
| Process Pipes | Transport raw materials and products between equipment |
| Utility Pipes | Steam, cooling water, compressed air, nitrogen |
| Drain & Vent Pipes | Remove condensate, vent gases, blanketing |
| Slurry / Solid Lines | Catalyst, ash, pulverized coal (often with special linings) |
| Sample Lines | For analytical instruments (small-bore tubing) |
| Fire-fighting | Water spray, deluge, sprinkler systems |

---

#### **Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Flow rate: $Q = 150 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h}$
- Velocity: $v = 1.8 \, \text{m/s}$
- Pipe: NPS 6, OD = 168.3 mm
- Allowable stress: $S = 96 \, \text{MPa}$
- Joint efficiency: $E = 1.0$ (seamless)
- Design pressure: $P = 4.0 \, \text{MPa}$
- Corrosion allowance: $C.A. = 1.5 \, \text{mm}$

**Step (i): Internal Diameter**

First convert flow rate:

$$Q = \frac{150}{3600} = 0.04167 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$D_i = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.04167}{\pi \times 1.8}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.1667}{5.6549}} = \sqrt{0.02948}$$

$$D_i = 0.1717 \, \text{m} = 171.7 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step (ii): Wall Thickness (ASME B31.3 — Seamless formula)**

$$t_m = \frac{P \times D}{2 \times S \times E + 0.8 \times P}$$

$$t_m = \frac{4.0 \times 168.3}{2 \times 96 \times 1.0 + 0.8 \times 4.0}$$

$$t_m = \frac{673.2}{192 + 3.2} = \frac{673.2}{195.2}$$

$$t_m = 3.45 \, \text{mm}$$

Add corrosion allowance:

$$t_{nom} = t_m + C.A. = 3.45 + 1.5 = 4.95 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step (iii): Schedule Selection**

From ASME B36.10 Table for NPS 6 (OD = 168.3 mm):
- Sch 10 → 2.77 mm ❌
- Sch 20 → 3.96 mm ❌
- Sch 30 → 4.78 mm ❌
- **Sch 40 (STD) → 7.11 mm ✓**

Since $7.11 \geq 4.95$, select **Sch 40 (Standard)**.

**Result:** Pipe selected is **NPS 6, Sch 40, ASTM A106 Grade B seamless**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 MARKS)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, PECHT636]**

**(a)** *Derive the Barlow formula for the wall thickness of a thin-walled cylindrical pipe under internal pressure. State the assumptions clearly. [7 Marks]*

**(b)** *Explain the following with reference to process piping: (i) ASME B31.3 code, (ii) ASTM A106, (iii) IS 2825, (iv) ASME B16.5, (v) ASME B36.10, (vi) ASME B16.9. List the application of each. [7 Marks]*

---

#### **Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Title: Derivation of Barlow's Formula for Pipe Wall Thickness**

**Assumptions:**
1. Pipe material is **homogeneous and isotropic**.
2. Pipe wall is **thin** (i.e., $D/t > 20$).
3. Internal pressure is **uniformly distributed**.
4. The **hoop stress** (circumferential) is the governing stress.
5. Plane sections remain plane (elementary stress analysis).
6. **No temperature gradient** through the wall.

**Derivation:**

Consider a pipe element of length $L$, inside diameter $D_i$, outside diameter $D_o$, and wall thickness $t = (D_o - D_i)/2$.

**Step 1:** Imagine the pipe is cut longitudinally along the top. This exposes a half-pipe section.

**Step 2:** The internal pressure $P$ acts radially outward on the wall. The force pushing the two halves apart (along the longitudinal cut) is the projected area of the cut × pressure:

$$F_{push} = P \times D_o \times L$$

**Step 3:** This force is resisted by the wall material on both sides of the cut. The total resisting cross-section is:

$$A_{wall} = 2 \times t \times L$$

(The factor 2 accounts for the two wall thicknesses — one on each side of the cut.)

**Step 4:** The resisting force is:

$$F_{resist} = \sigma_h \times A_{wall} = \sigma_h \times 2 \times t \times L$$

**Step 5:** For equilibrium (pipe must not burst):

$$F_{push} = F_{resist}$$

$$P \times D_o \times L = \sigma_h \times 2 \times t \times L$$

**Step 6:** Cancelling $L$:

$$P \times D_o = 2 \times t \times \sigma_h$$

**Step 7:** Solving for hoop stress:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \times D_o}{2 \times t}$$

**Step 8:** Setting hoop stress equal to allowable stress $S$ and solving for $t$:

$$t = \frac{P \times D_o}{2 \times S}$$

This is the **minimum required thickness**. With **joint efficiency $E$** and **corrosion allowance $C$**, the ASME B31.3 form becomes:

$$t = \frac{P \times D}{2 \times S \times E \pm 0.8 P} + C$$

$$\blacksquare$$

---

#### **Solution to Q-B(b):**

| Code | Full Form | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) ASME B31.3** | Process Piping Code | Governs the design, materials, fabrication, installation, inspection, and testing of piping systems for chemical, petroleum, pharmaceutical, and food processing plants. Most widely used code. |
| **(ii) ASTM A106** | Standard Spec. for Seamless Carbon Steel Pipe for High-Temperature Service | Used for high-temperature service in refineries, power plants, and process plants. Grades A, B, C; Grade B is the most common. |
| **(iii) IS 2825** | Indian Standard for Unfired Pressure Vessels | Indian equivalent; governs design of pressure vessels and high-pressure piping in Indian industries. |
| **(iv) ASME B16.5** | Pipe Flanges and Flanged Fittings (NPS ½ to 24) | Standardizes flange dimensions, ratings (150#, 300#, 600#, 900#, 1500#, 2500#), and facing types (RF, FF, RTJ). |
| **(v) ASME B36.10** | Welded and Seamless Wrought Steel Pipe | Defines the dimensional standards (OD, wall thickness, schedule) for carbon and alloy steel pipes. |
| **(vi) ASME B16.9** | Factory-Made Wrought Butt-Welding Fittings | Standardizes dimensions of elbows (90°, 45°), tees, reducers, caps used in butt-welded piping. |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Sch 40 (CS) with Sch 40S (SS)** — These are DIFFERENT! `S` suffix = stainless steel (B36.19) vs no suffix = carbon steel (B36.10).
> 2. **Forgetting corrosion allowance** in wall thickness problems — leads to under-designed pipe and failure.
> 3. **Wrong sign** in ASME B31.3: `+0.8P` for seamless, `-0.8P` for welded. Interchanging causes incorrect answer.
> 4. **Mixing up ID and OD**: Always use **OD** (since schedule is based on OD).
> 5. **In velocity questions**: Do not exceed recommended velocity — water hammer risk above 3 m/s for liquids.
> 6. **In colour codes**: Memorize the **primary** colours; band colour is secondary and not always asked.
> 7. **Common confusion**: HF acid (yellow/toxic in service) is actually handled in **carbon steel**, not SS or lined pipes. This is the **most-missed viva question**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Read this section 10 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

### ✅ Must-Memorise Formulas
- $D_i = \sqrt{4Q/(\pi v)}$
- $t_m = P D / (2 S E \pm 0.8 P)$ — **seamless: +0.8P; welded: −0.8P**
- $t_{nom} = t_m + C.A.$
- $\sigma_h = P D_o / (2 t)$

### ✅ Must-Know Definitions
- Pipe, Piping System, Schedule, NPS, OD, ID, Allowable Stress, Corrosion Allowance
- Service, Joint Efficiency, Allowable Stress, Hoop Stress

### ✅ Must-Know Classifications (4 types)
1. By material — CS, SS, Alloy, CI, Cu, Plastic, FRP, Lined
2. By manufacture — Seamless, ERW, EFW, Spiral, Cast, Drawn
3. By service — Process, Utility, Drain/Vent, Sample, Slurry, Fire
4. By pressure — Class A (vacuum) to Class E (>100 bar)

### ✅ Must-Memorise Codes
- **ASME B31.3** = Process piping (most important)
- **ASME B16.5** = Flanges
- **ASME B16.9** = Butt-weld fittings
- **ASME B36.10** = CS pipe dimensions
- **ASTM A106** = Seamless CS high-temp pipe
- **ASTM A312** = SS pipe
- **IS 2825** = Indian pressure vessel code

### ✅ Must-Memorise Schedule Codes
- **Sch 40 = STD**, **Sch 80 = XS**, **Sch 160 = XXS** (CS)
- **40S, 80S** = Stainless Steel schedules

### ✅ Must-Memorise Colour Codes
- **Red** = Fire-fighting
- **Green** = Water (Sea Green)
- **Grey** = Steam
- **Blue** = Air
- **Yellow** = Toxic/Hazardous
- **Yellow-Orange** = Fuel Gas
- **Brown** = Oil
- **Light Green** = Inert Gas
- **Yellow + Red** = Acid
- **Yellow + Green** = Alkali

### ✅ Special Engineering Cases
- **HF Acid** → Carbon Steel (not SS, not plastic)
- **Seawater** → Cu-Ni or Super Duplex
- **Cryogenic** → SS304L (austenitic, no phase change)
- **Hot strong H₂SO₄** → Cast Iron or special alloys
- **Pharma** → SS316L sanitary

### ✅ Velocity Limits (For Sizing)
- Liquids: 1–3 m/s | Steam: 20–40 m/s | Air: 10–20 m/s

### ✅ Important Constants
- NPS → OD is **fixed** regardless of schedule
- Wall thickness = **variable** with schedule
- ID = OD − 2t (varies with schedule)

### ✅ Key Differences to Remember
- **Nominal** vs **Actual** diameter
- **OD** vs **ID**
- **Schedule** vs **Wall thickness**
- **STD** vs **XS** vs **XXS**
- **Sch 40 (CS)** vs **Sch 40S (SS)** — different wall thicknesses!

### ✅ KTU Likely Question Types
1. Define Schedule / Classify pipes → 3 mark short question
2. Calculate pipe size given Q and v → 7 mark problem
3. Calculate wall thickness using ASME B31.3 → 7 mark problem
4. Explain codes and standards → 7 or 14 mark question
5. List colour codes → 3 mark / viva
6. Choose pipe material for a given service → 7 mark question

---

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — INTRODUCTION TO PIPING**
> *Refer to ASME B31.3 and B36.10 for numerical values during open-book tests.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piping components: pipes, fittings, flanges, gaskets, and valves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT636 - PROCESS UTILITY AND PIPING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Piping components: pipes, fittings, flanges, gaskets, and valves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Piping Components?

**Piping components** are the standardized, engineered elements used to connect, control, direct, isolate, support, and protect the flow of fluids (liquids, gases, slurries, steam, and multi-phase mixtures) within an industrial process plant. Together, they form the **piping network** that connects equipment such as pumps, compressors, heat exchangers, reactors, distillation columns, and storage tanks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (PECHT636):** *Piping components are the discrete mechanical elements — pipes (the pressure-containing conduit), fittings (geometry-changing elements), flanges (bolted detachable joints), gaskets (sealing elements), and valves (flow-control elements) — that collectively constitute a pressure piping system designed, fabricated, and inspected in accordance with process industry codes such as ASME B31.3 (Process Piping).*

The five primary families of piping components, as listed in the KTU Module 4 syllabus, are:

| S.No. | Component | Functional Role |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pipes** | Pressure-containing flow conduit |
| 2 | **Fittings** | Change direction, branch, or change size of flow |
| 3 | **Flanges** | Provide bolted, detachable (and re-makeable) joint |
| 4 | **Gaskets** | Provide leak-tight static seal at flange faces |
| 5 | **Valves** | Control, isolate, regulate, or prevent backflow of fluid |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Circulatory System

Imagine a **chemical plant** as a giant living organism:

- The **Pipes** are the **arteries and veins** — long cylindrical conduits that carry blood (process fluid) under pressure.
- The **Fittings** are the **branches and junctions** — the elbows (knees), tees (forks), and reducers (tapering capillaries) that redirect or split the flow.
- The **Flanges** are the **surgical joints** — bolted connections that allow disassembly for maintenance, just as a surgeon can clamp and replace a section of artery.
- The **Gaskets** are the **sealing tissue** — soft, conformable material that fills the microscopic gaps between flanges, much like biological tissue prevents blood leakage.
- The **Valves** are the **heart's own valves** — they open, close, throttle, or check flow; without them, a process plant cannot be controlled, isolated, or protected from overpressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why every component matters:** A piping system is only as strong as its weakest link. A single undersized gasket, a wrong flange class, or a mis-selected valve can lead to **process downtime, product loss, fire, explosion, or toxic release**. Component selection is therefore a matter of both **process engineering** (pressure, temperature, corrosion) and **mechanical integrity** (code compliance, material strength, fatigue).

## 1.3 Key Physical Standards & Constants

The following **international standards** govern the design and selection of piping components and will appear repeatedly in KTU examination questions:

- **ASME B31.3** — Process Piping (the most commonly used code in chemical/process plants).
- **ASME B16.5** — Pipe Flanges and Flanged Fittings (NPS ½ through NPS 24).
- **ASME B16.9** — Factory-Made Wrought Buttwelding Fittings.
- **ASME B16.11** — Forged Fittings, Socket-Welding and Threaded.
- **ASME B16.34** — Valves — Flanged, Threaded, and Welding End.
- **API 600 / API 602** — Gate, Globe, and Check Valves.
- **API 6D** — Pipeline Valves.
- **MSS-SP-25** — Standard Marking System for Valves, Fittings, Flanges, and Unions.

Universal physical quantities to keep in mind:

- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** 101.325 kPa (1.01325 bar)
- **Design temperature** for carbon steel upper limit: **427 °C (800 °F)** — beyond this, creep becomes critical.
- **Design pressure** is the **maximum sustained pressure** the system can experience, typically the **set pressure of the relief device** at coincident design temperature.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component 1 — Pipes

### 2.1.1 Definition
A **pipe** is a long, hollow, cylindrical pressure vessel whose primary function is to convey process fluid. The wall thickness is determined by the **internal design pressure** and the **allowable stress** of the material.

### 2.1.2 Key Pipe Nomenclature
- **NPS** (Nominal Pipe Size) — a dimensionless designator (e.g., NPS 2, NPS 6, NPS 24).
- **DN** (Diamètre Nominal) — the metric equivalent in millimetres.
- **Schedule (Sch)** — the wall-thickness designator (Sch 5S, 10S, 10, 20, 30, 40S, 40, 60, 80S, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, XXS).
- **OD (Outside Diameter)** — fixed for a given NPS regardless of schedule.
- **ID (Inside Diameter)** = OD − 2 × (wall thickness)

### 2.1.3 Pipe Materials
- **Carbon Steel (CS)** — most common, used for non-corrosive service. ASTM A106 Gr.B, A53 Gr.B, API 5L.
- **Stainless Steel (SS)** — AISI 304, 316 for corrosive/clean service. ASTM A312.
- **Alloy Steel** — A335 P11/P22 for high-temperature service.
- **Non-metallic** — PVC, CPVC, PP, PVDF, HDPE, FRP for corrosive service.
- **Lined pipes** — rubber-lined, glass-lined, PTFE-lined CS for severe corrosion.

### 2.1.4 Pipe Wall Thickness Calculation
According to **ASME B31.3**, the minimum required thickness for straight pipe under internal pressure is given by:

$$t_m = \frac{P \cdot D}{2(SE + PY)}$$

Where:
- $t_m$ = minimum required thickness (mm)
- $P$ = internal design pressure (MPa)
- $D$ = outside diameter of pipe (mm)
- $S$ = allowable stress of material (MPa) at design temperature
- $E$ = joint efficiency (1.0 for seamless, 0.85 for ERW)
- $Y$ = coefficient from Table 304.1.1 of B31.3 (0.4 for ferritic steels ≤ 482 °C, 0.5 for austenitic SS)

The **design (nominal) thickness** is then:

$$t = t_m + c$$

Where $c$ is the **corrosion + mechanical allowances** (typically 1.5–3.0 mm).

The **internal diameter** (for hydraulic calculations) is:

$$D_i = D_o - 2t$$

## 2.2 Component 2 — Fittings

### 2.2.1 Definition
A **fitting** is a piping component whose primary function is to change the **direction**, **size**, or **branching** of flow. They introduce additional **pressure loss** and are characterized by an **equivalent length ($L_e/D$)** ratio or a **resistance coefficient ($K$)**.

### 2.2.2 Classification of Fittings

| Type | Function | Common Variants |
|---|---|---|
| **Elbow** | Change direction | 90°, 45°, 180° (return bend); Long radius (LR, R=1.5D), Short radius (SR, R=1D) |
| **Tee** | Branch the flow | Straight tee, reducing tee, barred tee |
| **Reducer** | Change pipe size | Concentric (CRE), Eccentric (ERE) |
| **Cap** | Close a pipe end | — |
| **Coupling** | Join two pipes | Full coupling, half coupling |
| **Union** | Detachable joint | — |
| **Cross** | 4-way branch | — |
| **Bend** | Smooth direction change | Hot/cold induction bend (R = 3D, 5D, 6D) |

### 2.2.3 Pressure Drop in Fittings — Equivalent Length Method
Fittings cause additional friction losses that are not captured by straight-pipe friction. The **equivalent length** concept converts each fitting into an equivalent length of straight pipe:

$$L_e = K \cdot \frac{D}{f}$$

Where:
- $L_e$ = equivalent length (m)
- $K$ = resistance coefficient (dimensionless)
- $D$ = inside diameter (m)
- $f$ = Darcy friction factor (dimensionless)

The **total equivalent length** of a piping run:

$$L_{total} = L_{straight} + \sum L_{e,fittings} + \sum L_{e,valves}$$

The **pressure drop** then becomes:

$$\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L_{total}}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

## 2.3 Component 3 — Flanges

### 2.3.1 Definition
A **flange** is a forged or cast ring-shaped component, usually bolted to a matching flange, with a gasket between them, to form a **detachable, leak-tight joint**. Flanges are classified by:
1. **Pressure–Temperature Rating (Class)** — e.g., Class 150, 300, 600, 900, 1500, 2500 (in lb/in² units).
2. **Facing Type** — RF (Raised Face), FF (Flat Face), RTJ (Ring Type Joint), T&G (Tongue & Groove), M&F (Male & Female).
3. **Connection Type** — Weld Neck (WN), Slip-On (SO), Socket Weld (SW), Threaded (TH), Lap Joint (LJ), Blind (BL).

### 2.3.2 Flange Selection Logic (Why and How)

- **Weld Neck (WN)** — High pressure/temperature; critical service; used for butt-weld termination. **Why:** Smooth bore, no crevices, low turbulence, full strength.
- **Slip-On (SO)** — Low pressure (Class 150/300), non-critical. **Why:** Easy alignment, cheaper; only two fillet welds.
- **Socket Weld (SW)** — Small-bore (≤ NPS 2), high pressure. **Why:** Good for steam and small-bore instrumentation.
- **Threaded (TH)** — Low pressure, non-hazardous service. **Why:** No welding, fast installation.
- **Blind (BL)** — Termination of a line or vessel nozzle. **Why:** Allows isolation and access.

### 2.3.3 Flange Design — Bolt Load (Pre-Qualifying for B16.5)

For **ASME B16.5** flanges, two operating bolt loads must be calculated and the greater one is used for design:

**Operating bolt load ($W_m$):**
$$W_m = H + H_p = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot G^2 \cdot P + 2 \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot G \cdot m \cdot P$$

**Gasket seating bolt load ($W_g$):**
$$W_g = \pi \cdot b \cdot G \cdot y$$

Where:
- $G$ = gasket mean diameter (mm)
- $P$ = design pressure (MPa)
- $b$ = effective gasket width (mm)
- $m$ = gasket factor (dimensionless)
- $y$ = gasket seating stress (MPa)
- $H$ = hydrostatic end force on the flange
- $H_p$ = pressure thrust on the flange

The **required flange bolt area** is then:

$$A_b = \frac{\max(W_m, W_g)}{S_b}$$

Where $S_b$ is the allowable bolt stress at design temperature.

## 2.4 Component 4 — Gaskets

### 2.4.1 Definition
A **gasket** is a deformable sealing element placed between two flange faces. It must:
- **Seal** the joint against internal pressure (sealing stress > internal pressure × area).
- **Conform** to flange surface irregularities.
- **Resist** chemical attack, temperature, and creep.

### 2.4.2 Classification of Gaskets

| Type | Construction | Service |
|---|---|---|
| **Compressed Asbestos Fibre (CAF / AF)** | Asbestos + binder | Legacy; mostly replaced |
| **Non-asbestos (compressed fibre)** | Aramid + NBR | General service, water, steam, oils |
| **Spiral Wound (SW)** | Metal + filler windings | High T, P; cyclic service |
| **Ring Joint (RTJ)** | Soft iron / SS oval/Octagonal ring | High P (Class 600+), hydrocarbons |
| **Graphite (Flexible Graphite)** | Foil/wire reinforced | High T, refinery service |
| **PTFE** | Virgin or filled PTFE | Corrosive chemicals, food/pharma |
| **Camprofile / Kammprofile** | Metal core + soft facing | Heat exchangers, high T/P |
| **Metal-jacketed** | Metal shell + filler | Heat exchangers, tube sheets |

### 2.4.3 Gasket Selection Logic
The **gasket factor (m)** and **seating stress (y)** — used in flange design — are tabulated in **ASME B16.5** Appendix C:

| Material | m | y (MPa) |
|---|---|---|
| Spiral-wound, flexible graphite filler | 3.0 | 68.9 |
| Compressed asbestos with binder | 2.0 | 31.0 |
| PTFE | 2.0 | 13.8 |
| Ring Joint (oval/octagonal) | 5.5 | 124.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection principle:** Gasket must be **chemically compatible** with the fluid, **temperature-rated** for the service, and **pressure-rated** to give a minimum seating stress ≥ design pressure × m.

## 2.5 Component 5 — Valves

### 2.5.1 Definition
A **valve** is a mechanical device by which the flow of fluid may be **started, stopped, regulated, or prevented from reversing**. Valves are the **most numerous and critical** components in any piping system.

### 2.5.2 Classification of Valves

**By Function:**
- **Isolation (on/off) valves** — gate, ball, plug, butterfly.
- **Regulation (throttling) valves** — globe, needle, control valve.
- **Check (non-return) valves** — swing check, lift check, dual-plate check, ball check.
- **Safety/Relief valves** — pressure safety valve (PSV), pressure relief valve (PRV), safety relief valve (SRV), rupture disc.

**By Body Design:**
- **Gate valve** — Wedge or parallel slide. Used fully open or closed. Low pressure drop. **Not for throttling.**
- **Globe valve** — Z-pattern or Y-pattern. **Best for throttling.** High pressure drop.
- **Ball valve** — Floating or trunnion-mounted. Quarter-turn. Tight shutoff.
- **Butterfly valve** — Concentric or double-eccentric. Lightweight, large sizes.
- **Plug valve** — Lubricated or non-lubricated. Quarter-turn.
- **Diaphragm valve** — Weir or straight-through. For corrosive/sterile service.
- **Check valve** — Prevents backflow; depends on flow for actuation.

### 2.5.3 Valve Selection Logic

| Service Condition | Recommended Valve |
|---|---|
| Isolation, clean liquid | Gate or Ball |
| Throttling, accurate control | Globe or Control valve |
| Quick shutoff, slurry | Ball or Butterfly |
| Corrosive/abrasive | Diaphragm or Lined ball |
| Backflow prevention | Swing or Lift Check |
| Overpressure protection | PSV / Rupture Disc |
| Cryogenic | Extended bonnet ball/ globe |
| High temp steam | Gate (flexible wedge) or globe |

### 2.5.4 Valve Coefficient ($C_v$)
The **valve sizing coefficient** is used to size valves for given flow conditions:

$$C_v = Q \sqrt{\frac{SG}{\Delta P}}$$

For liquids:

$$C_v = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{\Delta P / SG}}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = flow rate (GPM, US)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across valve (psi)
- $SG$ = specific gravity of fluid (water = 1.0)

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "select" a valve, the examiner expects you to: (1) state the **service**, (2) identify **function needed** (isolation/throttling/check/safety), (3) justify choice by listing **two advantages** and **one limitation**.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Variables | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_m = \dfrac{PD}{2(SE+PY)}$ | $P,D,S,E,Y$ | mm / MPa | Minimum pipe wall thickness (ASME B31.3) |
| 2 | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{PD}{2t}$ | $P,D,t$ | MPa | Hoop (circumferential) stress in pipe |
| 3 | $\sigma_L = \dfrac{PD}{4t}$ | $P,D,t$ | MPa | Longitudinal stress in pipe |
| 4 | $L_e = K \cdot \dfrac{D}{f}$ | $K,D,f$ | m | Equivalent length of fitting |
| 5 | $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | $f,L,D,\rho,v$ | Pa | Darcy–Weisbach pressure drop |
| 6 | $W_m = \dfrac{\pi}{4}G^2 P + 2\pi b \cdot G \cdot m \cdot P$ | $G,b,m,P$ | N | Operating bolt load on flange |
| 7 | $W_g = \pi b \cdot G \cdot y$ | $b,G,y$ | N | Gasket seating bolt load |
| 8 | $A_b = \dfrac{\max(W_m,W_g)}{S_b}$ | $W_m,W_g,S_b$ | mm² | Required bolt cross-sectional area |
| 9 | $C_v = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{\Delta P/SG}}$ | $Q,\Delta P,SG$ | — | Valve sizing coefficient |
| 10 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{vD}{\nu}$ | $\rho,v,D,\mu$ | — | Reynolds number (flow regime) |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hoop Stress in a Thin-Walled Pipe

A pipe under internal pressure behaves as a **thin-walled pressure vessel** when $D/t \geq 20$. We will derive the hoop (circumferential) stress from **force balance**.

### Step 1 — Imagine a horizontal pipe of length $L$ and internal diameter $D$

Cut the pipe longitudinally along a horizontal plane. Two equal halves result. The **internal pressure $P$** acts on the projected area $\frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ on the cut face, producing an **axial force** that must be resisted by the **wall tension** acting on the two cut edges (each of length $L$, thickness $t$).

### Step 2 — Write the force balance

The total axial force exerted by the internal pressure on the projected area is:

$$F_P = P \cdot A_{projected} = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

This force is resisted by the **wall stress** acting on **two cut sections**, each of area $t \times L$:

$$F_{wall} = \sigma_h \cdot (2 \cdot t \cdot L)$$

### Step 3 — Set $F_P = F_{wall}$ and solve for $\sigma_h$

$$P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \sigma_h \cdot 2 t L$$

Because the pipe is in equilibrium at any cross-section, we can take the force per unit length by setting $L = 1$ (per unit length), giving:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P D}{2 t}$$

### Step 4 — State the assumption

This derivation is valid for **thin-walled** pipes where $D/t \geq 20$ and the wall is **isotropic and homogeneous**. For thick-walled pipes, the **Lamé equations** are used.

### Step 5 — Numerical Example

> **Given:** $P = 1.5$ MPa, $D = 200$ mm, $t = 10$ mm.
>
> **Find:** $\sigma_h$
>
> **Solution:**
>
> $$\sigma_h = \frac{(1.5) \cdot (200)}{2 \cdot (10)} = \frac{300}{20} = 15.0 \text{ MPa}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Pipe Wall Thickness (ASME B31.3)

### Step 1 — Start from Hoop Stress
From Section 3.1, hoop stress in a pipe is:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{PD}{2t}$$

### Step 2 — Introduce Allowable Stress
The actual hoop stress must not exceed the **allowable stress** of the pipe material at design temperature. ASME B31.3 introduces a **joint efficiency (E)** to account for welded seams and a **coefficient (Y)** to account for temperature-dependent creep:

$$S \cdot E = \frac{PD}{2t_m} \Rightarrow t_m = \frac{PD}{2SE}$$

### Step 3 — Account for the Pressure-Thrust Term
In thick-walled design, the longitudinal stress is half the hoop stress. The factor $Y$ is added to the denominator as a correction to the thin-wall equation because of biaxial stress state:

$$t_m = \frac{PD}{2(SE + PY)}$$

### Step 4 — Add Corrosion Allowance
The final design thickness is the calculated minimum plus a corrosion + mechanical allowance $c$:

$$t = t_m + c$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example
> **Given:** A NPS 6 pipe ($D = 168.3$ mm), $P = 2.0$ MPa, $S = 110$ MPa (carbon steel at 200 °C), $E = 1.0$ (seamless), $Y = 0.4$, $c = 1.5$ mm.
>
> **Find:** Required pipe wall thickness and recommended schedule.
>
> **Solution:**
>
> Step 1 — Calculate $t_m$:
>
> $$t_m = \frac{(2.0)(168.3)}{2[(110)(1.0) + (2.0)(0.4)]} = \frac{336.6}{2[110 + 0.8]} = \frac{336.6}{221.6} = 1.519 \text{ mm}$$
>
> Step 2 — Add corrosion allowance:
>
> $$t = 1.519 + 1.5 = 3.02 \text{ mm} \approx 3.0 \text{ mm}$$
>
> Step 3 — Select a schedule:
>
> For NPS 6 (168.3 mm OD), the schedules with wall ≥ 3.0 mm are:
> - Sch 5S → 2.77 mm ❌
> - Sch 10S → 3.40 mm ✅ (chosen)
>
> So the recommended pipe is **NPS 6, Sch 10S** with actual wall = 3.40 mm.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Flange Operating Bolt Load ($W_m$)

### Step 1 — Identify Two Components of Bolt Load
The bolts must:
1. Resist the **hydrostatic end force** that tries to push the flanges apart.
2. Maintain **gasket contact pressure** (seating stress) so the gasket does not blow out.

So $W_m = H + H_p$.

### Step 2 — Derive the Hydrostatic End Force (H)
Consider the fluid pressure acting on the area **inside the flange bore**, i.e., on diameter $G$ (the gasket mean diameter is taken as a close approximation for force on the flange):

$$H = P \cdot A_{inside} = P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} G^2$$

This force pushes the flanges apart and must be balanced by bolt tension.

### Step 3 — Derive the Pressure Thrust on the Flange ($H_p$)
Because the area inside the bore is at fluid pressure $P$, the **net** force pushing the flange face outward is the pressure times the **area of the flange face inside the bolt circle** that pressure acts upon. ASME B16.5 approximates this as:

$$H_p = 2 \cdot b \cdot \pi \cdot G \cdot m \cdot P$$

Where $b$ is the **effective gasket width** and $m$ is the **gasket factor** (a multiplier representing the contact pressure needed per unit internal pressure to seal).

### Step 4 — Combine

$$W_m = H + H_p = \frac{\pi}{4} G^2 P + 2\pi b \cdot G \cdot m \cdot P$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example
> **Given:** NPS 4 Class 150 RF flange with spiral-wound graphite gasket.
> - $G = 117.5$ mm (from ASME B16.5 table for NPS 4)
> - $b = 12.7$ mm (effective width)
> - $P = 1.0$ MPa
> - $m = 3.0$ (spiral-wound graphite)
>
> **Find:** $W_m$
>
> **Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 — Hydrostatic end force:**
>
> $$H = \frac{\pi}{4} (117.5)^2 (1.0) = \frac{\pi}{4} (13806.25) (1.0) = 10839.5 \text{ N} = 10.84 \text{ kN}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Pressure thrust:**
>
> $$H_p = 2 \pi (12.7)(117.5)(3.0)(1.0) = 2 \pi (12.7)(117.5)(3.0)$$
>
> $$H_p = 2 (3.1416)(4476.075) = 28127.4 \text{ N} = 28.13 \text{ kN}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Total operating bolt load:**
>
> $$W_m = 10.84 + 28.13 = 38.97 \text{ kN}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Equivalent Length of a 90° Standard Elbow (K-method)

### Step 1 — Start with the Energy Equation
For a fitting causing a minor loss $\Delta P_{minor}$, define the **resistance coefficient $K$** such that:

$$\Delta P_{minor} = K \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Equate to Darcy–Weisbach Form
Setting the minor loss equal to the friction loss in an equivalent straight length $L_e$:

$$f \cdot \frac{L_e}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = K \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $L_e$

$$L_e = K \cdot \frac{D}{f}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Standard Tabulated K Values

| Fitting | $K$-value (turbulent flow) |
|---|---|
| 90° standard elbow | 0.9 |
| 90° long-radius elbow (R = 1.5D) | 0.45 |
| 45° standard elbow | 0.4 |
| Tee — line flow | 0.2 |
| Tee — branch flow | 1.0 |
| Concentric reducer | 0.1 |
| Gate valve (fully open) | 0.15 |
| Globe valve (fully open) | 10.0 |
| Ball valve (fully open) | 0.05 |
| Swing check valve | 2.5 |

### Step 5 — Numerical Example
> **Given:** A water line (ρ = 1000 kg/m³, μ = 1 cP) has 4 standard 90° elbows, 2 gate valves, and 1 swing check. Pipe ID = 100 mm, flow velocity = 2 m/s.
> - For water at 2 m/s in 100 mm: $f \approx 0.018$ (turbulent, smooth pipe).
>
> **Find:** Total equivalent length of fittings.
>
> **Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 — Equivalent length per component:**
>
> $$\text{Elbow 90°: } L_e = 0.9 \cdot \frac{0.1}{0.018} = 5.0 \text{ m}$$
>
> $$\text{Gate valve: } L_e = 0.15 \cdot \frac{0.1}{0.018} = 0.83 \text{ m}$$
>
> $$\text{Swing check: } L_e = 2.5 \cdot \frac{0.1}{0.018} = 13.89 \text{ m}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Total:**
>
> $$L_{total,eq} = (4 \times 5.0) + (2 \times 0.83) + (1 \times 13.89)$$
>
> $$L_{total,eq} = 20.0 + 1.66 + 13.89 = 35.55 \text{ m}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Valve Sizing for Liquid Service

### Step 1 — Define the $C_v$ Coefficient
By definition, $C_v$ is the number of GPM of water at 60 °F that flows through the valve with a 1 psi pressure drop:

$$C_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{\frac{SG}{\Delta P}}$$

### Step 2 — Numerical Example
> **Given:** A control valve is to be sized for a flow of 200 GPM of a liquid with $SG = 0.85$ and allowable pressure drop $\Delta P = 12$ psi.
>
> **Find:** Required $C_v$.
>
> **Solution:**
>
> $$C_v = 200 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.85}{12}} = 200 \cdot \sqrt{0.07083} = 200 \cdot 0.2662 = 53.24$$
>
> Therefore, select a valve with $C_v \geq 53.24$ — typically a valve with $C_v$ rating 60 (next standard size).

## 4.1 Piping System Block Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **interaction hierarchy** of the five piping component families in a typical chemical-process piping run.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Process Equipment: Pump, Vessel, Exchanger] --> B[Pipe Stock: NPS, Schedule, Material]
    B --> C[Change of Direction: Fittings]
    C --> D[Detachable Joint: Flanges]
    D --> E[Sealing Element: Gasket]
    D --> F[Flow Control: Valves]
    F --> G[Next Equipment or Termination]
    C --> F
    E --> D
    B --> F
    style A fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    style B fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
    style C fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    style D fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000
    style E fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#000
    style F fill:#ffe4e6,stroke:#9f1239,color:#000
    style G fill:#cffafe,stroke:#155e75,color:#000
```

### Interpretation of the Flow
- **Process equipment** (A) initiates or terminates flow.
- **Pipe stock** (B) is the main conduit between any two pieces of equipment.
- **Fittings** (C) interrupt the straight run to **change direction, size, or branch**.
- **Flanges** (D) are inserted at strategic points — at equipment nozzles, isolation valves, and inspection points — to allow **disassembly**.
- **Gaskets** (E) sit **between** flange faces, providing the static seal.
- **Valves** (F) are placed in-line to **isolate, regulate, or protect**.
- Flow proceeds to the **next equipment** (G).

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: List any three types of flanges and state one specific application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A flange is a bolted, detachable joint connector. Three common types are:

1. **Weld Neck (WN) Flange** — *Application:* Used in high-pressure and high-temperature services such as refinery hydrocracker reactor outlet piping, because it provides a smooth bore (no crevices) and full reinforcement at the weld.

2. **Slip-On (SO) Flange** — *Application:* Used in low-pressure fire-water mains and cooling-water service at Class 150 rating, because it is economical, easy to align, and requires only two fillet welds.

3. **Blind (BL) Flange** — *Application:* Used to terminate a piping run (e.g., a future tie-in point) or to blank off a vessel nozzle during maintenance, because it has no bore and provides complete isolation.

(Any other valid type: Socket Weld, Threaded, Lap Joint — with appropriate service accepted.)

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Q: Differentiate between a gate valve and a globe valve in terms of (i) flow direction change inside, (ii) suitability for throttling, and (iii) typical $C_v$ / pressure drop.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Gate Valve | Globe Valve |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Flow path | Straight through; no change in direction | S-shaped (Z-pattern) or Y-pattern; flow changes direction twice |
| (ii) Throttling suitability | **Not suitable** for throttling; used fully open or fully closed (partial opening causes vibration and seat damage) | **Best suited** for throttling and flow regulation |
| (iii) Pressure drop | Very low pressure drop; $K \approx 0.15$ | High pressure drop; $K \approx 10$ |

Hence, gate valves are used for **isolation**, while globe valves are used where **accurate flow regulation** is needed.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B questions in the End Semester Examination carry 14 marks and offer an **internal choice** (either (a)/(b) sub-parts of one question, OR two completely different question options of equal difficulty). The structure below presents the standard "Option A vs Option B" pattern.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PECHT636 Module 4]**
**(a) With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a globe valve. State its typical applications and two advantages over a gate valve.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
A globe valve consists of:
- A **globular (spherical) body** with an internal Z-shaped or Y-shaped flow passage.
- A **disc or plug** that moves vertically on a **stem** operated by a handwheel.
- A **seat** ring fitted into the body, providing a tight seal with the disc.
- **Packing** around the stem to prevent external leakage.
- **Bonnet** bolted to the body, providing access to internal parts.

*(Sketch: a vertical cross-section showing body, seat, disc, stem, handwheel, bonnet, packing.)*

**Working:**
When the handwheel is rotated, the stem lowers the disc onto the seat, gradually throttling the flow. Raising the disc increases the flow area. The S-shaped path forces the fluid to change direction, creating turbulence that enables fine flow control.

**Applications:**
- Throttling service in steam, oil, and water lines.
- Feedwater regulation in boilers.
- Sampling and instrument isolation.

**Two advantages over a gate valve:**
1. Superior **throttling capability** — accurate flow regulation is possible without seat erosion.
2. **Faster operation** — only a small stem travel is needed to close, compared with a gate valve that requires multiple turns.

**(b) Calculate the minimum required wall thickness for an NPS 8 seamless carbon-steel pipe (OD = 219.1 mm) carrying a process fluid at design pressure 3.0 MPa and design temperature 250 °C. Allowable stress $S = 95$ MPa, joint efficiency $E = 1.0$, temperature coefficient $Y = 0.4$. Add a corrosion allowance of 1.5 mm. Recommend a suitable schedule.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Apply the ASME B31.3 minimum thickness formula:**

$$t_m = \frac{P \cdot D}{2(SE + PY)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$t_m = \frac{(3.0)(219.1)}{2[(95)(1.0) + (3.0)(0.4)]}$$

$$t_m = \frac{657.3}{2[95 + 1.2]} = \frac{657.3}{192.4} = 3.417 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Add corrosion allowance:**

$$t = t_m + c = 3.417 + 1.5 = 4.917 \text{ mm} \approx 5.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Select schedule from NPS 8 pipe table:**

For NPS 8 (OD = 219.1 mm), the available schedules and wall thicknesses are:
- Sch 5S → 2.77 mm ❌
- Sch 10S → 3.76 mm ❌
- **Sch 20 → 5.16 mm ✅** (chosen, slightly above required)
- Sch 30 → 6.35 mm ✅ (over-design, more expensive)
- Sch 40S → 5.49 mm ✅ (alternate choice)

**Recommended:** **NPS 8, Sch 20** (or Sch 40S), with actual wall = 5.16 mm (or 5.49 mm).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice to Question A)*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PECHT636 Module 4]**
**(a) Explain with a diagram the construction of a spiral-wound gasket. List the materials used for the winding and the centering rings, and state two services where spiral-wound gaskets are preferred.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
A spiral-wound gasket consists of:
- A **metal winding strip** (typically V-shaped) and a **filler strip** (typically flexible graphite or non-asbestos fibre), wound together in a spiral around the **inner ring**.
- An **inner ring (centring ring)** — a solid metal ring that sits inside the bolt circle. It prevents over-compression of the gasket and acts as a stop flange.
- An **outer ring (guide ring)** — a solid metal ring outside the winding that centres the gasket on the flange face and provides radial support.

*(Sketch: Cross-section showing inner ring, spiral winding, outer ring, with bolt/passage holes.)*

**Materials:**
- **Winding metal:** 304 SS, 316 SS, Monel 400, Inconel 600, Inconel 825, titanium.
- **Filler:** Flexible graphite (most common), PTFE, ceramic, mica, non-asbestos fibre.
- **Centring rings:** Carbon steel (CS) with anti-corrosion paint, or SS to match the winding metal.

**Two preferred services:**
1. **High-temperature and high-pressure hydrocarbon service** — refinery hydrocracker, catalytic reformer, ethylene cracker headers (Class 600/900 flanges at 400–600 °C).
2. **Cyclic thermal service** — where flange face differential expansion is high; spiral-wound gaskets tolerate differential expansion better than soft cut gaskets.

**(b) A horizontal pipeline of 100 mm nominal diameter carries water at 1.5 m/s. The line has six standard 90° elbows and one fully-open gate valve. The straight length of the pipe is 50 m. Assume Darcy friction factor $f = 0.02$. Calculate the total equivalent length of the line and the total pressure drop. Take $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Equivalent length of each fitting:**

- Standard 90° elbow: $K = 0.9$ → $L_{e,elbow} = K \cdot D / f = 0.9 \cdot 0.1 / 0.02 = 4.5$ m each.
- Gate valve (fully open): $K = 0.15$ → $L_{e,gate} = 0.15 \cdot 0.1 / 0.02 = 0.75$ m.

**Step 2 — Total equivalent length of fittings:**

$$\sum L_e = 6 \cdot 4.5 + 1 \cdot 0.75 = 27.0 + 0.75 = 27.75 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Total equivalent length of line:**

$$L_{total} = L_{straight} + \sum L_e = 50 + 27.75 = 77.75 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Pressure drop using Darcy–Weisbach:**

$$\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L_{total}}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute values:**

$$\Delta P = 0.02 \cdot \frac{77.75}{0.1} \cdot \frac{1000 \cdot (1.5)^2}{2}$$

$$\Delta P = 0.02 \cdot 777.5 \cdot \frac{1000 \cdot 2.25}{2}$$

$$\Delta P = 0.02 \cdot 777.5 \cdot 1125 = 17493.75 \text{ Pa} \approx 17.5 \text{ kPa}$$

**Therefore, the total pressure drop is 17.5 kPa.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing NPS with DN:** NPS is dimensionless, DN is in mm. NPS 6 ≈ DN 150, but **not exactly equal** to OD. Always state OD separately.
> 2. **Forgetting the $Y$ term:** In pipe wall thickness (ASME B31.3), students often write $t_m = \frac{PD}{2SE}$ and forget the $PY$ term in the denominator. This is a **3-mark loss** in the valuation.
> 3. **Forgetting corrosion allowance:** Calculation of $t_m$ alone is incomplete. Add $c$ to get design thickness, then pick schedule.
> 4. **Mixing up $W_m$ and $W_g$:** $W_m$ is the **operating** bolt load; $W_g$ is the **seating** bolt load. Both must be calculated; the **greater** is used for design.
> 5. **Using $K$-values without $f$:** When asked for equivalent length, you **must** divide $K \cdot D$ by $f$, not just multiply $K \cdot D$. A common mistake that loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Wrong unit for $C_v$:** $C_v$ requires $Q$ in GPM and $\Delta P$ in psi. If the problem gives SI units, **convert first**, or use the SI form of the equation.
> 7. **Not justifying valve choice:** "Use a globe valve" is **not enough**; the examiner expects *one function* (throttling/isolation) and *one reason* (tight shutoff/low pressure drop).
> 8. **Schematic mistakes in flange/gasket:** A spiral-wound gasket **must show** the inner ring, the spiral winding, and the outer ring. Drawing only a "ring" is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist as your final-day revision sheet before the exam.**

- [ ] **Pipe sizing** uses the **ASME B31.3** formula $t_m = \dfrac{PD}{2(SE+PY)}$; add **corrosion allowance** to get design thickness.
- [ ] **Sch 40** and **Sch 80** are the most common schedules in carbon-steel service; **Sch 5S/10S/40S** are stainless steel designators.
- [ ] **NPS** is dimensionless; **OD** is fixed per NPS regardless of schedule; **ID** = OD − 2t.
- [ ] **Fittings** are sized by the pipe **OD** for buttweld (BW) and by **ID** for socket weld (SW) and threaded (TH).
- [ ] **Elbows** — long radius (1.5D) is preferred over short radius (1D) to **reduce pressure drop**.
- [ ] **Reducers** — **concentric** for gas/vapour (symmetric), **eccentric** for liquid (to keep top or bottom flat and avoid air pocket / liquid trap).
- [ ] **Flanges** — selection by **Class × NPS × Material × Facing**; weld neck for high P/T, slip-on for low P, blind for termination.
- [ ] **Gasket factor (m)** and **seating stress (y)** come from **ASME B16.5 Appendix C**; memorize spiral-wound (m=3.0, y=68.9) and RTJ (m=5.5, y=124).
- [ ] **Spiral-wound gaskets** are the industry workhorse for **Class 150–2500** raised-face flanges.
- [ ] **RTJ gaskets** are used for **Class 600 and above** ring-type-joint flanges, especially in hydrocarbon service.
- [ ] **Valves** — gate & ball = isolation; globe = throttling; check = backflow prevention; PSV = overpressure protection.
- [ ] **Gate valve** is **NOT for throttling**; **globe valve** has the **highest pressure drop** among common valves.
- [ ] **Ball valve** has the **lowest pressure drop** ($K \approx 0.05$) and **best leak-tightness**.
- [ ] **Equivalent length** $L_e = \dfrac{K D}{f}$; the **Darcy–Weisbach** equation is the universal pressure-drop tool.
- [ ] **Valve sizing** uses the **$C_v$ coefficient**: $C_v = Q \sqrt{SG / \Delta P}$ (US units).
- [ ] **Bolt load** must include **both** hydrostatic end force and gasket pressure thrust: $W_m = \frac{\pi}{4}G^2 P + 2\pi b G m P$.
- [ ] **The key memory phrase**: "**P**ipes carry, **F**ittings turn, **F**langes join, **G**askets seal, **V**alves control."

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Piping Components.**
> **Best of luck for your KTU examinations!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pipe fittings: functions and properties, types of pipe fittings and their selection
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT636 - PROCESS UTILITY AND PIPING ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pipe fittings: functions and properties, types of pipe fittings and their selection

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Pipe fittings** are engineered piping components used to connect straight sections of pipe or tubing, adapt to different sizes or shapes, and regulate (or measure) fluid flow within a piping system. They are essential auxiliary items that complement pipes, pumps, and equipment in any chemical process plant, refinery, or utility network.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT636)**, pipe fittings are classified under process utility components that are mandatory for:
- Directional changes
- Branching of flow
- Diameter transitions
- Flow termination
- Maintenance access
- Pressure and flow control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT636 Module 4):** A piping system is *never* complete without fittings. The mechanical integrity, hydraulic efficiency, and safety of the entire plant depend on the **correct selection, material compatibility, and pressure rating** of the fittings used.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Road Junction" Model

Imagine a **highway road network** for vehicles. Roads are the straight paths (like pipes carrying fluid), but a network of roads is useless without:
- **Bends** (elbows) — to turn the road
- **Y-junctions** (tees) — to split traffic
- **Lane mergers** (reducers) — to join a wide road to a narrow one
- **Dead ends** (caps/plugs) — to terminate a road
- **Traffic signals** (valves) — to control flow
